title: HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities.pdf
text:
Keys—if locked out, call HSAA. For interns/volunteers not issued keys, we will not let them in w/o permission from the member or OM.
<mark>Sheriffs do NOT have keys to offices.</mark>

Parking—administered and enforced by DAGS, not HSAA.

IDs—you **MUST** wear your ID at all times while on premises.

Capillary readers—do not attempt to ride in private elevators using capillary, it will not work. When using elevators or stairs, do not allow non-building occupants to piggy-back into the building.

3

title: Sergeant-at-Arms
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities.pdf
text:
# Sergeant-at-Arms

![State of Hawaii House of Representatives Sergeant-at-Arms badge](image)

Pursuant to Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS) 21-17 and 21-18, the Sergeant-at-Arms shall, among other things, <mark>maintain order and decorum</mark> under the direction of the Speaker of the House and/or Chairs of committees; furthermore, have all the powers and authority of a police officer, including the power of arrest.

4

title: Order and Decorum
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities.pdf
text:
# Order and Decorum

* What constitutes order and decorum? Proper behavior when in legislative proceedings (floor session/hearings).
* Better question: what constitutes behavior that would be considered "out of order"
* <mark>Behavior so disruptive that the proceeding cannot continue.</mark>
    - For Floor Sessions—it is at the discretion of the Speaker of the House
    - For Committee Hearings—it is at the discretion of the Chair

Interpretation is made by the Speaker/Chair only—not other members or staff.

It applies to everyone participating in the proceeding, including the public, staff and other members.

As staff, learn proper decorum as preferred by the Chair.

Dos and don'ts for hearing rooms and Chamber.

5

title: What else do we do?
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities.pdf
text:
# What else do we do?

* Mail-incoming/outgoing
    - Do not have personal mail sent to the State Capitol
* Conference room/auditorium and equipment use
    - Do not use rooms or equipment for personal purposes
* Supplies
* Property control
* Hearing notice filing
* Printshop
    - Core function: producing legislative documents
    - Secondary function: producing congratulatory documents and mailers
* Facilities Management (DAGS liaison)

6

title: Personal wellness and safety
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities.pdf
text:
# Personal wellness and safety

* HSAA staff is trained in first aid
* All floors have an AED
* All corner offices have first aid kits
* For <u>medical</u> emergencies needing paramedics, call 911 and then call HSAA

# Security

* State Capitol – Open, accessible, no screening of vehicles.
* Our work involves vigorous debate on contentious issue attracting passionate individuals
* Capitols are symbols of democracy and can attract bad actors
* HSAA and Capitol Sheriffs work cooperatively to provide a safe work environment for Members, staff, and visitors.

# Need help?

* State Capitol Law Enforcement—State Sheriffs, call 586-1352
* Fire—call 911
* Use “ctrl + alt + H” to call for help silently from your desk
* Call HSAA and say “Rep.      cannot attend the INA meeting”

title: Safety and other reminders
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/HSAA Operations and General Responsibilities.pdf
text:
# Safety and other reminders

* Don’t let non-occupants “piggy back” in elevators or stairwells, especially after hours
* Plan to move your car to the basement parking lot if working late
* Drive slowly when in parking lots
* “If you see something, say something”
* Become familiar with evacuation and lock down procedures
* Don’t forget your keys
* **ALWAYS** wear your ID
* Add State Sheriffs to your contact list: 808-586-1352
* Add House Sergeant-at-Arms to your contact list: 808-586-6500

We will be going over a few of Excel's built-in features that can help you take better advantage of LiveStatus. ![Screenshot of the LiveStatus Excel interface showing various buttons and configuration options for tracking legislative bills.](image)
A B C D E F G H I J K L M N O P Q R
1 Clear all Prefill all Instructions Update All on open? Auto Spreadsheets to update: (All sheets) Year: 2021
2 [colspan=3]Bill No./Drafts A <- There should always be a letter here. [colspan=3]House Conferees <- House Conferees
3 Yes Link to status on web?
4 [colspan=3]Measure Title B <- Measure Title [colspan=3]Companions <- Companions
5 (Measure title is in all capital letters) No Show status and referral for companion?

23 No LiveStatus to skip that row for updates

title: <u>Using the Sort Function</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# <u>Using the Sort Function</u>

To sort your list:
1. Click somewhere in the column in which you wish to sort (do not highlight the entire column).
2. Click on the Data command tab, then click on one of the two sorting buttons:

![Screenshot of the Excel Data ribbon showing the Sort & Filter group. A red circle highlights the AZ (ascending) and ZA (descending) sort buttons.](image)

Use the AZ button to sort in ascending order and use the ZA button to sort in descending order.

If you want to sort by multiple criteria, use the following method:
1. Say you want to sort by column A, then by column B. Perhaps column A has multiples of the same thing, or maybe you have the bill type in column A (example: HB, SB) and the bill number in column B.
2. First click in column B, then sort that column.
3. Next click in column A, then sort by that column.

Since you sorted column B first, when you sort by column A, all of the column A items that are similar will be grouped together, and within each of those groupings, column B will be sorted.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 2

title: <u>Using the Filter Function</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# <u>Using the Filter Function</u>

Filtering allows you to display only the rows of data that meet certain criteria.

To turn on Excel's filtering feature:
1. Make sure the cell pointer is somewhere within your dataset, then select the entire dataset by pressing Ctrl+A (hold down the Ctrl key as you press the letter "A").
2. Click on the Data command tab, and click on the Filter button:

![Excel Data tab ribbon showing the Filter button circled in red within the Sort & Filter group.](image)

You should now see dropdown buttons next to each column header:

![Excel spreadsheet view showing dropdown filter arrows circled in red next to column headers 'Measure', 'Report Title', and 'Description'.](image)

You will use these dropdowns to filter your data.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 3

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 4

Let's say you want to see only bills that have a referral to FIN. You would click on the dropdown for the House Referral column, uncheck the box for "Select All" (so that all of the check marks go away), then put a check mark next to "FIN":

![Screenshot of Excel filter dropdown menu for House Referral column. Red circles highlight the filter dropdown arrow, the "Select All" checkbox, and the "FIN" checkbox.](image)

When you click on the OK button, only the rows that have "FIN" as their referral would show. All other rows will be hidden.

Other things to notice are:

![Screenshot of filtered Excel spreadsheet. Red circles highlight the filter icon in the House Referral column header, the blue row numbers (17, 18, 19), and the status bar showing "9 of 24 records found".](image)

The dropdown for the House Referral looks different (indicating that filtering is happening on that column); the row numbers are now blue; and the status bar along the bottom shows how many records meet the filter criteria.

**NOTE:** If you filter on more than one column, the only rows that show up are those that meet ALL specified criteria.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 4

To clear all filters at once, click on the Clear button on the Data command tab:

title: <u>Custom Filters</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# <u>Custom Filters</u>

Excel can also do specialized, custom filters. Depending on the type of data you have, when you drop down a filter, you'll see an option for text filters (pictured below), number filters, or date filters.

![Screenshot of Excel's House Referral column filter menu showing Text Filters options like Equals, Does Not Equal, Begins With, Ends With, Contains, Does Not Contain, and Custom Filter.](image)

If you choose any of the filters from this flyout menu, you'll get a dialog box similar to the following:

![Screenshot of the Custom AutoFilter dialog box in Excel. It shows options to "Show rows where: House Referral begins with" followed by a text entry field, with radio buttons for "And" and "Or" logic.](image)

From here you can specify filters to match anything that begins or ends with specific text, or match anything that contains or does not contain specific text. It's fairly flexible. For example, this will help you search for any measure that needs to end up in FIN, or any measure that has AGR as the lead at some point.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 6

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
To find measures with AGR as the lead in the first part of the referral, use begins with "AGR":

![Custom AutoFilter dialog box showing "House Referral" "begins with" "AGR"](image)

To find measures with AGR as the lead in the second (or subsequent) part of the referral, use contains ", AGR" (that's a comma then a space, followed by AGR):

![Custom AutoFilter dialog box showing "House Referral" "contains" ", AGR"](image)

To find measures that have AGR as the lead in the first part of the referral and need to be laterally referred, use begins with "AGR" and contains a comma:

![Custom AutoFilter dialog box showing "House Referral" "begins with" "AGR" AND "contains" ","](image)

For number filters, you'll get options for things like greater than or less than a certain value, above or below the average, and other cool things.

For date filters, you get options for before, after, or on a specific date. You also get interesting ones like "Next Week" or "Yesterday". The nice thing about those filters is that when you open the workbook the next day, the filter will adjust to continue to show things from the week ahead or the day prior.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 7

title: <u>Saving Custom Filters</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# <u>Saving Custom Filters</u>

You can create custom filters, then save them in your Excel workbook, if you would like to reapply them later.

Begin by setting up your LiveStatus workbook using your HMSO LiveStatus Template or a blank LiveStatus document and creating a report.

Once you have your LiveStatus report, click on the House Referral column header, then select Filter under the Data tab.

![Screenshot of the Excel Data tab ribbon, highlighting the Filter button within the Sort & Filter group with a red circle.](image)

Click on the arrow that appears in the bottom right corner of the House Referral column header, select Text Filters, then Custom Filter.

![Screenshot of an Excel spreadsheet showing the filter dropdown menu for the "House Referral" column. Red circles highlight the filter arrow on the column header, the "Text Filters" menu option, and the "Custom Filter..." option in the submenu.](image)

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 8

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
<u>**Creating a Filter for Triple Referral Bills:**</u>

1. Enter the characters below, replacing "COM" with your committee's acronym:
   **Begins with: COM\*, \*,**
2. Hit OK.
3. Go to the View tab, then click Custom Views
4. Select "Add"
5. Name your filter as "Triple Referrals" (or any other name you choose)
6. Hit OK.

***

<u>**Creating a Filter for Double Referral Bills**</u>

1. Enter the characters below, replacing "COM" with your committee's acronym:
   **Begins with: COM\*, \***
   **AND**
   **Does not contain: \*, \*,**
2. Hit OK.
3. Go to the View tab, then click Custom Views
4. Select "Add"
5. Name your filter as "Double Referrals" (or any other name you choose)
6. Hit OK.

***

<u>**Creating a Filter for Single Referral Bills**</u>

1. Enter the characters below, replacing "COM" with your committee's acronym:
   **Begins with COM\***
   **AND**
   **Does not contain: ,**
2. Hit OK
3. Go to the View tab, then click Custom Views
4. Select "Add"
5. Name your filter as "Single Referrals" (or any other name you choose)
6. Hit OK

***

<u>**To view your filter:**</u>

1. Go back to the View tab;
2. Click Custom Views;
3. Select your filter, then click Show

***

<u>**To Clear Your Filter:**</u>

1. Under the Data tab, unselect "Filter";
2. Click on the arrow that appears in the bottom right corner of the House Referral column header, Choose "Clear Filter From House Referral"; or
3. Press Ctrl+Shift+L (this is the shortcut key to turn your filter on and off)

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 9

**Quick note on custom filters:**

Occasionally, a custom filter may cause some of your worksheet panes to freeze. (You will notice it if you scroll down and some of the rows aren’t scrolling and seem frozen in place.) If this happens, don't worry! Your worksheet will be ok. To fix this issue, go to your View tab. click on Freeze Panes, and select **Freeze Top Row**. This will reset your worksheet to the default.

title: <u>Conditional Formatting</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 13

# <u>Conditional Formatting</u>

Filtering hides rows that don't meet your criteria. If you don't want to hide rows and you merely want to highlight them in some significant way, then you'll want to use conditional formatting. One big benefit of using conditional formatting over filtering is that you can set multiple conditional formats and have records look different depending on what criteria you set. Also, you can re-run the Update All (or Auto Update) button on the Buttons worksheet and when the Report sheet updates, the conditional formatting will highlight any new records that meet the criteria and un-highlight records that no longer meet the criteria.

![Excel Home tab ribbon showing the Conditional Formatting menu with options like Highlight Cells Rules, Top/Bottom Rules, Data Bars, Color Scales, Icon Sets, New Rule, Clear Rules, and Manage Rules.](image)

To set conditional formatting, highlight the column in which you wish to set criteria and highlight on (by clicking on the column header); then on the Home tab, click on the Conditional Formatting button; then choose New Rule from the dropdown. In the New Formatting Rule dialog box, under Select a Rule Type, choose "Format only cells that contain". In the Edit the Rule Description section, in the first dropdown, choose "Specific Text". In the next dropdown, your choices are "containing", "not containing", "beginning with", and "ending with". Depending on what you want to do, choose the appropriate selection. In the box to the right of that, type the text you are looking for.

![New Formatting Rule dialog box in Excel. The rule type "Format only cells that contain" is selected. In the Edit the Rule Description section, "Specific Text" and "containing" are selected from dropdown menus. A secondary dropdown menu is open showing options: Cell Value, Specific Text, Dates Occurring, Blanks, No Blanks, Errors, and No Errors.](image)

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 13

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
After you've made your selections, click on the **Format** button – this is where you will tell Excel what format to use for cells that meet the criteria you've specified. You probably want to choose the **Fill** tab on this dialog box. Now pick a color that you want to fill the cells with.

![The Format Cells dialog box in Excel, showing the Fill tab selected with various background color options, pattern settings, and a sample preview of a light red fill.](image)

From here you would click on **OK**, then **OK** again to close out the dialog boxes. Now all cells that meet your criteria are colored in the way you wanted. They'll stand out nicely.

You can set multiple criteria by adding new rules and cells that meet different criteria can be colored differently. So, following in the examples in the previous section, perhaps you want to make all cells that begin with "JHA" in one color, and all cells that contain ", JHA" in another – thus separating the bills that JHA needs to deal with before the lateral deadline from the bills it will need to deal with after the lateral deadline.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 14

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 15

<u>**Sorting on Color**</u>

Once you have set conditional formatting to highlight cells with different criteria in different colors, you can now sort the list by color. As an example, let's say you've set the status column so that all cells with "Passed Second Reading" are blue, all cells with "Public Decision Making" are yellow, and all cells with "Passed, with Amendments" are green. So now you want to group the cells by color. With your cell pointer somewhere in the database, from the Data command tab, click on the Sort button:

![Screenshot of the Excel Data tab ribbon, highlighting the Sort button within the Sort & Filter group.](image)

You will get the Sort dialog box:

![Screenshot of the initial Excel Sort dialog box with default settings.](image)

First, make sure the checkbox for "My data has headers" is checked. This ensures that Excel recognizes the header row, Row 1. In the first dropdown box, choose "Current Status". Change the next dropdown from "Values" to "Cell Color". Dropdown the next box and choose the color you want on top. Next, click on the Add Level button and repeat the process, choosing the color you want next. Do the same for the third color, until your dialog box looks something like this:

![Screenshot of the Excel Sort dialog box configured with three levels of sorting by Cell Color for the "Current Status" column: Green on top, then Blue, then Yellow.](image)

When you click on OK, the sheet with have the green cells on top, the blue ones after that, followed by the yellow cells, and then any cells that do not have any color.

Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 15

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
**Using Flash Fill** Flash fill can be used to extract data from a column, or it can be used to combine data from two or more columns into one column. Prior to the invention of this feature, you would have had to use fancy text formulas to accomplish this. Now you can show Excel what you want to do and have Excel figure it out. Most commonly, people want to sort their bill lists by bill number. Here's an example:
A B
1 **Measure** **Measure Title**
2 HR1 ELECTING THE CHAIR OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.
3 HCR1 RELATING TO RECESS DAYS FOR THE REGULAR SESSION OF 2021.
4 HR2 ELECTING THE SPEAKER AND VICE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.
5 HR3 ELECTING THE OFFICERS OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.
6 HB1 MAKING APPROPRIATIONS TO PROVIDE FOR THE EXPENSES OF THE LEGISLATURE.
7 HB26 RELATING TO UNEMPLOYMENT BENEFITS.
8 HB101 RELATING TO TORT LIABILITY.
9 HB3 RELATING TO REVENUE GENERATION.
The problem here is that when you try to sort by the Measure column, Excel sees that column as text, not numbers. And text is usually sorted character by character. For example, when comparing HB101 to HB3, you and I can clearly see that 3 comes before 101, but when Excel is doing a character by character comparison, when it gets to the third character of the measure, it will compare "3" to "1" and since 1 comes before 3, it will be HB101 before HB3. What you'll need to do is extract the bill number, or extract both the bill type and number if you have a mixture of bill types (HB, SB, HCR, etc.). To do this you would create a new column. It doesn't have to be right next to the Measure column, but there cannot be any blank columns between this new column and the Measure column. In the new column, on Row 2, you would type "HR" and press Enter. This puts your cell pointer in Row 3. Type "HC" and pause. Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 16

title: Getting More Out of LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 17 If your screen looks something like this, Excel has guessed what you want to do. All you have to do at this point is press Enter. Excel will flash fill everything going all the way down.
A B C
1 Measure Type Measure Title
2 HR1 HR ELECTING THE CHAIR OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.
3 HCR1 HCR RELATING TO RECESS DAYS FOR THE REGULAR SESSION OF 2021.
4 HR2 <mark style="background-color: grey">HR ELECTING THE SPEAKER AND VICE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.
5 HR3 <mark style="background-color: grey">HR ELECTING THE OFFICERS OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.
6 HB1 <mark style="background-color: grey">HB MAKING APPROPRIATIONS TO PROVIDE FOR THE EXPENSES OF THE LEGISLATURE.
7 HB26 <mark style="background-color: grey">HB RELATING TO UNEMPLOYMENT BENEFITS.
8 HB101 <mark style="background-color: grey">HB RELATING TO TORT LIABILITY.
9 HB3 <mark style="background-color: grey">HB RELATING TO REVENUE GENERATION.
If Excel can't, or just doesn't guess, then complete the second entry and press Enter. With you cell pointer on the next row down (Row 4), press Ctrl+E or click on the Data command tab and click on the Flash Fill button. ![Screenshot of the Excel Data Tools ribbon highlighting the Flash Fill button with a red circle.](image) To extract the number portion, you would do the same thing, typing only the number portion of the measure and using flash fill to have Excel do all the work for you. You can use this same trick to extract the data from the Status column. Flash fill can also be used to combine information from multiple columns. For example, if you had first names in one column, middle names in another column, and last names in a third column, you could create a fourth column that combines all three. You could also do things like the first name, middle initial, and last name. Excel is pretty smart at figuring out what you are trying to do. Getting More Out of LiveStatus Last Updated 02-01-21 Page | 17

title: <u>Text to Columns</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/Getting More Out of LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# <u>Text to Columns</u>

For the House Referral column, there are times in which you may want to split it into multiple columns. For example, double and triple referrals, which are separated by commas (*e.g.*, PDP, FIN) can be separated into two columns. To do this, make sure there is a sufficient number of blank columns to the right of that column, then highlight the column with the House referrals and click on Data, Text to Columns.

In Step 1 of the text to columns wizard, you would choose "Delimited", then click on the Next button.

![Screenshot of the Convert Text to Columns Wizard - Step 1 of 3. The "Delimited" radio button is selected. The preview of selected data shows House Referral codes like EEP/LAT, CPC, FIN.](image)

In Step 2 of the wizard, make sure the Comma checkbox is checked, then click on Finish.

![Screenshot of the Convert Text to Columns Wizard - Step 2 of 3. The "Comma" checkbox is checked under Delimiters. The data preview shows the text split into columns.](image)

Getting More Out of LiveStatus | Last Updated 02-01-21 | Page | 18

title: <mark>What is a resolution?</mark>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 Resolutions.pdf
text:
# <mark>What is a resolution?</mark>

*   A legislative measure that expresses the will or wish of either the House (HR), the Senate (SR), or both (HCR, SCR).
*   Usually does not have the force of law, but **still reflects the position of the House and the Legislature.**

2

title: <mark>Types of Resolutions</mark>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 Resolutions.pdf
text:
# <mark>Types of Resolutions</mark>

<u>Non-substantive</u>
* **Congratulatory Resolutions/Certificates.** Not referred to committees. Usually no limit to offer, but congratulatory resolutions are only used for floor presentations.
* **Procedural Resolutions.** Involve the workings of the House. Generally offered by Leadership for internal process reasons.

<u>Substantive</u>
* <u>**Policy Resolutions and Study Resolutions.** These resolutions, which declare the wishes or position of the House, may be House concurrent resolutions (HCRs), or House resolutions (HRs).</u>

3

title: PRESENTATION 2026 Resolutions
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 Resolutions.pdf
text:
<mark># What Can Resolutions Do?</mark>

* Request the establishment of a working group or task force
* Request an audit
* Urge another body, such as a county, to take certain steps regarding county-specific issues
* Bring awareness to certain issues through the designation of days, weeks, or months

4

title: <mark>What Can't Resolutions Do?</mark>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 Resolutions.pdf
text:
# <mark>What Can't Resolutions Do?</mark>

* Require or demand an entity, agency, or organization take specific actions
* Amend statute
* Appropriate funds

5

title: <mark>Where Do You Fall In?</mark>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 Resolutions.pdf
text:
# <mark>Where Do You Fall In?</mark>

* Resolutions do not need to be drafted by a drafting agency (HMSO, LRB, HMIR)
* You get to draft a resolution to be filed and introduced in the Legislature!
    - <u>With your boss's approval</u>
* Drafting agencies are still available to help you

![A cartoon illustration of a man in a suit holding a yellow document and pushing another man's face away with his hand.](image)

text:
<mark># Writing a Resolution</mark>

* Understand the goal of your resolution (will be your **title** and first **"Be it resolved"** clause)
* Gather as much information about the subject matter and **connect its importance to the State**
* Fact Check the information you use for your Whereas clauses (review news articles, auditor reports, past measures)
* Do not add too much information into your resolution, be clear and concise
* <u>Sign up for the House Training on Creating Resos</u>

## <mark>Legislative Process</mark>

source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 Staff Training Legislative Process.pptx
text:
2

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SENATE [SENATE BEGIN]
        S1[Introduced] --> S2[First Reading]
        S2 --> S3[Referral]
        S3 --> S4["Committee Hearing & Report"]
        S4 --> S5[Second Reading]
        S5 --> S6["Committee Hearing & Report (if any)"]
        S6 --> S7[48-Hr Notice]
        S7 --> S8[Third Reading]
        S8 --> S9[First Crossover]
    end

    subgraph HOUSE [HOUSE BEGIN]
        H1[Introduced] --> H2[First Reading]
        H2 --> H3[Referral]
        H3 --> H4["Committee Hearing & Report"]
        H4 --> H5[Second Reading]
        H5 --> H6["Committee Hearing & Report (if any)"]
        H6 --> H7[48-Hr Notice]
        H7 --> H8[Third Reading]
        H8 --> H9[First Crossover]
    end

    S9 --> H2
    H9 --> S2

    S9 --> SC[Second Crossover]
    H9 --> SC

    subgraph FINAL_STAGES [Final Stages]
        SC --> D["Disagree & Conference Committee"]
        SC --> A[Agree]
        SC --> U["Unamended (by non-originating house)"]
        D --> FR[Final Reading]
        A --> FR
        FR --> CSG[Certified & Sent to the Governor]
        U --> CSG
    end
```

![Illustration of the Hawaii State Capitol building centered between the Senate and House process flows.](image)

# Veto & Override Terminology

Governor may veto for any reason. However, the governor must give notice of an intent to veto.

If a bill is vetoed, the Legislature may override the veto by two-thirds majority vote in each chamber. If successful, the bill becomes law.

```mermaid graph TD subgraph SENATE_PROCESS [SENATE BEGIN] S_INTRO[Introduced] --> S_R1[First Reading] S_R1 --> S_REF[Referral] S_REF --> S_CHR[Committee Hearing & Report] S_CHR --> S_R2[Second Reading] S_R2 --> S_CHR2[Committee Hearing & Report
(if any)] S_CHR2 --> S_48H[48-Hr Notice] S_48H --> S_R3[Third Reading] S_R3 --> S_C1[First Crossover] end subgraph HOUSE_PROCESS [HOUSE BEGIN] H_INTRO[Introduced] --> H_R1[First Reading] H_R1 --> H_REF[Referral] H_REF --> H_CHR[Committee Hearing & Report] H_CHR --> H_R2[Second Reading] H_R2 --> H_CHR2[Committee Hearing & Report
(if any)] H_CHR2 --> H_48H[48-Hr Notice] H_48H --> H_R3[Third Reading] H_R3 --> H_C1[First Crossover] end S_C1 --> H_INTRO H_C1 --> S_INTRO S_C1 --> SC[Second Crossover] H_C1 --> SC subgraph FINAL_STAGES [Final Stages] SC --> DCC[Disagree &
Conference Committee] SC --> AG[Agree] SC --> UN[Unamended
(by non-originating
house)] end ``` ![Illustration of a government building, likely a capitol, situated between the Senate and House process diagrams.](image) ### Legislative Process Flow **Senate Path (Blue)** * **Introduced** * **First Reading** * **Referral** * **Committee Hearing & Report** * **Second Reading** * **Committee Hearing & Report (if any)** * **48-Hr Notice** * **Third Reading** * **First Crossover** (Leads to House Introduction or Second Crossover) **House Path (Red)** * **Introduced** * **First Reading** * **Referral** * **Committee Hearing & Report** * **Second Reading** * **Committee Hearing & Report (if any)** * **48-Hr Notice** * **Third Reading** * **First Crossover** (Leads to Senate Introduction or Second Crossover) **Final Resolution (Purple)** * **Second Crossover** * **Disagree & Conference Committee** * **Agree** * **Unamended (by non-originating house)**

text:
Readings
4

```mermaid
graph LR
    Start(( )) --> Introduced
    subgraph Process
        Introduced[Introduced] --> FirstReading[First Reading]
        FirstReading --> Referral[Referral]
        Referral -.-> Introduced
    end
    
    style Introduced fill:#b30000,color:#fff,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style FirstReading fill:#b30000,color:#fff,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style Referral fill:#b30000,color:#fff,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

Governor may veto for any reason. However, the governor must give notice of an intent to veto.

If a bill is vetoed, the Legislature may override the veto by two-thirds majority vote in each chamber. If successful, the bill becomes law.

```mermaid
    subgraph FINAL_STAGES [Final Stages]
        SC --> DCC[Disagree & Conference Committee]
        SC --> AG[Agree]
        SC --> UN[Unamended by non-originating house]
        
        DCC --> FR[Final Reading]
        AG --> FR
        
        FR --> CSG[Certified & Sent to the Governor]
        UN --> CSG
    end
```

![Illustration of the Hawaii State Capitol building behind the legislative process flowchart](image)

# Veto & Override

Governor may veto for any reason. However, the governor must give notice of an intent to veto.

If a bill is vetoed, the Legislature may override the veto by two-thirds majority vote in each chamber. If successful, the bill becomes law.

# Referrals

<description>
A diamond shape containing the number 5 is positioned to the left of the main heading "Referrals".
</description>

<mark>**Bills are referred to one or more committees.**</mark>

<mark>
**Commas signify successive referrals**

**Ex: HSG, JHA**

**Bill goes to HSG first, which acts first, then JHA**
</mark>

<mark>
**Slashes signify joint referrals**

**Ex: HSG/JHA**

**Bill goes to HSG and JHA and they must act together**
</mark>

# Conference

### Joint Committee of senators and representatives

**Types of Actions:**
* **Agree with latest draft**
* **Amend (conference draft)**
* **Defer**

**Final Reading:**
* **If House agrees to SD, bill must pass Final Reading in House only**
* **If the committee amends the bill, the bill must pass Final Reading in both chambers**

# Veto & Override

<description>
The page has a light blue sky background with white clouds. A black horizontal line separates the header from the content. On the left side of this line, there is a diamond shape containing the number 11.
</description>

> **Governor may veto for any reason. However, the governor must give notice of an intent to veto.**

> **If a bill is vetoed, the Legislature may override the veto by two-thirds majority vote in each chamber. If successful, the bill becomes law.**

title: Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
# Overview of the Legislative Process

## <mark>Objectives of This Training</mark>

```description
A large rectangular graphic with a repeating geometric pattern of triangles in various shades of blue.
```

This training is intended to help session staff in the following ways:

* Assist session staff gain familiarity with common legislative terminology;
* Provide a broad overview of the legislative process; and
* Introduce session staff to the specific processes and concepts that will be discussed in further detail in the other trainings.

title: Terminology
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
# Terminology

![Blue geometric pattern consisting of various shades of triangles.](image)

## *Session*

There are two general uses of the term "session".

First, "session" means the period of the year beginning with Opening Day (the third Wednesday in January) and ending with Sine Die. This is also referred to as the "Regular Session", in contrast to "Special Sessions" that occur at other times of the year.

Second, "session" refers to the floor sessions that occur in chamber (House meets as a whole). Floor session typically occurs at noon, but times may vary depending on the deadline and circumstances.

title: *Standing Committees or Committees*
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
# *Standing Committees or Committees*

Standing Committees consider and report any bills, resolutions, budgets, or any other matters referred to them.

They are organized by subject matter (i.e., HSG or the Committee on Housing will hear and consider bills and resolutions that affect housing development financing, assistance for homebuyers and renters, affordable and rental housing, public housing, and other pertinent matters referred to it by the House) and meeting times (morning / afternoon).

Committees consist of a Chair, Vice Chair, and Members.

# *Legislative Process*

The legislative process is the process by which a bill becomes a law. This training will primarily talk about **bills**, which are the *only* vehicles by which a law may be enacted. There are other legislative documents, such as resolutions and certificates. Those will be discussed in another training.

Most of the essential parts of the legislative process are mandated by the Hawaii State Constitution.

And this is where **you** (legislative staff) come in, because most of the work during the legislative session falls onto the staff to ensure that this process runs smoothly.

title: The Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf

title: 1. Idea / Concept
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
*The red path indicates the progress of House bills through the legislative process. Similarly, the blue path indicates the progress of Senate bills. The purple blocks indicate the final progress of both House and Senate bills once they pass Third Reading in both chambers.*

# 1. Idea / Concept

![Lightbulb icon with a gear inside, representing an idea or concept.](image)

Every piece of legislation, and the legislative process, begins with an **idea**. Ideas for legislation may come from a constituent, experts, government officials / departments, interest groups, or the legislator.

To become *law*, ideas must be drafted into a **bill**. The Hawaii State Constitution provides that "[n]o law shall pass except by bill."

*See the **Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates training** for a greater in-depth discussion of the various legislative documents and how they are typically employed.*

Further, **legislators** are the only persons that may introduce a bill. Thus, all ideas must be presented to a legislator, who then requests that a bill be drafted to bring that idea to life.

To have an idea drafted into a bill in the House, submit a request to one of the following drafting agencies (as appropriate):

title: 2. Bill Introduction
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
*   **Legislative Reference Bureau (LRB):** The nonpartisan drafting agency available to all representatives and senators. The LRB Request for Service form is on the House Gateway with the following pathway: House Gateway > House Tab > Forms > Under the header "Session Forms" > LRB Transmittal.

*   **House Majority Staff Office (HMSO):** The partisan drafting agency available to all majority party representatives. The HMSO Request for Service forms are accessible on the Office Manager's desktop in the folder labeled "HMSOManuals&Forms" or on the House Gateway with the following pathway: House Gateway > House Tab > Forms > Under the header "Session Forms" > HMSO Request Form - General.

*   **House Minority Research Office (HMIR):** The partisan drafting agency available to all minority party representatives.

*See the **Requests for Service training** for a greater in-depth discussion on the forms used to request bill drafting services and tips to assist agencies to fully understand a Representative's desired outcome.*

## 2. Bill Introduction

![Icon of a document with a signature line and an 'X' mark](image)

Once a drafting agency has transmitted a bill to the legislator, staff will be responsible for preparing the bill for introduction. Preparation will include the following:

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
*   Signature(s);
*   Paperwork; and
*   Filing.

***

### *Signature(s)*

***

For a bill to be introduced, a Representative must introduce it with a signature. Typically, the Representative who requested the bill from a drafting agency will be the Representative signing on the printed signature line. However, there are instances where a Representative gives the bill to another Representative for purposes of introduction. Additionally, the Representative may want other Representatives to sign the bill as primary introducers or co-sponsors using the e-sign process.

All signatures fall into the following categories:

*   **First Primary Introducer:** The Representative who signs on the printed line. On the Capitol website, this Representative will be listed first before all other introducers and sponsors;
*   **Primary Introducers:** Representatives who e-sign the bill as primary introducers will be listed alphabetically in all capital letters on the Capitol website;
*   **Co-Sponsors:** Representatives who e-sign the bill as a co-sponsor will be listed alphabetically in upper and lower case letters on the Capitol website; and

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
* **By Request (BR):** A Representative may introduce a bill by request or BR. Bills introduced by request will typically only have one signature with the (BR) notation next to it. One instance in which this is common are on bills proposed by other governmental bodies, such as the Executive Branch (through the Governor's Package), the Judicial Branch, and the counties. Signatures must be done with a wide-tipped black pen; ball point pen signatures will likely be rejected since they are difficult to see on the scanned copy of the bill. **TIP:** More information on the e-sign process, including how to prepare an e-sign invitation request, is on the House Gateway with the following pathway: House Gateway > Training Tab > Manuals & Guides > Under the header "E-Sign". *** ### *Paperwork* *** ![BILLS/RESOLUTIONS TRANSMITTAL SHEET HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES form](image) **BILLS/RESOLUTIONS TRANSMITTAL SHEET** **HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES** 1. List all documents being transmitted. 2. The "Document Name" is the footer on the bottom left corner of the document. 3. title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
**Bills / Resolutions Transmittal Sheet:** Staff indicate all documents being filed with House Chief Clerk's Office on this form.

This form is required for each *batch*. For example, if an office files four bills at the same time, only one transmittal sheet is necessary; but if the office returns five minutes later to file another batch of bills, then another transmittal sheet will be required.

The form is electronically accessible on House Gateway with the following pathway:

House Gateway > House Tab > Forms > Under the header "Session Forms"

***

### *Filing*

***

Once the bills are signed and the necessary paperwork is completed, they may be taken down to the House Chief Clerk's Office and filed.

***

### *Important Dates for Filing*

***

![Save the Date graphic featuring a stylized calendar icon.](image)

The introduction of bills are subject to deadlines and number constraints.

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
The following are important days during January, prior to the Bill Intro Deadline

*   **The second Wednesday in January or earlier:** This is the first day bills may be pre-filed for introduction.
*   **The third Wednesday in January:** Opening Day.
*   **The second (session) day after Opening Day:** Last day for introducing bills as part of a legislative caucus.
*   **The third (session) day after Opening Day:** The 5-bills-per-day limit begins. (Legislators may not introduce more than five bills in the entire day.)
*   **The seventh or eighth (calendar) day after Opening Day:** Last day to introduce bills.

***

### *Filing Limits*

***

![A purple triangle with a white exclamation mark inside, serving as a warning or attention symbol.](image)

In addition to the deadlines, each legislator is constrained in the number of bills they may introduce. Please see any memo from Speaker specifying the number of bills that may be introduced in each of the following categories:

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
*   **All Members:** Each member may introduce bills relating to the member's district, constituent concerns, or personal area of interest.

*   **Chairs:** Each Chair may introduce bills on behalf of the Chair's committee. Chairs of the following committees are typically allowed to introduce more bills:
    - Committee on Consumer Protection & Commerce;
    - Committee on Education;
    - Committee on Finance;
    - Committee on Higher Education & Technology; and
    - Committee on Judiciary & Hawaiian Affairs.

Additionally, all Chairs may introduce 5 short form bills.

*   **Caucuses:** Each Caucus may introduce bills as part of its caucus package.

NOTE: The 5-bills-per-day limit applies to all of the above. For example, if a Representative waits until the 5-bills-per-day period begins to file bills, the Representative will be limited to filing 5 bills per day even though the Representative may file more bills in total.

### 3. Readings

![Icon of a person sitting at a desk reading or working](image)

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
Once a bill is introduced, it is put to the House for its *first reading*. A **reading** is a vote on the bill by the House as a whole. The Hawaii State Constitution requires that a bill pass three readings in each chamber before a bill becomes law. The *first reading* occurs right after a bill is introduced and the House (by its own rule) passes the bill based simply on its title. No further scrutiny is given to the bill to pass first reading. The *second reading* occurs after the bill is reported from a Standing Committee. The *third reading* occurs after the last committee referred to consider the bill has reported the bill. NOTE: The Hawaii Supreme Court recently held that the three readings must begin anew after a non-germane amendment changes the object or subject of a bill so that it is no longer related to the original bill as introduced. Thus, a bill may need to undergo more than three readings depending on the extent of the amendments. ***

All readings shown in the process:

*** ```mermaid graph LR A[Introduction] --> B[1st Reading] B --> C[Commitee] C --> D[2nd Reading] D --> E["Commitee
(if any)"] E --> F[3rd Reading] style A fill:none,stroke:none style B fill:none,stroke:none style C fill:none,stroke:none style D fill:none,stroke:none style E fill:none,stroke:none style F fill:none,stroke:none ``` ![A flowchart showing the legislative process: Introduction (folder icon), 1st Reading (checklist icon), Commitee (group meeting icon), 2nd Reading (checklist icon), Commitee (if any) (group meeting icon), and 3rd Reading (checklist icon).](image)

The Legislative Process (Cont.)

```description
A large rectangular banner with a geometric pattern of triangles in various shades of blue and purple.
```

### 4. Referrals

![A purple icon showing a curved arrow pointing out of a square box, representing a referral or share action.](image)

After *first reading*, bills are referred to one or more committees. Per House Rules, referrals are done by the Speaker of the House.

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
This training is intended to only provide a brief overview of the referral process, however later portions of this training will provide a more in-depth discussion of referrals. As a preview, the following are the types of referrals: **Single:** Bill is referred to one committee for consideration. *Example: A bill has the referral of JHA. This bill is only referred to one committee: the House Committee on Judiciary and Hawaiian Affairs (JHA).* **Double:** Bill is referred to two committees for consideration. *Example: A bill has the referral of AGR, JHA. This bill will be heard by the House Committee on Agriculture & Food Systems (AGR) first. AGR may hear the bill and, if it deems appropriate, report the bill from committee. Only after AGR reports the bill may JHA hear, consider, and report the bill. The comma in the referral indicates that subsequent committees may not consider the bill until prior committees have heard and reported the bill.* **Triple:** Bill is referred to three committees for consideration. *Example: A bill has the referral of AGR, CPC, JHA. Similar to a double referral, this bill will be heard by AGR first. If AGR reports the bill out of committee, then the House Committee on Consumer Protection & Commerce (CPC) may hear and report the bill out. After CPC reports the bill out of committee, then JHA may hear and report the bill out.* **Joint:** More than one committee must jointly hear, consider, and report out the bill. *Example: A bill has the referral of AGR/JHA. In this example, both AGR and JHA must simultaneously hear, consider, and report out the bill with the same recommendation. The slash indicates that committees must jointly take action on a bill.* ***

**_Why do referrals matter?_**

***

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
![A purple stopwatch icon](image)

**DEADLINES!** The Legislature established deadlines to ensure the timely progress of bills through the legislative process. Bills that do not progress past a certain deadline on time are essentially put on hold until the next regular session.

Here are the deadlines:

**Triple Referral Filing:** For bills with three or more committee referrals (triples or more), the bill must be filed by the first committee by this date.

*Example:* If the referral is AGR, CPC, JHA - the AGR committee must schedule, hear, consider, and file the bill by this deadline.

*Note:* If the referral is AGR/CPC, JHA - this bill does not need to be filed by the Triple Referral Filing deadline because there are technically only two committees that need to hear this measure: the joint AGR/CPC counts as one committee.

**Lateral Filing:** For bills with two or more committee referrals (doubles or more), the bill must be filed by the second-to-last committee by this date.

*Example:* If the referral is AGR, CPC, JHA - the CPC committee must schedule, hear, consider, and file the bill by the Lateral Filing deadline. Note that CPC won't have an opportunity to

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
schedule, hear, consider, and file the bill if AGR does not do so by the Triple Referral Filing deadline.

*Example:* If the referral is AGR, JHA - the AGR committee must schedule, hear, consider, and file the bill by the Lateral Filing deadline.

**Decking:** For all bills, the bill must be filed by the last committee by this date.

*Example:* If the referral is AGR, CPC, JHA - the JHA committee must schedule, hear, consider, and file the bill by the Decking deadline. Note again that JHA won't have the opportunity if AGR does not file by the Triple Referral Filing deadline and CPC does not file by the Lateral Filing deadline.

*Example:* If the referral is JHA - then the JHA committee must schedule, hear, consider, and file the bill by the Decking deadline.

***

### *More Examples:*

***

Click on the card to see the answers.

### 5. Committee Hearings and Reports

![Icon depicting three people in a meeting with a speech bubble containing lines of text above them.](image)

Your bill has been (1) drafted from an idea, (2) signed and introduced, (3) passed First Reading, and (4) referred to committees. Now a committee may schedule the bill for a hearing and

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
consider the bill. This training will cover highlights of hearings and committee reports while other trainings go further in-depth.

The **Chair** of the committee determines whether a bill is scheduled for a hearing or not. If a hearing is scheduled, the committee will receive testimony from interested persons, such as affected agencies, businesses, nonprofit organizations, and concerned citizens.

The committee may take several actions on the bill:

*   **Pass As Is:** The committee votes to report the bill out of committee without changing any language in the bill;
*   **Pass With Amendments:** The committee votes to report the bill out of committee with some changes to the language of the bill;
*   **Hold:** The committee votes to keep the bill in committee and halt its progress through the legislative process; and
*   **Defer:** The Chair, without calling for a vote of the committee, may defer a bill for consideration. A defer may be indefinite or the chair may specify a date on which the committee will consider the bill further.

Note that in the first three actions, the committee votes to take the actions. Also note that if a committee votes to pass a bill out of committee, a committee report must be drafted to express that intent. If a bill is passed with amendments, an amended bill must accompany the committee report.

*See the **Scheduling Hearings and Legislative Timetable training** for a greater in-depth discussion on the procedures of scheduling a hearing and helpful tips.*

title: 6. Crossover
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
See the **Public Hearings training** for a greater in-depth discussion on the procedures during and after the hearing and helpful tips.

## 6. Crossover

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Introduction] --> B[1st Reading]
    B --> C[Commitee]
    C --> D[2nd Reading]
    D --> E["Commitee\n(if any)"]
    E --> F[3rd Reading]
    F --> G[First\nCrossover]
    
    style A fill:none,stroke:none
    style B fill:none,stroke:none
    style C fill:none,stroke:none
    style D fill:none,stroke:none
    style E fill:none,stroke:none
    style F fill:none,stroke:none
    style G fill:none,stroke:none
```

Your bill has been (1) drafted from an idea, (2) signed and introduced, (3) passed First Reading, (4) referred to committees, and (5) heard by and reported out of the first committee.

Once reported out of the first committee, the bill will be up for its Second Reading. After Second Reading, the remaining committees, if there are any, will have the opportunity to hear, consider, and report the bill. If the bill is reported out of all committees, the bill will be up for its Third Reading.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[First\nCrossover] --> B[1st Reading]
    B --> C[Commitee]
    C --> D[2nd Reading]
    D --> E["Commitee\n(if any)"]
    E --> F[3rd Reading]

    style A fill:none,stroke:none
    style B fill:none,stroke:none
    style C fill:none,stroke:none
    style D fill:none,stroke:none
    style E fill:none,stroke:none
    style F fill:none,stroke:none
```

Once a bill passes Third Reading in the House, it will cross over to the Senate for consideration by the Senate, where it repeats the process in the Senate with passage of First Reading, referral, committee consideration, Second Reading, further committee consideration, and Third Reading. While the House bills are considered in the Senate, the Senate bills are heard in the House.

title: After a bill passes Third Reading in the non-originating house, the bill follows one of the following paths:
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
# After a bill passes Third Reading in the non-originating house, the bill follows one of the following paths:

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[3rd Reading] --> Unamended
    Start --> Amended
    
    Unamended --> Gov[Governor for approval]
    
    Amended --> Cross[2nd Crossover]
    
    Cross --> Agree
    Cross --> Disagree
    
    Agree --> Final[Originating house does a Final Reading]
    Disagree --> Conf[Conference]

    style Start fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Gov fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Final fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Conf fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

If your bill passes Third Reading in the Senate, it will do one of the follow:

*   If it passed the Senate with no amendments, the bill will go straight to the Governor for the Governor's approval;

*   If the bill was amended by the Senate at any point, then the House will scrutinize the Senate version and determine whether to agree with that version or not:

    -   If the House agrees, the House votes on the Senate version in a Final Reading. If the bill passes Final Reading, it is sent to the Governor; or

title: 7. Conference
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
- If the House disagrees, the bill goes to Conference.

## 7. Conference

![Two people sitting at a table facing each other, representing a meeting or conference.](image)

In Conference, a joint committee (Conference Committee) comprising Representatives and Senators (called Managers of the Conference Committee) considers the two versions of a bill -- the House version and the Senate version -- and determines what will be the ultimate version of the bill.

After the Conference Committee considers the bill, the Committee may take one of three actions:

* The House Managers can agree with the Senate draft of a House measure and vote to agree (Senate managers do not vote)(vice versa for Senate Managers with a House draft of a Senate measure);
* The House and Senate Managers may pass out an amended version of the bill (Conference Draft);
* The House and Senate Managers may decide to defer the bill (no need to vote).

title: 1 Overview of the Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
If the House Managers agree to the Senate draft of a House measure, the measure must pass Final Reading in the House only. If the Conference Committee passes out a Conference Draft, the Conference Draft must pass Final Reading in both the House and the Senate.

Once the bills pass the appropriate Final Readings, they are sent to the Governor for approval.

### 8. Governor

![A blue silhouette icon of a person representing the Governor.](image)

All bills that pass the Legislature, with the exception of constitutional amendments, are submitted to the Governor for approval. The Governor may take three actions:

*   **Approve and Sign:** The Governor may affirmatively approve the bill by signing the bill into law;
*   **Allow the Bill to Become Law Without a Signature:** The Governor, for any reason, may choose not to sign the bill, but still allow the bill to become law; or
*   **Disapprove and Veto the Bill:** The Governor may disapprove of the bill and veto the bill. To veto a bill, the Governor must provide advance notice and the Legislature may

title: That's the Legislative Process!
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/1 Overview of the Legislative Process.pdf
text:
convene a Special Session to override a veto with two-thirds of each chamber voting in approval.

## That's the Legislative Process!

![Blue geometric pattern of triangles in various shades of blue.](image)

This completes the legislative process portion of this Sway training, please proceed to the next portion of the training covering Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates. If viewing this Sway in full screen mode, you may need to press Esc and then scroll down to see the next part of the presentation.

text:
![A hand reaching out from under a large pile of envelopes and papers labeled "BILL" and "TAX".](image)

# Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates

*Here's **Bill** and hopefully he's familiar to some of you.*

![A cartoon of "Bill" from Schoolhouse Rock sitting on the steps of the U.S. Capitol building next to a young boy.](image)

title: What is a Bill?
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
![An anthropomorphic rolled-up document character wearing a "BILL" badge, sitting on the steps of a capitol building.](image)

## What is a Bill?

![A glowing lightbulb hanging from a cord against a yellow background with abstract shapes and symbols, representing an idea.](image)

***

*It starts with an **IDEA**.*

*A bill is simply an idea that someone would like to see become law. It could be anything from the penalty for committing a crime to the amount of money that can be spent on a state program. The idea can come from anyone, but only a State Representative or State Senator can take the idea and guide it to final passage through the State Legislature.*

title: 2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
*There are two basic types of legislation: **bills** and **resolutions**. The official legislative process begins when a bill or resolution is introduced, numbered, and referred to a committee.*

![An illustration of a person sitting at a drafting desk, surrounded by various creative and technical symbols like a lightbulb, geometric shapes, a ruler, and a paintbrush, representing the process of creation or drafting.](image)

title: Types of Bills
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
# Types of Bills

![A dictionary entry for the word "legislation" highlighted in pink. The text reads: "principles, honesty. 2. the state undivided legislation noun 1. the act of 2. a law or a body process of m"](image)

*While the Hawaii State Constitution requires bills to contain certain elements, neither constitutional nor statutory law prescribes specific formats for particular types of bills. With the exception of bills that amend the State Constitution, elements of different types of bills may be combined in a single measure. In their basic form, examples of four of the most common types of bills are:*

1. *Bills amending laws in the **Hawaii Revised Statutes**;*

2. *Bills for **general (uncodified) laws, a.k.a Session Laws**, not intended or not appropriate for inclusion in the Hawaii Revised Statutes;*

3. ***Appropriation bills*** *(actually a subset of No. 2, but common enough to justify a separate category); and*

title: Bills Amending the HRS
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
4. *Bills proposing **constitutional amendments**.*

![Meme of Gandalf from Lord of the Rings with the text "YOU SHALL NOT PASS...... EXCEPT BY BILL"](image)

*1 - The Hawaii State Constitution states that **"No law shall pass except by bill."** This means that bills are the only method by which a law may be enacted.*

## Bills Amending the HRS

![A wooden gavel resting in front of a row of law books titled "Revised Laws of Hawaii 1955"](image)

> *The **Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS)** and **Session Laws of Hawaii (SLH)** are comprised of measures passed by the Hawaii State Legislature and enacted into law. All laws passed and enacted into law in a given year are included in the SLH for that year. However, not all laws printed in the SLH will be codified in the HRS. Usually, the HRS only includes laws of permanent importance.*

For example, a law permanently raising the fine for jaywalking to $500 will likely be included in the HRS. However, a law establishing a temporary one-year task force would likely be published

title: 2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
in the SLH only. Similarly the State Budget or State Supplemental Budget Act for a given year will only be included in the SLH for that year.

![A row of blue and white hardbound volumes of the Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS) on a shelf.](image)

*2 - Every office should have hard copies of the HRS with cumulative supplements and the yearly volumes of the Session Laws of Hawaii.*

![A row of white hardbound volumes of the Session Laws of Hawaii (SLH) on a shelf, showing various years and sessions.](image)

*Here's an example of a request for a bill to:*

1. *Remove the tip credit; and*

2. *Increase the minimum wage.*

*\*You can use the slider to see the existing statutory provisions and the changes made to accomplish the request.*

# A BILL FOR AN ACT

RELATING TO THE MINIMUM WAGE.

**BE IT ENACTED BY THE LEGISLATURE OF THE STATE OF HAWAII:**

SECTION 1. Section 387-2, Hawaii Revised Statutes, is amended to read as follows:

"**§387-2 Minimum wages.** (a) Except as provided in section 387-9 and this section, every employer shall pay to each employee employed by the employer, wages at the rate of not less than:
(1) $6.25 per hour beginning January 1, 2003;
(2) $6.75 per hour beginning January 1, 2006;
(3) $7.25 per hour beginning January 1, 2007;
(4) $7.75 per hour beginning January 1, 2015;
(5) $8.50 per hour beginning January 1, 2016;
(6) $9.25 per hour beginning January 1, 2017; and
(7) $10.10 per hour beginning January 1, 2018.

(b) The hourly wage of a tipped employee may be deemed to be increased on account of tips if the employee is paid not less than:
(1) 25 cents;
(2) 50 cents per hour beginning January 1, 2015; and
(3) 75 cents per hour beginning January 1, 2016,
below the applicable minimum wage by the employee's employer and the combined amount the employee receives from the employee's employer and in tips is at least 50 cents more than the applicable minimum wage; provided that beginning January 1, 2015, the combined amount the employee receives from the employee's employer and in tips is at least $7.00 more than the applicable minimum wage."

SECTION 2. Statutory material to be repealed is bracketed and stricken. New statutory material is underscored.

SECTION 3. This Act shall take effect on July 1, 2021.

title: Session Laws
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
# Session Laws

![A large group of people gathered in a legislative chamber, with hula dancers performing in the center aisle.](image)

*Session Laws are the laws of the State published in the exact form enacted each Regular Session by the Legislature. The Session Laws of a particular year, for example the Session Laws of Hawaii 2021, include all Acts enacted by the Legislature and not vetoed by the Governor, in the order by which they became law. Thus, these Acts are arranged solely in chronological order, not by subject.*

*Laws considered to be of a general and permanent nature are organized by general subject area and codified as the Hawaii Revised Statutes. The fact that an Act (or any part thereof) is not codified in the Hawaii Revised Statutes does not make that Act or provision any less of a "law".*

*Any provision in any of the Session Laws of Hawaii of any year that is not repealed or otherwise lapsed by operation of law is a validly enacted law, regardless of whether it is codified in the Hawaii Revised Statutes.*

text:
True or False: A bill for a 2 year pilot-project would be codified in the HRS?

**False.** The timeframe of 2 years makes the law temporary and unlikely to be codified in the HRS.

HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES
TWENTY-NINTH LEGISLATURE, 2018
STATE OF HAWAII
H.B. NO. 2467 H.D. 1
Page 2
H.B. NO. 2467 H.D. 1

# A BILL FOR AN ACT
RELATING TO LABOR.

<mark>Bill to be compiled in SLH</mark>

**BE IT ENACTED BY THE LEGISLATURE OF THE STATE OF HAWAII:**

**SECTION 1.** The legislature finds that health, safety, and efficiency are key cost considerations in construction projects. The legislature further finds that Hawaii continues to face high costs of construction as the State and counties continue to explore how to create more affordable housing and transit-oriented development.

The purpose of this Act is to require the legislative reference bureau to conduct a study of the factors that contribute to the costs of construction projects in the State.

**SECTION 2.** (a) The legislative reference bureau shall conduct a study on the factors that contribute to the costs of construction projects in the State. The study shall also include key contributors to cost overruns in construction projects.

(b) The legislative reference bureau shall submit the study, including findings and recommendations, to the legislature no later than twenty days before the convening of the regular session of 2019.

**SECTION 3.** There is appropriated out of the general revenues of the State of Hawaii the sum of $      or so much thereof as may be necessary for fiscal year 2018-2019 for the legislative reference bureau to conduct a study of the factors that contribute to the costs of construction projects in the State.

The sum appropriated shall be expended by the legislative reference bureau for the purposes of this Act.

**SECTION 4.** This Act shall take effect on January 1, 2050.

## Appropriations

![A scattered pile of United States one hundred dollar bills.](image)

title: Constitutional Amendments
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
*Similar to the federal government, our state government may not spend money unless the Legislature has appropriated the funds. Article VII, section 5, of the Hawaii State Constitution provides that: "No public money shall be expended except pursuant to appropriations made by law." In this context, an appropriation is a "legislative act authorizing the expenditure of a designated amount of public funds for a given purpose."*

*The Attorney General has characterized an appropriation bill as one that authorizes the expenditure of public money and stipulates the amount, manner, and purpose of the various items of expenditure. (Att. Gen. Op. 72-6.) As a practical matter, most of the appropriations authorized by the Legislature for state operating expenditures and capital expenditures are accomplished through the budgetary process.*

*Appropriations may be made through the designation of cash from a funding source, usually the general fund but sometimes from a special or revolving fund, or through authorizing the issuance of general obligation bonds, revenue bonds, or in certain cases, special purpose revenue bonds.*

## Constitutional Amendments

![The preamble of the United States Constitution written in calligraphy on parchment paper, starting with the words "We the People".](image)

title: 2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text: *Statutory law requires that any bill proposing a constitutional amendment contain the exact question that is to be printed on the ballot. The question must be phrased to require a "yes" or "no" response by the voter. Constitutional amendments must be ratified by the voters at a general election before they can take effect. A bill to implement a constitutional provision that has not yet been ratified cannot itself become effective before the constitutional provision.*

text:
*The Hawaii State Constitution requires all laws (bills) to have:*

* \* A **"Be It Enacted by the Legislature of the State of Hawaii" clause**; and
* \* A title, which shall embrace **one** subject. The scope of the title cannot be narrower than the scope of the subject matter of the bill.

*Additionally, most bills also have:*

* \* A **Ramseyering provision**, which tells you how the bill amends statutory language; and
    * \* An **effective date**.

*It is also a good practice to check that the bill contains:*

* \* The **correct year** and **legislature**; and
* \* The **correct bill** and, if any, **draft number(s)**. Bill draft numbers change as bills are amended by committees in each house.

No. 100), as well as a bar code at the bottom of the measure for the same purpose. The measure's number is added by the Chief Clerk's Office after the measure is introduced. Measure draft numbers are added and changed as the bill is amended by committees in each chamber.*

title: Drafting
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
# Drafting

![A pen resting on a spiral notebook, which is placed on top of a computer keyboard.](image)

***

*Anyone can write a bill; however, the drafting of the idea into a bill must go through a drafting agency, such as the Legislative Reference Bureau (the permanent, nonpartisan staff office of the Legislature) or a partisan staff office (including HMSO, SMA, HMIR, and SMO).*

*Bills are drafted by the drafting agencies and filed at the Chief Clerk's Office by the office of the Representative who is the primary introducer. House bills are drafted pursuant to Representative requests. These requests can be for new or amended bills, re-introductions of previous bills, and various "form" bills.*

***

title: 2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
Resolutions

![Seal of the State of Hawaii featuring the year 1959, King Kamehameha I, the Goddess of Liberty, the state coat of arms, and the motto "UA MAU KE EA O KA AINA I KA PONO"](image)

*Resolutions are legislative measures that express the will or wish of either the House (HR), the Senate (SR), or both chambers (HCR, SCR). Resolutions usually do not have the force of law, but still reflect the position of the House, Senate, or Legislature as a whole.*

title: 2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
*Note: Because resolutions generally do not have the force and effect of law, avoid trying to require or direct someone to do something through a resolution (i.e., do not use the words "shall", "is directed to", "is required to", etc.). Instead, a resolution more appropriately requests or urges a person, agency, or entity to do something.*

***You can draft resolutions and their committee reports.*** *In contrast to bills, resolutions can be drafted by staff and do not need to go through a drafting agency before filing. This can save you time if or when drafting agencies are inundated with bill drafting requests and immediate service is needed on a resolution. This goes for both substantive and nonsubstantive resolutions.*

![Illustration of several hands of various skin tones raised upwards.](image)

title: Types of Resolutions
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
# Types of Resolutions

![A vibrant display of numerous colorful flower leis hanging in rows at a market.](image)

*   ***Policy Resolutions and Study Resolutions:*** *Declare the wishes or position of the House. May be House Concurrent Resolutions (HCRs) or House Resolutions (HRs).*

*   ***Congratulatory Resolutions/Certificates:*** *Not referred to Committee. No limit to offer, but congratulatory resolutions are only used for floor presentations.*

    *   ***Procedural Resolutions:*** *Involve the workings of the House. Generally offered by Leadership for internal process reasons.*

title: Reso components
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2 Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates.pdf
text:
# Reso components

![A group of people standing in a semi-circle in a legislative chamber, many wearing flower leis, facing a central desk area.](image)

*   *The **Title** usually begins with a verb ending in "-ing." Unlike bill titles, resolution titles can be amended.*

    *   ***Whereas Clauses** contain background information to support the purpose of the resolution.*

*   ***Be It Resolved Clauses** are the main point of the resolution, and often summarize the position of or action requested by the House, Senate, or entire Legislature.*

*   ***Transmitting Clauses** state the recipient(s) of the resolution. Each recipient is listed as a person, not an organization.*

![Seal of the State of Hawaii]

# The House of Representatives
## State of Hawaii

hereby presents this certificate to

### THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO
### 2009 'Ō'Ō AWARD RECIPIENTS

**WHEREAS**, the Native Hawaiian Chamber of Commerce was founded in 1974 to encourage and promote the interests of native Hawaiians engaged in business and the professions by building on the foundation of relationships, resources and the Hawaiian values of *Aloha*, *'Imi Ola*, *Lōkahi*, *Pono*, *Mālama*, *A'o*, *Alaka'ina*, *Pa'ahana*, *Ho'okipa* and *Ha'aha'a*; and

**WHEREAS**, the 'Ō'Ō awards were established 33 years ago by the Native Hawaiian Chamber of Commerce and have become one of the most prestigious honors presented to outstanding Native Hawaiians who, through their talents and dedication, have made significant contributions to improve the communities and status of Native Hawaiians; and

**WHEREAS**, the Native Hawaiian Chamber of Commerce has selected THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO as 2009 'Ō'Ō Award recipients in recognition of their lasting contributions to the community and their leadership in the Hawaiian music renaissance through their innovative, award-winning music and performances; and

**WHEREAS**, for more than 40 years, THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO have been innovators in Hawaiian music, sparking a new interest in music performed in the Hawaiian language with a contemporary sound that has never been duplicated; and

**WHEREAS**, the music and performances of THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO have received the highest accolades, including recognition by their peers with a lifetime achievement award from the Hawaii Academy of Recording Arts in March 2008, a Grammy nomination for Best Hawaiian Music Album in 2005, and numerous Na Hoku Hanohano awards; and

**WHEREAS**, THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO remain a local favorite; their concerts often sell-out and many families made a tradition of attending their annual Lei Day Concert which ran for 30 consecutive years at the Waikiki Shell and continue to enjoy the popular Cazimero Christmas Concert; and

**WHEREAS**, with unparalleled instrumental and vocal virtuosity, THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO, over their four decades in the entertainment industry, have developed a unique musical style and globally-known brand that are a part of Hawaiian musical history; now, therefore,

The House of Representatives of the Twenty-fifth Legislature of the State of Hawaii hereby commends and congratulates THE BROTHERS CAZIMERO on receiving the 2009 'Ō'Ō Award for their significant contributions to improve the community and raise the status of native Hawaiians and their culture, and extends to them its warmest aloha and best wishes for continued success.

    
Calvin K.Y. Say, Speaker of the House

    
Representative Mele Carroll

<u>Patricia Mau-Shimizu</u>
Patricia Mau-Shimizu, Chief Clerk

    
Representative Pono Chong
