text:
**Website Guide**
**Hawaii State Legislature**
<u>capitol.hawaii.gov</u>

Public Access Room
http://lrb.hawaii.gov/par
par@capitol.hawaii.gov
808-587-0478

![Screenshot of the Hawaii State Legislature website homepage featuring the State Capitol building and the main navigation menu.](image)

**Aloha!**
**Welcome to the Hawai'i State Legislature.**

Welcome to the Hawaii State Legislature website, **https://www.capitol.hawaii.gov**. As you navigate the site, you can return to the homepage at any time by clicking on the Hawai'i state seal or the "Hawai'i State Legislature" title in the upper left-hand corner.

Use the **<u>Log In</u>** & **<u>Register</u>** links at upper right of the website to register and log in to your account to use interactive features (submit testimony, hearing notification, and measure tracking). See page 6 for more info!

![Main navigation bar with tabs: LEGISLATURE, EVENTS, PARTICIPATE, LAWS & RESEARCH, HELP & INFO.](image)

The web design uses a **tab structure** with dropdown menus to allow you to quickly navigate the site. See pages 2-3 for more details on what you'll find under each of the tabs. There are **shortcut buttons** to frequently used content:

![Four shortcut buttons: My Legislature, Hearings, Live & On-Demand Video, and Reports & Lists.](image)

*   **My Legislature** provides you with a shortcut to the interactive features of the website, allows you to edit your account, and offers the "Find Your Legislator" feature.
*   **Hearings** is a shortcut to the list of upcoming scheduled hearings, as well as a search tool for finding past hearings.
*   **Live & On-Demand Video** brings you to links for the House and Senate YouTube channels, where you can watch current and past events.
*   **Reports & Lists** is an easy shortcut to one of the "go to" pages of the website — where you can find bills and other measures by year, and various lists and reports. It's also got a great search feature!

![Public Access Room Logo](image)

YouTube <u>Hawaii Public Access Room</u>
Facebook <u>PublicAccessRoom</u>
Instagram <u>PublicAccessRoom</u>

title: PAR informs the Public through
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/LRB 2026 House New Session Staff Training Presentation.pdf
text:
# PAR informs the Public through
* Handouts
* Workshops
* Social Media
    - Instagram
    - Facebook
    - YouTube
* Answering phone calls & emails

----

### QUICK VIEW OF HOW A BILL BECOMES LAW IN HAWAII
[Solid Lines = HOUSE Bills] [Dotted Lines = SENATE Bills]

```mermaid
graph TD
    IDEA((IDEA)) --> DRAFT[Drafted into a Bill]
    DRAFT --> INTRO_H[INTRODUCED IN HOUSE]
    DRAFT --> INTRO_S[INTRODUCED IN SENATE]
    
    INTRO_H --> R1_H[1ST READING in House]
    INTRO_S --> R1_S[1ST READING in Senate]
    
    R1_H --> REF_H[REFERRED TO COMMITTEE S]
    R1_S --> REF_S[REFERRED TO COMMITTEE S]
    
    REF_H --> HEAR_H[COMMITTEE HEARING S]
    REF_S --> HEAR_S[COMMITTEE HEARING S]
    
    HEAR_H --> R2_H[2ND READING in House]
    HEAR_S --> R2_S[2ND READING in Senate]
    
    R2_H --> CHEAR_H[COMMITTEE HEARING]
    R2_S --> CHEAR_S[COMMITTEE HEARING]
    
    CHEAR_H --> R3_H[3RD READING in House]
    CHEAR_S --> R3_S[3RD READING in Senate]
    
    R3_H --> INTRO_S
    R3_S --> INTRO_H
    
    R3_H -.-> DIS_H{IF DISAGREE}
    R3_S -.-> DIS_S{IF DISAGREE}
    
    DIS_H --> CONF[CONFERENCE COMMITTEE]
    DIS_S --> CONF[CONFERENCE COMMITTEE]
    
    R3_H --> AGR_H{IF AGREE}
    R3_S --> AGR_S{IF AGREE}
    
    CONF --> FINAL[FINAL READING IN HOUSE AND/OR SENATE]
    AGR_H --> FINAL
    AGR_S --> FINAL
    
    FINAL --> GOV[GOVERNOR]
    
    GOV --> VETO[VETO]
    VETO -.-> OVER[c. A veto may be overridden a 2/3 vote in each chamber.]
    GOV --> LAW[BILL BECOMES A LAW]
    
    subgraph Governor Actions
    LAW_A[a. Signed into law by Governor.]
    LAW_B[b. Not signed by Governor, but after a specified number of days it becomes law]
    end
```

Public Access Room: http://lrb.hawaii.gov/par [10/08/2020]

----

![Profile page for publicaccessroom on Instagram showing 97 posts, 523 followers, and 90 following. The bio includes a link to workshops and a linktree.](image)

![Collage of social media posts including recruitment announcements for the Hawaii State Legislature, information about Grant-in-Aid applications, and a "We Are Hiring" notice for session staff.](image)

----

### How a Bill Becomes a Law in Hawaii (No Kitten Around!)

**Someone has an idea for a state law.** They talk about their idea to a legislator. The legislator writes a bill asking for that law. Senators and representatives are our state legislators.
![Illustration of cats talking to a person representing a legislator](image)

**All the members in that legislator's chamber** (the House of Representatives or Senate) gather together at the Capitol and vote on the bill for the first time. This first large group vote is called a **"First Reading."**
<mark>The bill is assigned to one or more committees.</mark>

**If the first committee likes the idea, they have a public hearing** to listen to what people think about the bill. People send in their thoughts electronically and some come speak to the committee in person in support of or against the bill.
![Illustration of cats at a podium speaking to a committee of cats](image)

**If this next committee likes the idea, they have a public hearing and hear from people.** Then, if the committee still thinks the bill is a good idea, they vote to pass the bill.
![Illustration of cats sitting in a row at a committee hearing](image)

**The bill goes for a second large group vote called a "Second Reading."** Then, if the bill is assigned to more committees, it goes to the second committee.

**If the committee still thinks the bill is a good idea, they vote to pass the bill.** Sometimes the committee changes the bill first. These changes are called **amendments**.

**If legislators in the House of Representatives liked the bill one way...** [text cuts off]

----

![Announcement for Act 310 (2025) Nonprofit Emergency Grants and a "Carryover Bill" infographic for 2025-2026.](image)

# THE 2026 SESSION CALENDAR Note: On the 60 session days (days when members convene in their respective chambers), the session day count appears in a box in the lower right, like this: [ # ]. ■ = bills ■ = resolutions ■ = budget See reverse side for more deadline information. ```description A calendar layout for January, February, and March 2026, with specific legislative deadlines and session day counts. To the right, a large text box says "We help explain what all those deadlines mean" followed by a detailed guide titled "The 2026 Session Calendar: A Guide to the Deadlines". ```

January 21

25 26 State-of-the-State Address & Admin Bill Package Cutoff [4] 27

## The 2026 Session Calendar: A Guide to the Deadlines **JAN 21 OPENING DAY** – Hawaii's constitution mandates that the regular legislative session starts at 10:00 a.m. on the third Wednesday of January. **JAN 23 LAST DAY TO INTRODUCE ALL NON-ADMINISTRATION BILL PACKAGES** – Bills bundled together by common interest groups and labeled as a package by the clerks. You can view the various packages of legislation by clicking on the "Reports and Lists" button on the capitol.hawaii.gov website. **& LAST DAY FOR ORGANIZATIONS TO SUBMIT GRANT AND SUBSIDY REQUESTS** – Deadline for "Grant-in-Aid" (GIA) applications. Grants may be appropriated to nonprofit and other organizations for various public purposes that are recognized as priorities and are seen as complementing state government functions. Applications, information, and more specifics regarding the deadline appear under "Legislative Information" at capitol.hawaii.gov. **JAN 26 STATE-OF-THE-STATE ADDRESS** – The Governor's annual address to the assembled joint legislature. The address presents an opportunity for the Governor to report on affairs of state, and to put forth recommendations and initiatives. Many visitors come to the Capitol to hear the Governor's speech and witness the proceedings from the gallery (accessible on the ground floor/atrium level). **& LAST DAY TO INTRODUCE ADMINISTRATION BILL PACKAGE** – This is what is known as the "Governor's Package." The bills are submitted by executive branch agencies for consideration by the legislature and are introduced on behalf of the executive branch by the President of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of Representatives. View the package of legislation by clicking on the "Reports and Lists" button at capitol.hawaii.gov. **JAN 28 LAST DAY TO INTRODUCE BILLS** – A bill is "introduced" when it has been filed with the House or Senate Clerk, who gives it a number (with an HB or SB prefix) and then puts it on the calendar for First Reading by the chamber. After First Reading, it is given its committee referrals, which specify which committees must hear and pass the measure for it to succeed. Only legislators may introduce bills. Note: At introduction, each bill is given a "Bill Status" webpage that can be accessed via the Legislature's website ([www.capitol.hawaii.gov) and used to track all of its activity.] **FEB 11 (HOUSE BILLS) & FEB 12 (SENATE BILLS) TRIPLE REFERRAL FILING** – All bills referred to three or more committees must be filed so that they can be in their second-to-last committee by the following day. (Note: A referral to a joint committee counts as one committee referral.) This deadline allows ample time for successful bills to make their way to the last committee in their originating chamber by the First Lateral deadline. **FEB 19 FIRST LATERAL FILING (BILLS)** – Filing Deadline for First Lateral Bills. **FEB 20 FIRST LATERAL (BILLS)** – All bills referred to more than one committee (i.e., those with multiple referrals) must move to their last committee in the originating chamber by this day. **FEB 26-MAR 4 MANDATORY 5-DAY RECESS** – Hawaii's Constitution mandates a 5 day recess between the 20th and 40th days of the session. Neither the House of Representatives nor the Senate convene or assemble formally in chamber on recess days. Committee...

![Legislative Reference Bureau logo](image) **Legislative Reference Bureau**
Home Learn Engage Persist Current Legislature Newsletters About Conta

## Find Your Own Senator and Representative
Not sure what districts you're in or who represents you? The Legislature's website makes it easy for you to find your Senator and Representative.

![Video thumbnail: Find Your Legislators - Using the Hawaii State Legislature's website (capitol.hawaii.gov) - Public Access Room](image)

## Register for an Account on the Website
Make an account to submit testimony, sign up for hearing notifications or create a measure tracking list. You'll only have to do it once!

![Video thumbnail: How to Register for an Account - Register for an Account on the Hawaii State Legislature's website (capitol.hawaii.gov) - Public Access Room](image)

## Sign Up for Hearing Notices
Make sure you don't miss a chance to offer testimony on bills that interest you. Sign up for hearing notices by bill or by committee.

![Video thumbnail: Receive Hearing Notices - How to... Receive Hearing Notices by Email - Using the Hawaii State Legislature's website (capitol.hawaii.gov) - Public Access Room](image)

## Submitting Testimony
Testimony provides an opportunity for citizens to influence policymakers. Read tips on testimony, how to submit testimony online or view sample testimony.

### Engage
Engagement 101
*   Tips on Testimony
*   Find and Track Bills at the Legislature

Using the Legislature's Website
Advocacy Tips
At the Capitol

# Bill Intro – Planning

## 2026 Official Caucuses

1. House Majority Caucus
2. House Minority Caucus
3. Aviation and Space Caucus
4. Environmental Legislative Caucus
5. Equality Caucus
6. Filipino Caucus
7. Future Caucus

10. Kupuna Caucus
11. Maritime Caucus
12. Native Hawaiian Caucus
13. Small Business Caucus
14. Women's Legislative Caucus
15. Working Families Legislative Caucus

5

# Bill Intro – Planning

### How many can I file with CCO?

**Unlimited up to your limit**
*   **Pre-filing:** Wed, Jan 14<sup>th</sup> to Tues, Jan 20<sup>th</sup>
*   **Filing:** Opening day, Wed, Jan 21<sup>st</sup> to Fri, Jan 23<sup>rd</sup>

**5-bill-a-day filing limit:**
Mon, Jan 26<sup>th</sup> to Wed, Jan 28<sup>th</sup>

**Last day to file bills:**
<u>**Wed, Jan 28<sup>th</sup>, <mark>6 p.m.</mark>**</u>

<page_footer>
6
</page_footer>

# Why file before Fri, Jan 23?

**Fri, Jan 23 – Filing deadline**
*   Non-Admin packages
*   Legislative Caucus Bills
*   Last business day unlimited bills filed

**Mon, Jan 26 – Filing deadline**
*   Admin package (Governor’s bills)

**Thurs, Jan 28 – Filing deadline**
*   Bill Intro cut off – last day to file

*   Get your measures referred early, before the backlog – early referral potentially means early hearings

<page_footer>
7
</page_footer>

# Bill Intro – Planning

### Who is drafting the bill?
*   Process through a drafting agency
    - HMSO/LRB/HMIR/SMA
*   Documents drafted by a Non-agency
    - Contact drafting agency on requirements, usually E-doc & hard copy
    - Agency reviews/creates RFS, formats, transfers e-docs to CCO when completed
*   Drafting agency will email you a PDF to be signed and filed with HCCO.

<page_footer>
8
</page_footer>

# Signatures – the old way

![A signature page of a bill showing multiple handwritten signatures under the "INTRODUCED BY:" line.](image)

<mark>**Signature page**
Last page of the Bill.
* **Introduced by line – first name listed** is the introducing Rep, First Primary
* **Underlined =** Primary introducer
* **Not underlined =** Co-sponsor
* **BR = Introduced by request – ONLY 1 Signature**</mark>

<mark>**Signature page**
**Only the first primary introducer should wet sign on the bill itself. Adding other members who wish to "sign" onto a bill, whether as a primary introducer or co-sponsor, will utilize Adobe Acrobat Sign.**</mark>

# PRINTING

<u>After printing a single measure:</u>
* [x] Verify all pages printed
* [x] Verify the pages are in sequential order
* [x] Move the Report title/Description page from first to the last
* [x] Remove any duplicate pages or pages from other measures that may have been mixed in
* [x] <u>**Clip**</u> on the top left. **DO NOT STAPLE**

25

<u>Additional notes about Bills</u>
* [x] Treat the documents with care. The originals are scanned to be preserved in perpetuity.
* [x] Remove Post-it Notes before filing
* [x] <u>**Clip**</u> **DO NOT STAPLE** originals

text:
# Bill Intro - Filing at CCO

* [x] Give the packet to a HCCO intake clerk.
* [x] Intake Clerk will:
    - Verify the documents.
    - Verify that e-docs have been dumped.
        - Email your office a time-stamped copy of your transmittal sheet.
* Please file early in the day.

![A person handing a document to a clerk behind a counter](image)

37

# Any questions so far?

<page_footer>
38
</page_footer>

# Resolution Intro
## How many can I file?

![Large orange question mark](image)

* [x] Filing Limits
    - [x] 5 study or policy per Representative
    - [x] Additional 5 or 10 for Committee Chairs and must be committee subject matter related

<page_footer>
39

# HEARING PREPARATIONS

* Visit conference room, know where members and staff will sit.
* Committee Chair and Vice Chair staff communicate individual responsibilities prior to the first hearing.
    - Name plates/gavel, laptop setup, vote sheets.
* Public Hearing Notices
    - At least 48 Hour Advance Notice
    - Created by Committee Chair
* Public Testimony
    - Green Leaf (receives/processes testimony)
    - Red Leaf (publishes testimony internally)
* Posting Testimony on Capitol Website
    - On-time and possibly late testimony

title: WORKING WITH HMSO
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 PRESENTATION 2nd In-Session Training Workshop.pdf
text:
# WORKING WITH HMSO

* We will staff all 18 House Standing Committees
    - Individual Attorneys or Analysts have been assigned to each Committee.
    - Primary contact for measures in your Committee.
* Pre-hearing Check (PHC) forms
    - One page form that lists all measures on the hearing notice and provides a recommendation on each measure whether technical nonsubstantive amendments are preferred and an HD1 drafted or whether the measure can pass unamended.
    - By default, the form is only shared with the Committee Chair and their designated staff.
    - Not a legal check, simply a review of the bill for consistency, accuracy, grammar, punctuation, etc., to improve the written form and accuracy of the measure.

title: COMMITTEE DECISION MAKING
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 PRESENTATION 2nd In-Session Training Workshop.pdf
text:
# COMMITTEE DECISION MAKING

* Committee Chair proposes a vote of the Committee to typically do one of these:
    * "As Is" or "Unamended"
    * "With Amendments" or "HD 1"
    * Hold

* Vote taken, usually by Vice Chair:
    * Ensure vote is accurately recorded and marked through all copies.
    * File copies of the vote sheets with the appropriate House agency.

* Committee Chair can also decide to defer the measure:
    * No vote is taken
    * Either indefinitely
    * To another day/time/room

### <u>**MEMORANDUM**</u>

**TO:** All House Members

**FROM:** Speaker Scott K. Saiki ![Signature of Scott K. Saiki](image)

**RE:** Policy for Official Legislative Social Media Accounts

title: <u>House of Representatives Interim Social Media Policy</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2024 06 07 SPKR Policy for Official Legislative Social Media Accounts (1).pdf
text:
# <u>House of Representatives Interim Social Media Policy</u>

The following pertains to a House member's use of any social media account considered their official legislative social media account.

"Official legislative social media account" means any account such as Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, or any other similar account which a member uses for communication with the public and identifies himself/herself as a state representative and is not used for campaigning, private business, or other purpose in violation of the State Ethics Code.

For the purpose of brevity, "official account" is synonymous with "official legislative social media account."

When using an official account, a member shall adhere to the following:

1. All content in official social media communication must be related to official legislative business. This includes matters which relate directly or indirectly to the legislative process, functions, and operations, constituent services, state issues of public concern, and issues related to the member's district.

2. Members shall not use their official accounts to disparage other members or individuals, and shall not encourage, support, or invite disparagement or threats by commenters. This includes posts on a member's own official account page as well as posts on other social media pages using the member's official account.

3. Content critical of policy may not be personalized or politicized, and may not be used to disparage other members, other individuals, or political parties. This includes featuring other members, individuals, or political parties in a way that invites disparaging or threatening comments or reactions, even if the content is presented in a neutral manner.

4. The use of original, unmanipulated recordings of official legislative business such as floor proceedings or committee meetings is allowed, as long as their use is not in violation of any other provisions of this policy. Content altered or edited from its original form is prohibited.

5. Hyperlinks may not link to any websites or documents that contain campaign-related information, comments, or that, by association with the member's official accounts, violate the State Ethics Code (HRS Chapter 84).

6. No campaign content, electioneering, or fundraising on official account pages.

7. No content developed using campaign resources.

8. No endorsement or promotion of non-governmental companies, products, or services, including charitable organizations.

title: 2024 06 07 SPKR Policy for Official Legislative Social Media Accounts (1)

title: PRESS RELEASES AND MEDIA ADVISORIES
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026-01-02 House Comms Training Presentation.pdf
text:
# PRESS RELEASES AND MEDIA ADVISORIES

![State of Hawaii Seal](image)

----

### [Press Release Example 1]
**HAWAI'I HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES**
Hale o nā Luna Maka'āinana

**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**
January 23, 2023
House Communications: Cathy S. Lee | 808-392-1097

**REPRESENTATIVE MATAYOSHI INTRODUCES BILL FOR UNIVERSAL FREE SCHOOL LUNCHES**
*Measure promotes relief to Hawai'i's families and increased school attendance for keiki*

Honolulu, Hawai'i – House Bill 540, introduced by Representative Scot Z. Matayoshi (D-49, Kāne'ohe, Maunawili), seeks to provide relief to working class families by allowing keiki enrolled in Hawai'i's public schools to receive free school lunches.

"During the pandemic, many families with children experienced hardship," said Representative Matayoshi. "We are directly supporting these families financially, providing nutritious food to our keiki to ensure they are ready to learn."

House Bill 540 states that regardless of a student's eligibility for participation in the Federal National School Lunch Program (NSL), the Hawai'i Department of Education (DOE) shall offer one school meal to any asking student at no expense to the student each school day. Currently, a family of four must earn less than $41,496 annually to be eligible for NSL free lunches.

According to the proposed measure, no student shall be denied a meal purely due to inability to pay. If the measure passes, Representative Matayoshi hopes for the program to begin fall of the 2023–2024 school year.

"During the pandemic, the DOE provided free school lunches to students, so we know they have the capacity and capability," said Representative Matayoshi. "I have been collaborating with the DOE, U.S. Senator Brian Schatz, and the United States Department of Agriculture to increase federal subsidies for Hawai'i student lunches. It is my hope that the State can make up the difference to ensure school lunches are free to all public-school students."

The U.S. Department of Agriculture's school nutritional waivers made free school meals universal throughout the pandemic, and the program ended June 30, 2022. Some states, like Maine and California, have enacted their own laws establishing universal free school lunches. Representative Matayoshi hopes for Hawai'i to follow suit.

----

### [Press Release Example 2]
**HAWAI'I HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES**
Hale o nā Luna Maka'āinana

**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**
May 3, 2023
House Communications: Cathy S. Lee | (808) 587-7242

**REP. KILA CALLS FOR ESTABLISHMENT OF NATIVE HAWAIIAN INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY WORKING GROUP**

Honolulu, Hawai'i – In light of Asian American and Pacific Islander Heritage Month, the House last Thursday adopted HCR108, urging the formation of a working group to study policies and legislation with respect to Native Hawaiian intellectual property. The resolution represents efforts to protect the intellectual property rights of Kānaka Maoli, as well as their cultural expressions, language, and art form.

Introduced by Representative Darius K. Kila, the measure calls for the creation of a nine-member working group consisting of experts in Native Hawaiian law, indigenous intellectual property, or Native Hawaiian cultural customs and art, or Native Hawaiian cultural practitioners. Support testimony was submitted by the State Department of Business, Economic Development, and Tourism, Office of Hawaiian Affairs, the Hawai'i County Council, the Council for Native Hawaiian Advancement, and advocates in the Native Hawaiian community.

"There is a kuleana for us to address the global cultural appropriation that has occurred in various forms of language, art, hula, and traditions," said Representative Darius K. Kila (D-44 Honokai Hale, Nānākuli, Mā'ili). "For years, we've observed mainland companies opening businesses and using a Hawaiian name to increase sales, despite having no connection to our culture. This is why we are beginning the discussion on how to safeguard and preserve Native Hawaiian culture, and prevent its tokenization."

Rep. Kila noted that disputes between indigenous peoples and third-party users of indigenous knowledge resources over ownership and control have steadily increased in the last ten years. A recent example includes a non-Hawaiian food chain that originated in Chicago, Aloha Poke Co., which issued cease-and-desist letters threatening small poke food shops in Hawai'i from using the words "Aloha" and "Poke", claiming ownership of these cultural expressions.

----

### [Media Advisory Example]
**HAWAI'I HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES**
Hale o nā Luna Maka'āinana

**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**
March 22, 2023
House Communications: Cathy S. Lee | (808) 587-7242

**BOLD IDEAS CROSSOVER OUT OF THE HOUSE FRESHMEN CLASS**
*First-term Lawmakers Advance Legislation to Support Hawai'i Residents*

Honolulu, Hawai'i – The House Majority Freshmen Class will come together to share their experiences since being elected to the House of Representatives. The group will discuss the progress they've made during their first few months of office.

"This year, 18 new members joined the House of Representatives, making it the largest class the legislature has seen in 28 years, said Speaker Scott K. Saiki. "We appreciate their bold ideas, new perspectives, and commitment to public service."

**WHO:** Hawai'i House of Representatives
**WHAT:** The House Majority Freshmen Class will be joined by House leadership to highlight their experiences serving in the House of Representatives.
**WHEN:** Thursday, March 23, 2023
1:00 p.m. or immediately after floor session
**WHERE:** Hawai'i State Capitol – House Chamber Gallery
415 South Beretania Street
Honolulu, HI 96813

### ###

----

### Definitions

**Official statement, announcement, news story, or update delivered to the media. Includes quote(s).**
*Examples include: introduction of legislation, status of measures, update on community initiatives, or topics that are newsworthy.*

**Invites the media to upcoming events including press conferences, meetings, or special occasions.**
*Includes details on WHO/WHAT/WHEN/WHERE. The purpose is to get media coverage at an event.*

text:
# GRAPHICS/FLYERS

![Representative Darius K. Kila](image)

Mahalo to the City Administration for taking a bold step by announcing a proposed location for O'ahu's next landfill. This decision marks a critical milestone toward the long-awaited closure of the Waimānalo Gulch Sanitary Landfill.

I understand that this announcement may not be welcomed by everyone. However, let me be clear: the Leeward Coast has endured the burden of hosting this landfill for decades—a landfill named after another community but placed in our backyard. For far too long, we have carried more than our fair share of this responsibility.

No matter where the new location is designated, the Administration has an obligation to follow through and shut down the Waimānalo Gulch Sanitary Landfill. Today's announcement reflects an overdue acknowledgment of that responsibility.

I want to thank and acknowledge Mayor Rick Blangiardi's commitment to keeping his promise that the Westside and Nānākuli will no longer shoulder the burden of the Waimānalo Gulch Sanitary Landfill.

**REPRESENTATIVE DARIUS K. KILA**
D-44, Honokai Hale, Nānākuli, Mā'ili

----

## STATE BUDGET HIGHLIGHTS
**$190 MILLION IN FUNDING FOR CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROJECTS IN KAUA'I**

> We have again been very successful with projects for Kaua'i. Kaua'i Veteran's Memorial Hospital is a critical hospital on the westside, and it is very important that we designate funds to upgrade our emergency room and rehab department to modernize and improve efficiency for our community's healthcare needs.
>
> I am also pleased to be able to work with the County of Kaua'i to obtain matching funds to resurface our westside basketball and tennis/pickleball courts. Additionally, food stability is contingent on supporting our agricultural initiatives, which this budget does as well as funding the maintenance of many parks and forest reserve facilities.
>
> **Majority Floor Leader Dee Morikawa**

![Representative Dee Morikawa](image)
District 17, Ni'ihau, portion of 'Ōma'o, Kōloa, Po'ipū, Lāwa'i, Kalāheo, 'Ele'ele, Hanapēpē, Kaawanui Village, Pākālā Village, Waimea, Kekaha

----

**Representative Mahina Poepoe**
**ANNOUNCES THE RELEASE OF CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROJECT FUNDING:**

----

![Wildfire Relief Flyer](image)

**WILDFIRE RELIEF: HOUSE INTRODUCES BIPARTISAN MAUI WILDFIRE BILLS**
*Measures stem from proposals in the House Interim Working Groups Final Report*

----

![House Majority Package Flyer](image)

**HOUSE MAJORITY INTRODUCES BILL PACKAGE FOR 2024 LEGISLATIVE SESSION**

----

![HB 2404 CD1 Tax Cut Flyer](image)

**HB 2404 CD1**
**Largest Tax Cut for Working Families in Hawai'i's History**
*In the 2024 Legislative Session, monumental tax reform legislation passed, leading to a substantial increase in take-home pay for working families by several thousand dollars annually by 2031.*

**How does it work?**
The legislation amends two key components of Hawai'i's tax code over a seven-year period. As a result, the income taxes paid by working-class families will fall by 71% by 2031.

**Standard Deduction:**
For income tax filings for taxes paid in 2024, the standard deduction, the amount of income that is exempt from taxation, is doubled. There will be further increases for taxes paid in 2026, 2028, 2030, and 2031. Increases in the standard deduction benefit low and medium-income households because they are less likely to itemize their deductions.

**Tax Brackets:**
In odd years through 2029, the tax brackets are modified by eliminating the lowest brackets, thereby reducing tax rates in the lower brackets.

[x] The legislation ensures that taxes are reduced as minimum wage rises in 2024, 2026, and 2028.

----

**HB1835** | Recommendation of the Food, Water and Other Supplies Working Group
**Relating to Distribution Management**
Establishes the distribution management advisory board to develop and advise on the statewide distribution management plan.

**HB1836** | Recommendation of the Food, Water and Other Supplies Working Group
**Relating to Health**
Allows pharmacists, during declared states of emergency, to refill prescriptions of up to 30-day supplies of dangerous drugs and dangerous devices if the prescriber is unavailable or cannot be contacted to authorize the refill and if, in the pharmacist's professional judgment, failure to refill the prescription might interrupt the patient's ongoing care and have a significant adverse effect on the patient's well-being.

**HB1837** | Recommendation of the Schools Working Group
**Relating to School Safety**
Requires the DOE to grant public access to emergency action plans that do not pose a security risk to students, staff, or guests of school campuses. Requires each Dept. school to have a comprehensive school evacuation communication plan for use during emergencies. Requires the DOE to collaborate with the DOT to assess all Dept. school campuses to determine if there are sufficient emergency evacuation routes for each campus and report to the Legislature.

**HB1838** | Recommendation of the Shelter Working Group
**Relating to Zoning**
Allows counties to enact a zoning ordinance to amortize or phase out nonconforming single-family transient vacation rental units over a reasonable period of time.

**HB1833**
**Relating to the Rental Housing Revolving Fund**
Appropriates funds into and out of the rental housing revolving fund to provide loans or grants for mixed-income rental projects or units in mixed-income rental projects for qualifying individuals and families. Appropriates funds for an unspecified number of finance specialist positions to assist with these loans and grants.

**HB1834**
**Relating to the Department of Human Services**
Appropriates funds to increase the Department of Human Services' homeless programs office budget.

**HB1831**
**Relating to Crisis Prevention**
Establishes the behavioral health crisis center pilot program. Appropriates funds for the pilot program.

**HB1830**
**Relating to Mental Health**
Establishes provisional or associate-level licensure requirements for marriage and family therapists, mental health counselors, and psychologists and authorizes insurance reimbursements in certain circumstances. Requires psychologist license applicants to possess a doctoral degree and complete certain supervised experience requirements before sitting for the licensing examination. Authorizes insurance reimbursements for services provided by a supervised social work intern in certain circumstances. Appropriates funds. Parts II, III, IV, and V effective 7/1/2026.

**HB1832**
**Relating to Hiring**
Authorizes a state department, division, or agency, rather than the Department of Human Resources Development (DHRD), to conduct a minimum qualification review of applicants for vacant positions within the department, division, or agency. Requires the Department of Human Resources Development to provide state departments, divisions, and agencies the applications received for vacancies under certain circumstances.

WHEREAS, the House of Representatives is proud to demonstrate academic excellence and a commitment to their com

WHEREAS, **ASHLIE FERIDO** has maintained a high achievement at Leilehua High School and has persisted in pursuing her goals despite the unprecedented challenges of virtual learning during the COVID-19 pandemic; and

WHEREAS, **ASHLIE FERIDO** has gone above and beyond by taking on leadership roles in school and extracurricular activities and was recognized as the youngest Major in the Honolulu Law Enforcement Explorers Program; and

WHEREAS, **ASHLIE FERIDO** has a shown a willingness to serve her school and fellow Class of 2021 graduates by helping to organize school activities, fundraisers, and food drives; and

WHEREAS, **ASHLIE FERIDO** has been recognized by her teachers for her resilience, compassion, and kindness towards her fellow students; and

WHEREAS, **ASHLIE FERIDO** has been nominated by her teacher and recognized by her school as the recipient of the 2021 Mighty Mule Scholarship, the highest honor given to a Leilehua High School senior; now, therefore,

The House of Representatives of the Thirty-first Legislature of the State of Hawaii hereby commends and honors **ASHLIE FERIDO** for receiving the 2021 Mighty Mule Scholarship, and extends to her its warmest aloha and best wishes for continued success as she begins her college career and takes her first steps into a bright and promising future.

text:
# Advice

* Don’t make it complicated!
* Create a folder to save all of your certificates from HMSO that is accessible to all of your staff.
* You are not required to submit your certificates through HMSO. You can draft them yourself.
* HMSO does not print your certificates. Allow yourself enough time to submit your certificate to printshop.
* Certificate requests during session will not be prioritized over HMSO's responsibilities for committee work.

# What is the Request for Service (RFS) Form?

![A hand holding a blue marker, having just finished writing the word "REQUEST" in large blue capital letters and underlining it.](image)

*Bills must be drafted and formatted by a drafting agency before being filed with the Chief Clerk's Office.*

*The following are the drafting agencies of the Hawaii State Legislature. Agencies serving the House are in bold:*

* *Exclusively for House (Partisan): **House Majority Staff Office, House Minority Research Office***
* *Exclusively for Senate (Partisan): Senate Majority Research Office, Senate Minority Research Office*
* ***Legislative Reference Bureau** (Nonpartisan): Serves both Majority and Minority members of the House and Senate; files Administration bills*

    -   **Revision of Previously Drafted Documents**

**Exception: No RFS for Committee Reports**

*   **The Rep's office is responsible for submitting an RFS to a drafting agency.**

    -   **Phone conversations or in-person meetings concerning drafting requests may necessitate following up with a formal request for services (RFS) form**

title: The Life Cycle of a Request for Service
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/3 Requests for Service.pdf
text:
# The Life Cycle of a Request for Service

![Three rows of colorful decorative bunting flags in orange, blue, green, pink, and brown.](image)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["**Representative's instructions**"] --> B["**Submit request to the appropriate drafting agency of your choice**"]
    B --> C["**Agency head assigns the request to an Attorney or Analyst**"]
    C --> D["**Request is internally edited and reviewed by management**"]
    D --> E["**Administrative staff process and finalize management edits and then dispatch request to Rep's office**"]
    E --> F["**Rep's Office reviews the document**"]
    
    %% Feedback loops
    C -.->|*(clarification)*| A
    F -.->|*(revision needed)*| B
```

### Begin with the Representative's Instructions

***Example:*** **Rep and a constituent discuss the minimum wage and how it is too low.**

1.  **Constituent is unsure how to address the problem and does not have a policy direction.**
    *   Your office's constituent relations staff will need to get additional information from the constituent. (Tip: Focus on the end result)

2.  **Constituent has language to use to change the law.**
    *   Attach the draft language to the RFS and any additional information for content and context, e.g., research meeting notes (check with your OM)

title: Creating a Request for a Bill
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/3 Requests for Service.pdf
text:
# Creating a Request for a Bill

![Three rows of colorful triangular bunting flags hanging on strings. The first row has orange and blue flags, the second row has green and blue flags, and the third row has pink and brown flags.](image)

Bills are used to:

* Create a new law
* Amend existing law
* Establish a task force or working group
* Commission a study
* Appropriate funds

***

> *When talking with your boss on a bill, the discussion can go far and wide, but the bill must be focused on one subject and your request should focus on that subject. If you find that you are covering many different ideas, those ideas should be submitted as separate requests.*

***

title: 3 Requests for Service
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/3 Requests for Service.pdf
text:
![An illustration of a person sitting on a stool, holding a piece of paper and pointing upwards.](image)

### **Helpful Framework for Drafting a Request**

1. **Problem**
2. **Behavior**
3. **Desired Result**
4. **Exceptions**
5. **Funds**
6. **Effective Date**

1. **Please draft a [bill/resolution] to address [state the problem]**
2. **... [explain behavior that is causing the problem and the desired change in behavior]**
3. **... to [state the desired result]**
4. **... but [list the following exceptions (if any)]**
5. **... and appropriate funds (if any).**
6. **This measure will be effective on [effective date].**

#### Bill Request Example: Raising the Minimum Wage

1. Please draft a bill to address the difficulty of affording to live in Hawaii.

**A good start, but only identifies a policy and needs more detail.**

1. Please draft a bill to address the difficulty of affording to live in Hawaii
2. By raising the minimum wage
3. To $25 by 2030.

**Request is looking better, but needs more specifics (incremental increase? effective date?)**

title: Creating a Request for a Resolution
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/3 Requests for Service.pdf
text:
1. Please draft a bill to address the difficulty of affording to live in Hawaii
2. By requiring employers to raise their minimum wage
3. To help workers in Hawaii afford basic necessities
4. By incrementally increasing the minimum wage to $20 by 2028 and $25 by 2030
5. Effective July 1, 2026

**This request adds more specifics on how to get where you want to.**

## Creating a Request for a Resolution

![Three rows of colorful triangular bunting banners. The top row has orange and blue flags, the middle row has green and blue flags, and the bottom row has pink and brown flags.](image)

### What is a Resolution?

***

*A resolution expresses the position of either the House or Senate (Single-House Resolution) or the position of both houses of the Legislature (Concurrent Resolution).*

title: H.C.R. NO. <sup>114</sup>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/3 Requests for Service.pdf
text:
<mark>*Resolutions are not laws. They are requests.*</mark>

<mark>*State agencies tend to be more responsive to requests from the Legislature if the requests pass through as a Concurrent Resolution.*</mark>

House Resolution: HR

Senate Resolution: SR

Concurrent Resolution Introduced in the House: HCR

Concurrent Resolution Introduced in the Senate: SCR

*Example*: Using the previous bill framework for raising the minimum wage:

Please draft a resolution requesting the Department of Labor and Industrial Relations:

1. To address the difficulty of affording to live in Hawaii
2. By examining the effects on employers if required to incrementally increase the minimum wage
3. To help workers in Hawaii afford basic necessities

HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES  
TWENTY-EIGHTH LEGISLATURE, 2016  
STATE OF HAWAII

# H.C.R. NO. <sup>114</sup>

# The Hawaii State Legislature
*Ka 'aha'ōlelo Moku'āina 'O Hawai'i*
hereby presents this certificate to

# BRETMAN ROCK
## INFLUENCER, MEDIA PERSONALITY, AND SOCIAL ADVOCATE

WHEREAS, the Hawai'i State Legislature is proud to honor exceptionally talented and dedicated individuals who are able to tell stories that challenge the status quo and preserve and perpetuate Hawaiian culture and values for future generations; and

WHEREAS, **BRETMAN ROCK** is a Filipino-American beauty influencer and social media personality who has broken boundaries across media and has become a breakout star for entertainment and trailblazed new paths for representation in the world of entertainment, and cemented his legacy as a social phenomenon and influential icon for Generation Z; and

WHEREAS, **BRETMAN ROCK**, born in the Philippines as Bretman Rock Sacayanan, immigrated to Hawai'i as a child, graduating from James Campbell High School, and while he still resides as a proud, queer, first-generation Asian-American following by 50 million fans around the world and widely acclaimed as one of the leading voices of Gen Z, he is adored by his fans for his outstanding fashion and beauty sense, laugh-out-loud charisma, confidence, and anomalous personal brand, demonstrating that it is possible to conquer all business verticals and forms of media while remaining unapologetically himself; and

WHEREAS, since his first viral YouTube video a decade ago, **BRETMAN ROCK** has continued to garner accolades and honors such as being named one of Forbes' inaugural Top 50 Creators and 30 under 30 honorees; and by the power of Young Hollywood 2023 honorees; he is the first openly gay male to be featured on the cover of VOGUE Philippines' inaugural Pride issue and has also graced the covers of magazines such as Attitude, NYLON MANILA, Gay Times, TeenVOGUE, tmrw Magazine, and StyleCaster, among many others; and

WHEREAS, **BRETMAN ROCK** remains outspoken about his social advocacy and environmentalism and continues to publicly showcase his passion for causes that support youth, specifically AAPI and LGBTQ communities, in addition to partnering with nonprofits, including the Ocean Conservancy, Make-a-Wish Foundation, and Act to Change; he is also a co-chair with former First Lady Michelle Obama on the board of her voting rights organization, When We All Vote; now, therefore,

BE IT RESOLVED by the Thirty-second Legislature of the State of Hawai'i, Regular Session of 2024, that this body hereby congratulates **BRETMAN ROCK** on his success across all social media, commends him for his contributions to supporting youth, honors his commitment and contributions to the State of Hawai'i, the United States and the world, including his continued efforts to support Native Hawaiians and Filipinos, and extends to him its warmest aloha and best wishes for continued success in all future endeavors.

title: Part I. Overview of State Government and the Legislature
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Part I. Overview of State Government and the Legislature

![Photo by HMSO. "The Spirit of Liliuokalani" statue in the Capitol Mall honors the memory of Hawaii's last reigning monarch.](image)

## Separation of Powers

The State of Hawaii follows the separation of powers doctrine as its governance model. Under this model, state government is divided into three co-equal branches: legislative, executive, and judicial. Each branch has separate and independent powers and areas of responsibility. Generally, the legislative branch makes laws, the executive branch implements and enforces laws, and the judicial branch interprets laws. No one branch has more power than another branch.

## The Legislature and its Responsibilities

The Hawaii State Legislature consists of two houses: the House of Representatives (House) and the Senate. The House has 51 members who are elected every two years and the Senate has 25 members who serve staggered four-year terms. The presiding officers for the respective houses are the Speaker of the House and the Senate President.

As required under Article III, Section 10 of the Hawaii State Constitution, the Legislature convenes annually in regular session at 10:00 a.m. on the third Wednesday of January to conduct its business, which includes the consideration of legislation. Each regular session is limited to a period of 60 legislative days and usually lasts from mid-January to early May.

Each legislature lasts for a biennium, beginning in an odd-numbered year and ending in an even-numbered year. The biennium coincides with the term of office of state representatives. The functions of the House and Senate during each biennium include: (1) enacting new laws and amending or repealing existing laws, (2) appropriating funds for state programs and projects, (3) conducting fact-finding investigations or program reviews, (4) proposing amendments to the Hawaii State Constitution, (5) considering requests and petitions, and (6) offering public congratulations or condolences.

The Legislature also has various internal administrative duties, which include the power to punish its members for misconduct and the exercise of certain impeachment powers that apply to the Governor and Lieutenant Governor. The Senate also exercises confirmation authority over various executive and judicial offices, such as judges and certain state board and commission members.

4

title: Role of Staff
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Role of Staff

Session committee clerks and legislative aides usually provide administrative, research, and outreach support during the legislative session to elected officials and their permanent staff. Accordingly, you may be asked to: (1) coordinate meetings, hearings, and site visits; (2) draft correspondence; (3) assemble and prepare bills and testimonies for hearings; (4) draft congratulatory documents; (5) draft resolutions and their committee reports; (6) digest legislation referred to your committee; (7) review reports and recommend courses of action to your representative; and (8) handle inquiries concerning the status of legislation referred to your committee. Please note that your responsibilities will depend on the needs of your office and may include other tasks not listed here.

<mark>![A red hibiscus flower with the word "Tip" in white text on it.](image) Session can be hectic and often involves learning quickly on the job. Remember that all staff, both permanent and session, have the same goal: to make session run as smoothly as possible. Do not hesitate to ask other more experienced staff, including those in various House agencies, for advice and help throughout the session.</mark>

## Drafting and Research Agencies

The **House Majority Staff Office (HMSO)** provides drafting and research services for the House members of the *majority* party.

The **Senate Majority Research Office (SMA)** performs a similar function for the *majority* members of the Senate.

The **Chief Attorney of the House** and the **Chief Attorney of the Senate** render legal counsel to their respective houses.

The **House Minority Research Office (HMIR)** and **Senate Minority Research Office**, respectively, provide drafting and research services for the House and Senate members of the *minority* party.

The **Legislative Reference Bureau (LRB)** provides drafting and research services for all members of the Legislature. LRB also conducts studies and runs the Public Access Room (PAR) and LRB Library, which are open to the public and all legislative staff.

5

title: 2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
### Other Agencies

The **House Chief Clerk’s Office (CCO)** is responsible for many fiscal, administrative, and recordkeeping functions of the House. CCO is where legislative documents are filed for introduction and after passage by a committee (e.g., bills, resolutions, committee reports). CCO also updates the status of bills and resolutions on the Hawaii State Legislature’s Website (capitol website).

The **House Sergeant-at-Arms (SAA)** is responsible for maintaining much of the inventory and facilities of the House, including room equipment, furnishings, conference rooms, and parking. SAA also coordinates and works with the Sheriffs Division to provide for the security and safety of House legislators and staff, which is especially useful when session duties run late into the night. You will often interact with SAA when filing hearing notices or requesting office supplies.

The **House Printshop** is responsible for the printing needs of House members and facilities with respect to legislative documents, including testimony and other documentation submitted to committees for distribution; newsletters; and other information materials that further a representative’s official duties and responsibilities.

**House Tech Support** provides general computer assistance as well as assistance with Committee hearing livestreams and remote testifiers.

**House Majority Communications** provides assistance with media relations for majority members.

Remember that there is a large support system available to assist you throughout the legislative session.

### Things to Remember

**Confidentiality** is one of the most important things to remember during session. Conversations between staffers (including HMSO staff) and a representative should be considered confidential and are not to be shared with anyone except those specifically authorized by that representative. Documents received from constituents, proponents of bills, and HMSO (or another drafting agency) that have not yet been filed, as well as internal office documents (such as measure tracking printouts), should be kept confidential. Although there is a sense of camaraderie between many of the representatives, it is good to remember that session occurs in an environment where information or the lack thereof can affect political bargaining and strategy.

6

title: *Things to Remember (continued)*
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# *Things to Remember (continued)* **Legislative Terminology.** The Legislature, like many workplaces, uses its own set of terms and shorthand terminology. You may hear words and phrases like "Sine Die," "Conference," "Decking," "Lateral," and "Defective Date" on a daily basis, often without context or introduction. A short glossary of such terms can be found on pages 49-52 as well as on the capitol website. If you are unfamiliar with any terms, ask. **Teamwork and effective communication** are perhaps the two most important tools you need during the course of session. Properly utilizing and sharing the variety of resources you have at your disposal allows for greater office flexibility, efficient dissemination of important information through appropriate channels, and effective time management and organization. **Organization** is also extremely important. Session workload often comes in waves that coincide with various session deadlines. An office with proper planning can effectively organize its workload to take advantage of downtime and "clear the decks" to prepare for the next wave of business or for any other adverse or unexpected situations that may arise. ![The Capitol's circular, 36-foot "Aquarius" mosaic by Tadashi Sato is made up of 600,000 tiles and represents submerged rocks as reflected through ocean water.](image)

*Photo by HMSO*

*The Capitol's circular, 36-foot "Aquarius" mosaic by Tadashi Sato is made up of 600,000 tiles and represents submerged rocks as reflected through ocean water.* 7

title: Part II. Sources of Law
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![The United States Constitution with the preamble "We the People" visible, resting against an American flag.](image)

*Photo by https://krausekorner.files.wordpress.com/2012/05/constitutionwithflag.jpg*

*Signed on September 17, 1787, the United States Constitution is at the top of the United States hierarchy of laws as the supreme law of the land.* # Part II. Sources of Law You will often encounter discussions of legislative topics that cover a wide variety of legal subjects and are affected by federal law, Hawaii state law, Hawaii Administrative Rules, and county ordinances. It is helpful to familiarize yourself with various sources of law and understand how they relate to each other. In the high volume, fast-paced session environment, it may be less important to know the more technical details of a legal concept such as "preemption" (by which one source of law may overrule another source of law) than it is to know that such a concept exists. For example, if a representative would like to pass a law that completely overrules the federal Affordable Care Act, it is helpful to know that federal law would preempt state law. Knowing the different sources of law will also help you in handling constituent concerns, which often require a preliminary determination on whether the constituent's problem lies within federal, state, or county jurisdiction. The following is a brief introduction to various sources of law and their place within the United States "hierarchy of laws." 8

title: The United States Constitution
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# The United States Constitution

You can find a copy of the United States Constitution in the first volume of the Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS). Under Article VI, Clause 2, the United States Constitution is the "supreme Law of the Land" and is at the top of the United States "hierarchy of laws." No law, whether state or federal, is valid unless it is made in accordance with the United States Constitution.

## Federal Law - The United States Code (USC)

Federal statutes are written and passed by the United States Congress and are generally held to be the second highest, after the United States Constitution, in the "hierarchy of laws." You can find a copy of the USC either online or in the LRB Library.

## Federal Regulations - The Code of Federal Regulations (CFR)

The CFR is a compilation of the various permanent administrative rules and regulations of the executive departments and agencies of the federal government of the United States. The CFR provides guidance on how the executive branch implements the laws of Congress. You can find a copy of the CFR online.

## Hawaii State Constitution

The Hawaii State Constitution is often the source of authority for the Hawaii legislative process, elections process, House Rules (as adopted by the House), and creation of many administrative divisions and departments within the executive branch. The Hawaii State Constitution may be revised or amended by constitutional convention or legislative action following a vote by the electorate to ratify the amendment. Provisions of the Hawaii State Constitution may be preempted by federal law pursuant to the supremacy clause. You can find the Hawaii State Constitution in the first volume of the HRS and on LRB's website at: <u>https://lrb.hawaii.gov/constitution</u>.

## <u>Hierarchy of Laws</u>

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[United States Constitution] --> B[Acts of Congress & Treaties]
    B --> C[State Constitution]
    C --> D[State Laws & Statutes]
    D --> E[County Ordinances]

    style A fill:#4A7EBB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#385D8A
    style B fill:#4A7EBB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#385D8A
    style C fill:#4A7EBB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#385D8A
    style D fill:#4A7EBB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#385D8A
    style E fill:#4A7EBB,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#385D8A
```

> ![Hibiscus flower icon representing Tips](image) **Tips**
> * Feel free to contact HMSO or another drafting agency for more information on the various sources of law.
> * The United States Constitution, Hawaii State Constitution, and HRS can be found on the capitol website at: <u>https://www.capitol.hawaii.gov/hrsall/</u>. Also included are important acts passed by Congress relating to Hawaii, including the Organic Act, Admission Act, and Hawaiian Homes Commission Act, 1920.

9

title: Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS) and Session Laws of Hawaii (SLH)
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![A wooden gavel resting against old law books titled "Hawaii Revised Laws of Hawaii 1955".](image)

Photo by HMSO

*The Hawaii State Constitution can be found in the first volume of the HRS and on LRB's website.* # Hawaii Revised Statutes (HRS) and Session Laws of Hawaii (SLH) Like the USC, the HRS and SLH comprise measures passed by a legislative body (in this case the Hawaii State Legislature) and enacted into law. All laws passed and enacted into law in a given year are included in the SLH for that year. However, not all laws printed in the SLH will be codified in the HRS. Usually, the HRS only includes laws of permanent importance. For example, a law permanently raising the fine for jaywalking to $500 will likely be included in the HRS. Whereas, a law raising the fine for jaywalking to $500 for one year will likely be published in the SLH only. Similarly the State Budget or State Supplemental Budget for a given year will only be included in the SLH for that year. The HRS may be preempted by the United States Constitution, federal law, and the Hawaii State Constitution. Your office should have hard copies of the HRS and SLH, but you may also find both collections on the capitol website. ![A row of Hawaii Revised Statutes volumes with cumulative supplements.](image)

Photo by HMSO

*The HRS with Cumulative Supplements*

![A row of yearly volumes of the Session Laws of Hawaii.](image)

Photo by HMSO

*Yearly volumes of the SLH*

10

title: Hawaii Administrative Rules (HAR)
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![Night view of the Waikiki skyline with illuminated buildings and palm trees.](image)
*Photo by http://www.reservehawaii.com/travelguide/things-to-do-in-waikiki-night/*

*A county ordinance relating to the Waikiki Special Improvement District can be found in the Revised Ordinances of Honolulu.*

## Hawaii Administrative Rules (HAR)

The HAR is the final product of rulemaking by the State’s various departments and agencies, including the Office of the Governor and University of Hawaii. The HAR can be preempted by federal laws and regulations as well as state laws. You can find copies of the HAR on each permanent state department’s website and on the Lieutenant Governor’s website at: <u>https://ltgov.hawaii.gov/the-office/administrative-rules/</u>.

## County Ordinances and Administrative Rules

The HRS, HAR, and county ordinances have the same relationship to each other as the USC, CFR, and HRS, respectively. Generally, the HRS, SLH, and HAR will preempt county ordinances. To determine whether preemption exists, an analysis similar to that of the relationship between federal and state laws may be needed. You can find copies of the county ordinances and county department rules on each county’s website.

<mark>
![Red hibiscus flower icon](image) **Tips**
* Resolutions (resos) are not included above because they are not laws and generally do not possess the full force and effect of law.
* Copies of the various county ordinances can be found online at:
    * City & County of Honolulu: <u>https://www8.honolulu.gov/ocs/revised-ordinances-of-honolulu/</u>
    * Hawaii County: <u>https://www.hawaiicounty.gov/our-county/legislative/office-of-the-county-clerk/county-code</u>
    * Kauai County: <u>https://www.kauai.gov/Government/Council/Ordinances</u>
    * Maui County: <u>https://www.mauicounty.gov/165/Ordinances</u>
</mark>

11

title: The Legislative Process
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![A close-up photo of a wall calendar showing Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday, with a yellow sticky note on one of the dates.](image)

*Photo by HMSO*

# The Legislative Process The following briefly describes the necessary steps for House bills to become laws: **Bill Introduction:** House bills are drafted by the drafting agencies and filed with CCO by the office of the representative who is the primary introducer. House bills are drafted pursuant to representative requests. These requests can be for new or amended bills, reintroductions of previous bills, and various "form" bills. **First Reading:** Introduced House bills are assigned numbers and acknowledged during floor sessions of the House. Subsequently, the Speaker reviews these measures and refers them within four session days to the appropriate House standing committee(s). **Second Reading:** Referred House bills are heard and considered by the first committee of referral at the Chair's discretion. House bills passed out of the first committee are then put to a second reading House vote in a floor session. **Third Reading:** After the final committee hears and reports out the House bills, they are then debated and put to a third reading House vote in a floor session. Generally, House bills passing third reading require a majority vote by all House members constituting a quorum. A House bill up for third reading requires a legal check form to be filed with CCO and must meet 48-hour notice requirements. **First Crossover:** All House bills that receive the required votes and pass the House on third reading cross over to the Senate for Senate consideration. **First and Second Reading (Senate Chamber):** House bills that crossed over to the Senate are formally acknowledged by a first reading Senate vote on the floor. Once crossed over, the House bills will be referred to the appropriate Senate standing committee(s) in accordance with Senate Rules. The first committee of referral may then hear and consider the House bills referred to it, and if reported out, the House bills go to a second reading vote by the Senate in a floor session. 13

title: 2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
***The Legislative Process (continued):***

**Third Reading and Second Crossover (Senate Chamber):** House bills heard and reported out of the <u>final</u> committee of referral are then debated and put to a third reading Senate vote in a floor session.

**From this point, there are three possible outcomes for House bills:**

A. Any House bill that passes the Senate <u>without amendment,</u> will be enrolled to the Governor.

B. Any House bill that passes the Senate <u>with amendment,</u> will undergo a "second crossover" back to the House. The House may then review the changes to the House bill and:

i. If the House <u>agrees</u> with the Senate's amendments in a final reading House vote in a floor session, the bill will be enrolled to the Governor; or

ii. If the House <u>disagrees</u> with the Senate's amendments, the bill will be referred to a Conference Committee.

**Conference Committee and Sine Die:** Both the House and the Senate must agree on the final version of a House bill to enroll it to the Governor. If different versions of the same House bill pass both the House and the Senate, the House and the Senate may meet to resolve the differences in Conference Committee. House bills that are amended by the Conference Committee are submitted to a final reading vote in both the House and Senate. Please note that many House bills referred to "Conference" require budgetary approval from the House Finance Committee and the Senate Ways and Means Committee before they can pass the Legislature.

<mark>
![Hibiscus flower icon](image) **Tips**
* You may hear people talk about deadlines that are not listed above. These are usually deadlines that appear on the House Internal Legislative Timetable. These deadlines can include references to various "Referral Deadlines," "Lateral Deadlines," and "Cutoffs." A discussion of some of these deadlines appears on pages 33-35 of this guide.
* Many of your staff responsibilities coincide with the legislative calendar. Each deadline signals that certain actions will occur, such as voting on the floor. Although different committees will be busy at different times throughout the session, it is always best to begin addressing tasks and preparation as early as possible. When feasible, a good practice is to always front-load your committee work.
</mark>

14

title: Part IV. Components of Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Part IV. Components of Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates

Typically, every measure introduced by the Legislature has a masthead that includes the chamber of the Legislature in which the measure originated, the year the measure was introduced, and a number to identify the measure (e.g., House Bill (H.B.) No. 100); line numbers along the side of the measure; page numbers; and a bar code at the bottom to identify the measure and which agency drafted it (e.g., HMSO, LRB, etc.). The measure’s number is added after the measure is introduced. Measure draft numbers are added and changed as the measure is amended by committees in each house.

<description>
A diagram of a sample Hawaii House Bill with numbered callouts pointing to specific sections.
</description>

> HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES  
> THIRTY-THIRD LEGISLATURE, 2025  
> STATE OF HAWAII  
> **H.B. NO.**
>
> ---
> # A BILL FOR AN ACT
>
> <mark>1</mark> `RELATING TO LABOR.`
>
> <mark>2</mark> **BE IT ENACTED BY THE LEGISLATURE OF THE STATE OF HAWAII:**
>
> | | |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | **1** |       SECTION 1. Section 387-2, Hawaii Revised Statutes, is |
> | **2** | amended by amending subsection (a) to read as follows: |
> | **3** |       "(a) Except as provided in section 387-9 and this section, |
> | **4** | every employer shall pay to each employee employed by the |
> | **5** | employer, wages at the rate of not less than: |
> | **6** |       (1) $6.25 per hour beginning January 1, 2003; |
> | **7** |       (2) $6.75 per hour beginning January 1, 2006; |
> | **8** |       (3) $7.25 per hour beginning January 1, 2007; |
> | **9** |       (4) $7.75 per hour beginning January 1, 2015; |
> | **10** |       (5) $8.50 per hour beginning January 1, 2016; |
> | **11** |       (6) $9.25 per hour beginning January 1, 2017; |
> | **12** |       (7) $10.10 per hour beginning January 1, 2018; |
> | **13** |       (8) $12.00 per hour beginning October 1, 2022; |
> | **14** |       (9) $14.00 per hour beginning January 1, 2024; |
> | **15** |      (10) $16.00 per hour beginning January 1, 2026; and |
> | **16** |      (11) [~~$18.00~~] <u>$20.00</u> per hour beginning January 1, 2028." |

<mark>3</mark> (Callout pointing to the body of the bill text)

### Components of a Bill:

The Hawaii State Constitution requires all laws (bills) to have:
1. <mark>1</mark> A **title**, which shall embrace but one subject. A bill’s title *should not* be amended; and
2. <mark>2</mark> A **“Be it enacted by the legislature of the State of Hawaii” clause.**

### Body:
3. <mark>3</mark> The **body** includes the substantive portions of the bill. Whether it be enacting new laws, amending or repealing existing laws, appropriating funds for state programs and projects, or proposing amendments to the Hawaii State Constitution. It may also include a findings and purpose section also known as a preamble.

**Components of a Bill:**

**Body:**

*   <mark>4</mark> Bills that amend the HRS or SLH should include a **Ramseyering provision**, which tells you how the bill amends the language; and
*   <mark>5</mark> All bills should include an **effective date**.

**Signature line:**

*   <mark>6</mark> When a bill is drafted for introduction, it will also include a signature line. Only the first primary introducer is required to physically sign a measure for introduction. Other members may electronically sign the bill by invitation. For more information on the e-sign process, including how to prepare an invitation request, please visit Manuals & Guides on House Gateway: <u>http://house/Training</u>.

```description
[Boxed and labeled with a blue 8]:

Increases the minimum wage to $20 beginning January 1, 2028.

The summary description of legislation appearing on this page is for informational purposes only and is not legislation or evidence of legislative intent.
```

**Components of a Bill:**

The **Summary Description** page is included for informational purposes. The information on this page will be integrated into the Legislature's information systems for display on the capitol website. The Summary Description page includes:

*   <mark>7</mark> A **Report Title** which includes a list of index terms, or keywords, that are most relevant to the bill; and
*   <mark>8</mark> A **Description** or brief summary of the measure. If a bill is scheduled for a hearing, this description will appear on the hearing notice.

HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES
THIRTY-THIRD LEGISLATURE, 2025
STATE OF HAWAII

# H.R. NO.     

# HOUSE RESOLUTION

<mark>1</mark> <mark>URGING THE DEPARTMENT OF LABOR AND INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS TO STUDY THE EFFECTS OF RAISING THE MINIMUM WAGE.</mark>

1 <mark>WHEREAS, Hawaii's minimum hourly wage is currently lower</mark>
2 <mark>than the minimum wage in eleven states; and</mark>
3
4 <mark>WHEREAS, the cost of living in Hawaii is much higher than</mark>
5 <mark>in most states; and</mark>

8 <mark>years old or older; and</mark>
9

11 <mark>wage disparity in the State; now, therefore,</mark>

### Components of a Resolution (Reso):

*   <mark>1</mark> The **title** usually begins with a verb ending in "-ing." Unlike bill titles, resolution titles can be amended.
*   <mark>2</mark> **Whereas clauses** contain background information to support the purpose of the resolution.
*   <mark>3</mark> **Be it resolved clauses** are the main point of the resolution, and often summarize the position of or action requested by the House, Senate, or entire Legislature.
*   <mark>4</mark> **Transmitting clauses** state the recipient(s) of the resolution. Each recipient is listed as a person, not an organization.
*   <mark>5</mark> **Signature line**, when drafted for introduction.
*   <mark>6</mark> **Report Title.** Unlike with bills, a resolution's Report Title is only visible in the word file and not the PDF.

title: Substantive Resolutions
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![The interior of the Hawaii State Capitol House of Representatives chamber, showing the curved wooden desks and the large tapestry on the wall.](image)

Photo by https://www.hawaiirealtors.com/recent_news/how-did-real-estate-do-in-the-2017-hawaii-legislature

*Resolutions are used by the Legislature to express its sentiments, request a particular action, state its policy, and provide direction.* ## Substantive Resolutions Unlike bills, resolutions do not usually have the full force and effect of law. Instead, most resolutions exist to express the will of the Legislature and encourage or discourage behavior. However, some resolutions may have substantive weight. Resolutions are required for certain actions that require legislative approval, such as granting easements, and can also be a method by which a State audit is requested. There are different types of resolutions. **House resolutions (HR)** express the position of the House and only require adoption by the House. **Senate resolutions (SR)** express the position of the Senate and only require adoption by the Senate. **House concurrent resolutions (HCR)** and **Senate concurrent resolutions (SCR)** express the position of the entire Legislature and require adoption by both the House and the Senate. A red hibiscus flower icon with the word "Tip" in yellow text is positioned at the top left of a light blue shaded box. **You can draft resolutions.** In contrast to bills, resolutions can be drafted by staff and do *not* need to go through a drafting agency before filing. This can save you time if or when drafting agencies are inundated with bill drafting requests and immediate service is needed on a resolution. This goes for both **substantive** and **nonsubstantive** resolutions. 18

title: Nonsubstantive Resolutions and Congratulatory Certificates
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
19

# Nonsubstantive Resolutions and Congratulatory Certificates

Congratulatory certificates are nonsubstantive documents that usually resemble resolutions in format and are used to officially recognize and congratulate an individual or organization on a special occasion or for an accomplishment. They may also be used to offer condolences.

The "whereas" and "be it resolved" clauses for congratulatory certificates usually point out the accomplishments of an individual or group and highlight the reason for issuing the certificate.

Certificates are typically drafted by a representative's staff. If a floor presentation is involved, typically a large certificate, such as the example from 2009 to the right, and a nonsubstantive resolution are required (to be placed on the Order of the Day). Please coordinate this process with CCO to ensure that the floor presentation does not interfere with other important legislative business.

![Example of a congratulatory certificate from the House of Representatives, State of Hawaii, presented to The Bernice Pauahi Bishop Museum for its 125th Anniversary. It includes several "WHEREAS" clauses detailing the museum's history and contributions, followed by a "BE IT RESOLVED" clause. It is signed by Calvin K.Y. Say, Speaker of the House, and Patricia Mau-Shimizu, Chief Clerk.](image)

<mark>**Tip** For more information on how to draft congratulatory documents, including congratulatory certificates and nonsubstantive resolutions, refer to the "Congratulatory Documents Manual" available in the HMSO Manuals and Forms folder on your office manager's desktop. This folder also includes examples of certificates that you may use to draft your congratulatory documents as well as a Certificate Questionnaire that you can ask the requestor to complete.</mark>

Part V. Working With Your Drafting Agency

The various drafting agencies may be able to assist you in fulfilling representative requests, including drafting bills and resolutions, finalizing congratulatory certificates, and providing legal assistance. Providing detailed information to the drafting agencies and following their procedures will help quicken the turn-around time for assignments and improve the quality of final products. The sample below is a "General Request for Service" form for HMSO (LRB and HMIR have different request for service forms). This form as well as the HMSO "Certificate Request for Service" form, House Chief Attorney "Legal Assistance Form", and LRB's transmittal "Request for Service" form may be found on House Gateway at: <u>http://house/House/Forms</u>. The HMSO forms and House Chief Attorney form may also be found in the HMSO Manuals and Forms folder on your office manager's desktop.

![Tips icon](image)
**Tips**
* Time is often the most valuable resource during the session. Always try to give your requests to a drafting agency as early as possible to help ensure that appropriate time is allotted for the requests to be completed.
* Do not hesitate to attach extra sheets (in addition to the pink sheet) containing more information on a measure.
* Requests may be submitted as "rush" or with a one-day deadline. However, it is important to remember that around deadlines, HMSO may receive many "rush" or "one-day" requests simultaneously, making the fulfillment of every request on a short turnaround difficult or impossible.
