title: General Request for Service
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# General Request for Service

The "General Request for Service" form on the preceding page is a standard example of a bill request to HMSO. Requests can vary in subject matter, from a request for a bill draft for introduction, to a research assignment or memorandum. To help ensure that a quality work product fulfills the request in a timely manner, it is imperative that each request form is completed accurately and concisely, and contains relevant background information. Email requests to hmso@capitol.hawaii.gov or hand-deliver requests to Room 410.

1.  **Contact Information, Due Date, and Subject:** Staff workload is often split among office personnel. Be sure to indicate the correct contact person for the request to ensure that drafting agency questions are directed appropriately. Because many of the laws drafted may have significant effects on the workings of the State, bills that pass through any drafting agency must undergo rigorous review. Although the exact process may differ among different drafting agencies, most bills will undergo, at a minimum: (1) legal analysis before drafting, (2) review of the drafter's work, (3) review from the legal counsel of the drafting agency, and (4) formatting review. When requesting general services from HMSO, please consider that the time needed for this process is intended to provide a better work product for your representative. In the example on the previous page, the due date is any time "prior to applicable deadline" (e.g., bill intro). HMSO will fulfill requests due "prior to applicable deadline" as quickly as possible subject to the "first in, first out" policy adopted by many drafting agencies. "Rush" due dates are sometimes necessary, but keep in mind that while the drafting agency will do its best within time constraints, unrealistic due dates may be reflected in the overall polish of the final work product. On the "Subject" line, indicate the general subject of the request. This will assist the drafting agency as it assigns requests to drafters.

2.  **Type of Request:** Indicate the type of request, such as whether your representative would like a House Bill, both a House bill and a Senate bill companion, or a concurrent or single house resolution. You may also request a memorandum or research. If the type of work product requested is not listed, please specify this under "Other." If your office would like to reintroduce a bill previously introduced, check the reintroduction box and specify the year, bill number, and draft number, if any, of the bill your office would like to use. If your office would like to revise a document that was transmitted to your office by HMSO, please enter its footer in the "Revision" box. If the revisions are extensive, please attach a hard copy mark-up.

3.  **Explanation of Request:** Specify the content of the request. Be as precise as possible with what your representative actually wants. It is important for you to provide the drafting agency with as much information as possible, including pertinent information such as a relevant past bill or affected HRS section. In the example on the previous page, the exact content of the new measure is listed and the specific HRS section is cited to give further guidance on the type of language the representative wants. This is a better request than one requesting HMSO to "please draft a bill raising the minimum wage." You may also list extra details on why the request for service is being asked of HMSO. Providing context for bills often leads to a better final work product. By providing context, it may be easier for HMSO staff to flag issues that are only apparent through practical application of your representative's request. If your representative would like a preamble or purpose section included in the bill, please provide draft language.

    **Relevant Materials:** Sometimes during the bill drafting process, a constituent may provide your representative with sample language for a bill (or your representative may have drafted sample language). Email electronic copies of any relevant materials (preferably as Word documents), along with the "General Request for Services" form, to hmso@capitol.hawaii.gov.

21

# *Certificate Request for Service (continued)*

The "Certificate Request for Service" form on the preceding page is a standard example of a congratulatory document request to HMSO. Email requests to hmso@capitol.hawaii.gov or hand-deliver requests to Room 410.

1.  **Contact Information, Due Date, and Subject:** Similar to the General Request for Service form, staff are responsible for entering the appropriate contact information, due date, and subject. For congratulatory document requests, your office may request a specific date. Compared to bills, congratulatory documents undergo a somewhat truncated review process that varies depending on the type of request. When requesting congratulatory document services from HMSO, please consider that our office will often receive multiple congratulatory document requests simultaneously, which makes "rush" due dates more difficult to fulfill.

2.  **Type of Request:** Indicate the type of request, such as whether your representative would like our office to embellish and revise (includes necessary changes to enhance the draft you provide, including rewrites) or format and proof only (focuses on grammar, spelling, and format changes only). You can also indicate whether your office would like a joint certificate with the Senate or a House Resolution (nonsubstantive resolution) for a floor presentation. If your office would like to revise a certificate that was transmitted to your office by HMSO, please attach a hard copy mark-up of the certificate and enter its footer in the "Revision" box.

3.  **Explanation of Request:** List the content of the request. A draft of the congratulatory document is required. Generally, HMSO will review grammar, diction, syntax, spelling, and style, but our office relies on the facts and information provided by your office. You should specify the pronouns for each individual, provide the official spelling of names, and spell out acronyms.

    **Documents Transmitted:** Staff must email a copy of the draft language to HMSO at hmso@capitol.hawaii.gov. The draft language must be in a Word document with a filename that includes the requesting representative's name and brief identifying information.

<mark>![Red hibiscus flower icon](image) **Tips**
* Any office can print small certificates. To do so, use the special bond paper from the House Printshop.
* House Printshop can print small and large certificates and is equipped to handle big volume jobs. The House Printshop Certificate Request Form may found on House Gateway at: <u>http://house/House/Forms</u>. For more information, please contact the House Printshop at ext. 6-6591.</mark>

title: Legal Assistance (continued)
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Legal Assistance (continued)

The "Legal Assistance" form on the preceding page is a standard example of a legal check request to the House Chief Attorney. Throughout the year, your office may request general legal assistance from the House Chief Attorney including legal opinions, legal research, bill statute interpretation, litigation assistance, or other assistance. During session, staff, particularly chair's staff, may need to request various types of legal checks from the House Chief Attorney. For example, every House bill up for third or final reading requires a legal check form to be filed with CCO. During the conference period, representatives may also request pre-legal checks. Requests may be emailed to r.nakamura@capitol.hawaii.gov.

1.  **Contact Information, Due Date, and Subject:** Similar to the previous forms, staff are responsible for entering the appropriate contact information, due date, and subject.
2.  **Type of Request:** Specify the type of request. If your office is requesting a legal check or pre-DM (decision making) legal check, enter the bill's footer. If requesting a legal check to agree, please provide the bill number and draft number(s), if any.
3.  **Explanation of Request:** Similar to the previous forms, list the content of the request, including the problem to be addressed or requested action. It is important for you to provide as much pertinent information as possible. Feel free to attach any background materials that may assist the House Chief Attorney. If you are requesting a pre-legal check for a proposed draft, please attach a copy of the proposed draft.

![The forty columns rising 60 feet high that surround the Capitol building represent the graceful coconut palm trees found on every island. Photo by HMSO](image)

> <mark>**Tip** Although requests submitted to HMSO and the House Chief Attorney are completed pursuant to the requesting representative's instructions, the requesting representative may provide the name and phone number for an outside contact on the Request for Service form in the "explanation of request" section if necessary.</mark>

25

title: Part VI. Search Tools & Measure Tracking
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Part VI. Search Tools & Measure Tracking

## Capitol Website (located at <u>www.capitol.hawaii.gov</u>)

The search tool on the capitol website is the easiest way to keep track of current measures (i.e., measures introduced in the most recent biennium) and search the HRS and SLH online. House Bills, Senate Bills, Governor's Messages, and Reports to the Legislature as well as the status of legislation through first lateral, crossover, and beyond may also be tracked through the "Reports and Lists" link on the capitol website. Additionally, users may create customized measure tracking lists and request hearing notifications for specific committees.

![Screenshot of the Hawai'i State Legislature website homepage showing the search bar, navigation menu (Legislature, Events, Participate, Laws & Research, Help & Info), and quick links for My Legislature, Hearings, Live & On-Demand Video, and Reports & Lists. Red boxes highlight the search bar, the Laws & Research menu, the My Legislature section, and the Reports & Lists section.](image)

> <mark>**Tips**</mark>
> * Various sources of legislative information, including the HRS, SLH, and budget and grant-in-aid information, may be found under the "LAWS & RESEARCH" drop-down menu located on the capitol website. Under the Archives option, you may view measures and other documents dating back to the 1999 Regular Session.
> * Along with links to various legislative documents located on the capitol website, House Gateway provides links to House forms, training manuals, and memos from House agencies. Staff may access House Gateway at <u>http://house/</u> or via the desktop icon. ![House Gateway Home icon](image)

26

title: Tracking Legislation—Reports and Lists
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Tracking Legislation—Reports and Lists

The "Reports and Lists" link on the capitol website allows legislative staff and the public to automatically generate reports on legislation based on the committee referral, introducer, legislative deadline, and other categories. Some of the most used categories are *Committee Referrals* and *Measures by Introducer*. Using these two categories, you can look up all measures currently in a given committee and all measures introduced by your representative.

![Screenshots of the Hawaii State Legislature website showing the "Reports and Lists" interface with various dropdown menus for Deadline Tracking, Committee Referrals, and Measures by Introducer.](image)

> [!TIP]
> There are categories that become more useful as session progresses, including the *Deadline Tracking* and *Conference Committee* categories. You should familiarize yourself with the various categories. This will help you respond to a variety of representative and constituent requests.

27

title: Tracking Legislation—Customized Measure Tracking
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Tracking Legislation—Customized Measure Tracking

Both legislative staff and the public may also use the capitol website to create customized measure tracking lists and reports. By clicking on the "My Legislature" box on the capitol website main page and then selecting the "Track Measures" box, users can sign up for a Hawaii Legislature account using either their assigned capitol e-mail or a personal e-mail address. After logging in, users can create customized lists using the "Create List" function. Once the list is created, users can add specific measures to the list and add notes describing the measure's contents or things to do for specific measures. The example below has several lists that follow commonly requested measure topics, including:

(1) Bills of interest to your representative;
(2) Bills of interest to your community;
(3) Measures assigned to your representative's committee; and
(4) Potentially important Senate bills set to cross over.

To generate a report for a customized measure tracking list, simply click the "Generate Report" button.

![Screenshot of the Hawai'i State Legislature website's Measure Tracking interface, showing options to create lists like "Rep's Bills", "Community Issues", "Committee Measures", and "Possible SBs", with buttons for "Create List", "Add a Measure", and "Generate Report".](image)

<mark>**Tip** Since the "Reports and Lists" and "Measure Tracking" functions of the capitol website are available to the public, you may want to improve efficiency by teaching constituents how to use these functions to track legislation.</mark>

28

source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf

These reports can be exported to Microsoft Excel, which allows you to further customize your reports using filters and other settings. ### Measure Tracking Report: Committee Measures **Your personalized measure tracking report.** **Hawaii State Legislature** [Export to Excel] [Export to CSV]

Note that the *bill number*, *bill draft number*, *current status*, *introducer*, and other information are listed in the report. Clicking on the hyperlinked bill number will bring you to the measure’s bill status page on the capitol website. Any report generated for your customized measure tracking lists will also display your notes in the “*notes*” column. Clicking on the header of each column (i.e., “*measure*,” “*current status*,” “*introducer*,” etc.) will sort the information generated in the report alphabetically, numerically, or by date according to that column. **Tips** * The main advantage of using the “Measure Tracking” function over the “Reports and Lists” function to track legislation is that it allows for total customization of the measures included in reports. The downside of using the "Measure Tracking" function is that the list only includes measures that the user inputs, therefore important measures that should be tracked may be unintentionally omitted. When using the “Measure Tracking” function, remember to enter *all* measures that you want to track. * Although measure information is updated when reports are generated, it is very important to continually check your reports for accuracy, especially if you are using print copies. As bill statuses may change from day-to-day, it may be helpful to include time stamps on printed copies. 29

title: Part VII. Scheduling a Hearing
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Part VII. Scheduling a Hearing

As you may remember from pages 12-14, the Speaker refers all bills to the standing committee(s) with the appropriate subject matter jurisdiction. As staff, one of your most important jobs is to facilitate the process so that measures are heard, vetted, and voted on (if appropriate) by a referred committee in accordance with the internal House deadlines. For staff serving Committee Chairs and Vice-Chairs, one of the first steps in this process is to schedule hearings. Usually, staff serving representatives who are not Committee Chairs or Vice-Chairs will not have to schedule bills and resolutions for hearings.

* Generally, there are three types of referrals: single (e.g., HB1566), double (e.g., HB1571), and triple referrals (e.g., HB2630). Within these referral types is an additional subset of referrals: joint referrals (e.g., HB2630, HB1566, HB1571).
* In referrals, commas (,) mean "then" and slashes (/) mean "and." Commas indicate successive referrals meaning the later committees may not take action on the measure until the prior committee(s) have considered and reported out the measure. Slashes denote a joint referral. For example, HB1571 has a joint double referral: JHA/HLT, FIN. HB1571 will first be heard *simultaneously* by the Judiciary & Hawaiian Affairs and Health & Homelessness Committees, **then** it will be heard by the Finance Committee.

<description>
A screenshot of a "NOTICE OF HEARING" document from the House of Representatives, The Thirty-Second Legislature, Regular Session of 2024. It lists the Committee on Judiciary & Hawaiian Affairs (Chair David A. Tarnas, Vice Chair Gregg Takayama) and the Committee on Health & Homelessness (Chair Della Au Belatti, Vice Chair Jenna Takenouchi). The hearing is scheduled for Tuesday, February 6, 2024, at 2:00 PM via videoconference in Conference Room 325. The agenda includes HB 2630 (Relating to Mental Health Treatment), HB 1566 (Relating to Consumer Health Data), and HB 1571 (Relating to First Responders).
</description>

<mark>
![Tip icon](image) **Tip**: Referrals made after first reading are not always final. Under certain circumstances, bills can be re-referred. For example, by authorization from the Speaker, a triple referral bill can become a double referral. A single referral can also become a joint referral or vice versa. The form to request a re-referral may be found on House Gateway at: <u>http://house/House/Forms</u>.
</mark>

30

title: Beginning the Scheduling Process:
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![Created by German artist Otto Piene, the gold-plated, kinetic light sculpture hanging in the House of Representatives chamber represents the sun.](image)
*Photo by https://www.civilbeat.org/2015/05/the-projector-lava-and-more/*
*Created by German artist Otto Piene, the gold-plated, kinetic light sculpture hanging in the House of Representatives chamber represents the sun.*

# Beginning the Scheduling Process:

1. Before discussing hearings with the Chair, you may want to prioritize bills and resolutions with the legislative timetable in mind. The order of priority is generally as follows: **(1) Triple Referrals (e.g., HLT, JHA, FIN), (2) Double Referrals (e.g., JHA, FIN), (3) Single Referrals (e.g., FIN), (4) Concurrent Resolutions, and (5) House Resolutions. Within each of the referral types listed in (1)-(5), joint referrals (e.g., JHA/HLT, FIN) have priority.** This priority order corresponds with deadlines set out in the House Internal Legislative Timetable as shown on pages 33-35. Note that these deadlines often correspond with the various readings and dates set forth on pages 12-14.

2. Once you prioritize and organize measures, discuss scheduling options with your Chair. Determine what your Chair wants to do, how your Chair would like things scheduled (for example, grouping all measures with the same subject matter together or grouping all controversial measures together), and determine which measures are your Chair's priorities. Since committees are only assigned a set number of hearing days a week, as determined by the conference room schedule, you should also discuss **the total number of hearing days available before a deadline.** For example, if your committee only has two hearing days per week, it may be more difficult to hear all triple referral bills before the triple referral deadline. **Always remember that to continue on through the legislative process, all bills must be reported out of their committee(s) by the applicable deadlines, whether it be a triple referral, lateral, or decking deadline.** For example, a triple referral bill must be reported out of its first committee by the triple referral deadline.

<mark>
### Things to Consider When Scheduling a Hearing:

* Session length (60 legislative days)
* Hearing days of your committee
* Deadlines and 48-hour notice
* Measure priorities (referral type; bills vs. resolutions)
* Committee workload
* Bill type (e.g., controversial, subject matter, complexity; bills with extensive amendments should be heard earlier, not right before a deadline)
* Availability of representatives to attend hearings
* Conflicts with other committees
* Drafting agency processing time (schedule bills early when feasible to avoid hearings on deadline days)
</mark>

31

title: 2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
### Beginning the Scheduling Process (continued):

3. Once you finalize the bills to be heard, create a hearing notice using the "House Hearing Notice" template. You can access the template from any Capitol computer with Microsoft Word. Click "☺ Templates" and select "House hearing notice." For more information on creating hearing notices, please sign up for the session staff computer training and review the training materials on House Gateway, including the guides on drafting notices under Manuals & Guides and SAA Hearing Notice Checklist under Forms.

![Screenshot of Microsoft Word interface showing the "Legislative" ribbon group with the "Templates" button highlighted in red, and a "New" document dialog box with the "House hearing notice" template selected.](image)

<mark>
**Tips**

* Typically, **CHAIR'S STAFF schedules the hearings for bills and VICE-CHAIR'S STAFF schedules the hearings for resolutions.** However, due to the unpredictable nature of session, it may be helpful for all staff to be familiar with scheduling hearings.
* Only schedule bills and resolutions referred to your committee.
* Your Representative may request that a measure be re-referred. Initial referrals of bills after first reading may be appealed. Remember to check if bills in your committee have been re-referred.
* Always prioritize bills in line with the legislative timetable.
* Schedule measures early. This makes it easier to leave an extra hearing day open for last minute add-on requests to hearing lists (such as requests from other legislators or officials).
* Try to hold joint bills with similar committees on the same hearing days (e.g., all JHA/HLT joint measures should be heard on the same day).
* To aid with hearing preparation, Chair's staff should typically communicate with the Vice-Chair's staff on hearing/scheduling matters. It is helpful to coordinate beforehand the expectations and responsibilities of each set of legislative staffers.
</mark>

32

title: 2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![A wide-angle photograph of the Hawaii State Legislature in session, showing the chamber filled with legislators and spectators under a large, circular ceiling with a distinctive light fixture.](image)

*Photo by http://www.staradvertiser.com/2016/01/20/photo-galleries/hawaii-state-legislature-opening-day-jan-20/*

*Legislators are usually allowed to pre-file bills with the Chief Clerk’s Office seven days before Opening Day.*

While the <u>Legislative Timetable</u> dictates the internal timetables for <u>both</u> the House and the Senate, you should refer to the <u>House Internal Legislative Timetable</u> because it also includes deadlines and milestones applicable only to the House. Please be aware that the dates on the timetable change from session to session so use the calendar for the current session.

### January – Bill Introduction

The first set of deadlines revolve around **bill introduction** (bill intro). This deadline is the last day on which bills may be submitted for introduction. You may submit bill drafting requests before Opening Day, and bills may be pre-filed before opening day in accordance with the introduction guidelines memo issued by Speaker. Note that different types of bills have different cut-off dates, such as non-administrative bill packages (e.g., county packages and caucus packages, like the Kupuna Caucus) and administrative bill packages (executive branch packages). Legislative measures requested by state officials, departments, or individuals may be denoted as introduced by the Speaker or Senate President “by request.”

### February – Triple Referral, First Lateral, First Lateral Filing

Two of the next major deadlines usually occur in February. The **triple referral deadline** refers to the deadline by which bills with three or more consecutive committee referrals, need to be reported out of the committee of first referral. On the timetable, there are two first lateral deadlines indicated. The **first lateral filing deadline** is the last day on which the penultimate (second to last) committee may report a bill out and file it with CCO. Subsequently, the **first lateral deadline** requires all bills with two or more consecutive referrals to be reported to the committee of last referral.

34

title: 2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
### End of February/Beginning of March – First Decking, First Crossover

The next set of deadlines, usually occurring during the final days of February and beginning of March, are First Decking and First Crossover. Bills up for Third Reading must be reported out of their final committee and filed with CCO by the **first decking deadline** so that they are available to members at least 48 hours before a vote by the entire House.

![Calendar icon showing MAR 6](image)

For example, a bill with a HLT, CPC, FIN referral would need to be reported out of FIN and filed with CCO by the first decking deadline. **First crossover** is the date by which all measures that have passed out of their referred committees must be voted on and passed on third reading by the House. Measures successfully "crossing over" are passed on to the Senate for consideration (in a similar process to that of the House). Note that nothing precludes bills from being decked or crossing over before each of these deadlines.

Senate bills, like House bills, also cross over. Senate bills that cross over to the House follow a similar procedure (receiving referrals, etc.). After the assignment of committee referrals, Senate bills must also pass a similar set of triple referral, lateral, and decking deadlines.

### April/May – Conference; Sine Die

The **conference** period begins in the middle of April. For a bill to pass the Legislature, both the House and the Senate must agree to the same version of the bill. If the House and the Senate have passed different versions of the same bill and there is no subsequent agreement by the house in which the bill was introduced to the changes made by the other house, that bill goes to conference. At conference, House and Senate members meet to discuss the differences in the bill and hopefully come to an agreement on the final conference draft language suitable to both the House and Senate. Since conference contains a unique set of procedures (that build upon what is in this manual), a separate conference training is generally held right before the conference period begins.

The last deadline to remember is **sine die**. Sine Die (Latin for "without [another] day") marks the end of the legislative session and the completion of your session! **All bills** must be decked at least 48 hours before sine die in order to pass the Legislature.

> ![Hibiscus flower icon with the word Tip](image) **Tip**: The House Internal Legislative Timetable can provide clues on when you will be busiest. For example, if you work for a first committee with many triple referrals, you will likely be busy around the triple referral deadline. Staff for all representatives will normally be busy around voting deadlines such as third reading.

35

title: PART VIII. The Public Hearing
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![Screenshot from the Hawaii House of Representatives YouTube channel showing a committee hearing in progress with a lower-third graphic reading "HB1648 TUE, JAN 30, 2024 4:31 PM"](image) *The Hawaii State Constitution and House Rules require decision making sessions and standing committee meetings to be public.* # PART VIII. The Public Hearing A public hearing can be divided into four main segments, each requiring staff to perform a specific set of duties. Chair’s staff, Vice-Chair’s staff, and member’s staff will all have varying duties during a public hearing. ![Illustration of a wooden gavel striking a block](image) ```mermaid graph TD A["**Set Up**
Preparations to ensure a smooth hearing"] A --> B["**When the Gavel Falls**
Testimony is heard and questions are asked"] B --> C["**Decision Making**
The Committee votes on the Lead Chair's
recommendations"] C --> D["**After the Hearing**
Returning the room to the way it was found and
getting ready to start again"] style A fill:#9db084,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style B fill:#9db084,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#9db084,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style D fill:#9db084,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` Public hearings provide legislators with opportunities to fact-find and receive public input on measures. By holding hearings, legislators are better equipped to make informed decisions on issues relevant to the particular subject matter committee’s jurisdiction. The following is a typical delineation of responsibilities. Be sure to check with your representative and office manager to ensure that you account for your representative’s hearing procedure preferences. 36

title: 2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
The following is a traditional breakdown of the duties of Chair, Vice-Chair, and member staff before, during, and after a public hearing:

### Before the Hearing Day

#### Chair's Staff
* Establish a filing system to maintain the committee files.
* Organize and schedule measures. Hearing notices must be filed at least 48 hours before each hearing to inform the public of when bills will be heard.
* Familiarize yourself with the bills and testimony so you can answer your Chair's questions.
* Determine which bills need special consideration (e.g., security or media for controversial bills).

#### Vice-Chair's Staff
* Process and assemble testimonies for electronic committee hearing packages.
* Once the testifiers are finalized, generate the electronic hearing testimony package.
* Process late testimony.
* Prepare Record of Votes forms for each measure on the hearing notice.
* Have sufficient supplies for all members of the committee.
* Coordinate with the Chair's staff to arrange for special services (e.g., interpreters), if necessary.

#### Member's Staff
* Ensure your representative knows when hearings are scheduled.
* Help your representative organize and prepare for the hearing.

![Designed by Ruthadell Anderson, the 39-foot tall tapestry that hangs in the House of Representatives chamber uses brown, gold, red, and orange tones of lava and earth. Photo by HMSO](image)

<mark>
**Tips**
* Know your Chair's schedule in case measures need to be deferred to future agendas.
* If necessary, a Chair may request a waiver of the 48-hour hearing notice requirement from Speaker by competing the form located on House Gateway at: <u>http://house/House/Forms</u>.
* Use the capitol website to familiarize yourself with the names and faces of legislators.
* To save time, you may want to fill out the Record of Votes form with the measure number, including any draft numbers, and committee referral before each hearing. You may fill in the date, but if the measure gets deferred, you will have to make a new Record of Votes form.
* Depending on the Chair, the Vice-Chair may be assigned the task of hearing all resolutions referred to a committee. Vice-Chair's staff should familiarize themselves with hearing procedures.
</mark>

37

title: On the Hearing Day
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# On the Hearing Day

### Chair's Staff
* Bring laptops for yourself and Chair.
* Take good notes on the testifiers, testimony, and discussion of measures.
* Remind the Chair if they forget to call up a testifier or skip testimony or a measure.
* Watch the time and alert the Chair if the hearing is going to run over into another committee's hearing time or floor session.

### Vice-Chair's Staff
* Release the master testimony e-packet.
* Bring all supplies to the hearing room, including a timer if Chair would like to limit oral testimony.
* Place the Chair's gavel, vote forms, testimony packets, and nameplates.
* Post the hearing notice outside the room.
* If necessary, set up the conference room by arranging tables and chairs as requested by the Chair.
* Process late testimony and distribute to members.
* Assist the public and media.

### Member's Staff
* Take notes for your representative if they cannot attend.
* Ensure that your representative has the necessary and desired materials, including laptop.
* Be aware if your representative will be present for voting.

```description
A yellow sticky note with handwritten text.
```
**SAMPLE CHECKLIST**
1) Bring Rep.'s laptop
2) Bring three-ring binder with today's bills
3) Check Rep.'s availability on Friday regarding EEP hearing

<mark>
![Red hibiscus flower icon](image) **Tips**
* Try to have at least one staffer present at the hearing to take notes (usually the Chair's staff).
* During the hearing, instead of directly speaking to a Chair or member, pass a note.
* Always keep a phone and House directory (available at the Public Access Room) handy for quorum and contact purposes.
* Although diligent note-taking is recommended, always try to keep an eye on your representative in case they have questions or tasks for you during the hearing. Also keep in contact with other staff in your office should you need to leave the hearing.
* Expect last-minute problems and interruptions and be prepared to meet them in a friendly and professional manner.
</mark>

38

title: After the Hearing
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# After the Hearing

## Chair’s Staff
* Check accuracy of the Record of Votes form and create a marked-up hearing notice.
* Turn in a copy of the marked-up hearing notice and the yellow copy of the Record of Votes form to CCO.
* Give the pink Record of Votes form to HMSO.
* File the original, white Record of Votes form with the bill jacket.
* Review notes. Follow up with Chair if needed.
* Communicate with drafting agency staff regarding drafts to be completed.
* Update your measure tracking lists.
* Review all documents received from the drafting agencies both individually and with the Chair before filing the documents with CCO.

## Vice-Chair’s Staff
* Clean up the conference room: straighten up furniture, collect materials left behind, and remove the hearing notice posted outside.
* Process late testimony that arrived during the hearing.
* Once all testimony data has been processed and finalized, upload the testimony file to the Legislative website.
* Archive the hearing testimony package.
* Re-organize supplies so it is ready for the next hearing.

## Member’s Staff
* Update the representative on what happened during the hearing (if your representative did not attend).
* Follow up on any questions or requests that your representative may have following the hearing.

![A yellow sticky note with handwritten text and a black Sharpie marker. The note reads: "SAMPLE HEARING UPDATE 1) Changed appropriation amount to $20K in H.B. 4 2) Title concern on H.B. 1 3) Check on Dept. of Health's question about program timeframe on H.B. 7"](image)

> **Tip**
> Make sure you have the correct Record of Votes form for your committee. Keep the vote forms in a separate folder, along with extra blank copies

39

title: *The Public Hearing — Testimony*
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# *The Public Hearing — Testimony* The Vice-Chair’s staff is usually in charge of committee testimony. Testimony can be received by the committee in various ways. It can be submitted through the Capitol website, through fax, delivered in hard copy, emailed as an attachment, and emailed in the body of a message. The House has developed an application to help Vice-Chair's staff assemble testimonies for electronic committee hearing packages. For more information, please review the Paperless e-Testimony Manual under Manuals & Guides on House Gateway: http://house/Training. ## Order of Testifiers Testimony is generally organized in the order set out below. However, the final method of organization should be left up to the Chair.
General order for testimony in the master packet Federal officials
Federal departments
State officials
State departments
Other branches of government (Judiciary, OHA)
County officials
County departments
Private organizations
Individuals
![Hibiscus flower icon](image) **Tips** * You can also organize testimony by whether it supports, opposes, or provides comments on a given measure. * Characterization of testimony depends on the Chair’s preferences and can be expanded to include more detail like “support with comments,” “oppose with comments,” or “support intent.” A yellow sticky note with handwritten text is placed over the bottom right of the page. > ORGANIZING TESTIMONY > FOR REP. SUNNY ALOHA > > 1) Chair's categories: support, oppose, comments > > 2) Unless testimony has unequivocal support, list testimony as "comments" 40

title: The Public Hearing — Testimony (continued)
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![A large group of people seated in a hearing room with tables and chairs arranged for a public meeting.](image)

Screenshot from Hawaii State Legislature House Webcast Archive Pilot Project

To accommodate large groups, seating at hearings can be modified by arranging tables and chairs as requested by the Chair. # The Public Hearing — Testimony (continued) ## Late Testimony ![LATE stamp](image) Written testimony should be submitted at least 24 hours before a hearing. However, late testimony may be accepted by a committee. Check with the Chair to see if there is a specific time when testimony is too late to be accepted. More information on processing late testimony may be found in the Paperless e-Testimony Manual. ## Oral Testifiers ![A man, identified as UH women's volleyball coach Dave Shoji, testifying at a microphone. A broadcast overlay shows "SB 83 SD1 (SSCR #2735) House RELATING TO APPROPRIATIONS. University of Hawaii Intercollegiate Athletics Program; Tourism Special Fund Appropriation".](image)

Screenshot from Hawaii State Legislature House Webcast Archive Pilot Project
UH women’s volleyball coach Dave Shoji testified on funding UH athletics.

Some testifiers do not provide written testimony. After all of the testifiers on the list have testified, the Chair usually asks other persons wanting to testify on the measure to come forward. For these testifiers, record as much information as possible, including the testifier’s name, organization, and position on the measure. The Chair may require the testifier to submit written testimony, which may require follow-up communication. You may need to speak to the testifier right after the testifier’s testimony is given and ask for relevant information. ![Hibiscus flower icon with the word "Tip"](image) You may want to have a sign-in sheet for oral testifiers not providing written testimony: **Sample Sign-in Sheet for Oral Testifiers**
Testifier List — Please sign-in if you do not plan on providing written testimony to accompany your oral testimony (please write legibly)
Name (Last, first) Organization (if any) Phone # or email address (optional) Bill or Resolution # Position: Support/Oppose/Comments

title: *The Public Hearing — Decision Making*
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# *The Public Hearing — Decision Making* Decision making occurs when committee members publicly vote on measures currently being heard in committee or deferred from a previous hearing. Committee members generally vote on the **lead Chair’s recommendation** for each measure. In decision making, the lead Chair has several options for recommendations. Recommendations include: (1) “Passing the measure as is” or unamended, (2) “Passing the measure with a House Draft,” or with amendments, (3) “Holding the measure,” (4) “Passing a short form bill with a House Draft to recommit for future public hearing,” (5) “Deferring the measure to another (specified) time,” and (6) “Deferring the measure indefinitely.” Recommendations (1), (2), (3), and (4) require a vote by the committee. Recommendations (5) and (6) do not require a vote by the committee and are at the Chair’s discretion. A Chair may defer a measure either temporarily (5) or indefinitely (6) to give the committee more time to consider a measure. Voting to hold a measure (3) is a statement of disapproval or non-support of a measure and will prevent the measure from proceeding further in the legislative process. ### **Quorum:** * A majority of the committee members, also known as a quorum, must be present for decision making to take place. For joint committees, a quorum is required for **each** committee participating in the decision making. * If quorum is not present, absent committee members may need to be contacted by phone or using the PA system through SAA. Bringing a copy of the House directory to hearings is helpful in these situations. * For the Chair’s recommendation to pass, there must be (1) a quorum, and (2) a majority of “aye” votes of the quorum. **Measure Outcomes** ```mermaid graph TD A[Chair recommends action on a measure] --> B[Vice Chair takes the vote] A --> C[Members may vote one of three ways] B --> D[Tallies votes and fills out the Record of Votes form] C --> E[No/Nay] C --> F["Aye/Yes with Reservations (w/r)"] C --> G[Aye/Yes] ``` **Determining Quorum**
Number of Committee Members Minimum Quorum Required to Vote Minimum Quorum Required for Recommendation to Pass
12 7 4
7 4 3
15 8 5
4 3 2
42

title: Committee Reports
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Committee Reports

Committee Reports are drafted when a committee decides to pass a bill or resolution out of the committee in either amended or unamended (as is) form. Committee reports do not have to be drafted when a measure is deferred or if a measure is held. Committee reports generally: state the purpose of the measure as it was when the committee received the measure; identify the testifiers and their positions; state findings of facts and conclusions; and describe amendments made to the measure, if any. Upon the Chair's request, the committee report can also include other language, including issues or concerns raised during the hearing.

<description>
The following section is a document overlay with various callout boxes and annotations.
</description>

```description
STAND. COM. REP. NO. 250-24
Honolulu, Hawaii
FEB 12, 2024
RE: H.B. No. 2736
    H.D. 1
```
<mark>5A</mark>

Honorable Scott K. Saiki
Speaker, House of Representatives
Thirty-Second State Legislature
Regular Session of 2024
State of Hawaii

<mark>5B</mark> Your Committee on Culture, Arts, & International Affairs, to which was referred <mark>H.B. No. 2736</mark> entitled:

"A BILL FOR AN ACT RELATING TO STATE GESTURE,"

begs leave to report as follows:

<mark>1</mark> The purpose of this measure is to adopt, establish, and designate the shaka as the official gesture of the State.

<mark>2</mark> Your Committee received testimony in support of this measure from the Hawaii Bulletin; Hawaii.bio Inc.; Pono Connections LLC; and numerous individuals.

<mark>3</mark> Your Committee finds that all origin theories of the shaka gesture have the shaka developing within the State. Your Committee further finds that the shaka has positive connotations as it is used to share aloha, foster connection, and be pono. By adopting, establishing, and designating the shaka as the official gesture of the State, Hawaii can better preserve its brand association with the shaka, better secure recognition as the birthplace of the shaka, and better preserve the meaning of the shaka.

<mark>4</mark> Your Committee has amended this measure by changing the effective date to July 1, 3000, to encourage further discussion.

<mark>6</mark> As affirmed by the record of votes of the members of your Committee on Culture, Arts, & International Affairs that is attached to this report, your Committee is in accord with the intent and purpose of <mark>H.B. No. 2736</mark>, as amended herein, and recommends that it pass Second Reading in the form attached hereto <mark>as H.B. No. 2736, H.D. 1,</mark> and be referred to your Committee on Judiciary & Hawaiian Affairs. <mark>5B</mark>

<mark>5C</mark>
<mark>7</mark>
Respectfully submitted on behalf of the members of the Committee on Culture, Arts, & International Affairs,

title: Components of a Committee Report
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Components of a Committee Report

1.  **Purpose of measure:** This is the purpose of the measure as it *came* to the committee (before amendments were made).
2.  **Testimony:** Subject to the Chair's preference, testimony is generally organized as "support," "oppose," and "comments." Within each category, the testifiers are usually listed in the following order: government organizations (US, State, and county), organizations (such as businesses and civic clubs), and then individuals. Do not list the names of individuals.
3.  **Findings:** This paragraph states the findings of facts and conclusions based thereon for the measure. It should be focused on the committee's subject matter.
4.  **Amendments:** This section applies to a measure that was passed out of committee with amendments. It describes how the committee(s) changed the measure. Check that all amendments are listed accurately and completely.
5.  **Draft numbers:**
    *   **5A** The bill's draft number as passed out of your committee (e.g., H.B. No. 2736, H.D. 1) will be reflected here. CCO will fill in the date and Standing Committee Report Number so you can leave those spaces blank.
    *   **5B** The first paragraph of the report and the first half of the recommendation paragraph contain the draft number as it came to your committee (e.g., H.B. No. 2736).
    *   **5C** The last part of the recommendation paragraph also contains the draft number reflecting that the committee has passed the measure out, as amended (e.g., H.B. No. 2736, H.D. 1). Ensure that draft numbers are listed correctly.
6.  **Recommendation:** This paragraph states the committee's recommendation, either to pass as is or with amendments.
7.  **Signature:** Before filing, a signature is needed from the Chair. For joint hearings, all Chairs must sign the committee report.

<mark>
<u>**Committee Report To Do List**</u>

*   At the hearing, listen carefully and take accurate notes on what happens, *especially during decision making*.
*   Read and understand the measure as it was received by your committee and as it was reported out of your committee.
*   Read the committee report. Ensure it accurately states the measure's purpose; amendments, if any; and the Chair's notes, if applicable.
*   Read and check that the testifier paragraph is accurate and includes late testimony, as directed by the Chair.
</mark>

46

title: Staff Responsibilities
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
![A person's hands taking notes with a pen on a notepad during a meeting or lecture.](image)
*Photo by https://digest.bps.org.uk/2015/05/11/most-students-struggle-to-take-effective-lecture-notes-here-are-two-ways-to-help-them/*
*Committee clerks and drafters often compare notes to verify the Chair’s amendments and requested committee report language.*

# Staff Responsibilities

## <u>Hearing Notes and the Drafting Process</u>
*The information below is mostly for Chair’s staff.*

* Taking good notes is an essential part of the duties of the Chair’s staff. The sample on the right is a good example of what your hearing notes should include for the bill. Notice that the notes include the precise amendments to be made to the bill.

* After the hearing, unlike during bill introduction, you do not need to fill out a Request for Service form for each measure and committee report. Instead, coordinate drafting assignments with the appropriate drafting agency or agencies.

<description>
A yellow sticky note with handwritten text.
</description>
> LAB Hearing [space] 2/4/26
>
> HB553 HD1 (Minimum wage)
>
> 1) Increase min. wage to $25 per hour beginning 1/1/30
>
> 2) Change effective date to 7/1/3000
>
> 3) Techs

* After the hearing, the assigned drafter for your committee may need to discuss the amendments and special committee report language, if any. Please make yourself available for any questions and check in with your drafter before leaving the office for the day.

47

title: Staff Responsibilities (continued)
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Staff Responsibilities (continued)

*   ***What documents will you receive back from a drafting agency after the hearing?*** Typically, you will receive the committee report and, if the measure was passed with amendments, the new draft of the measure. You may also receive a transmittal memorandum noting any issues or concerns with the measure.

![Staff should double-check all measures and committee reports for accuracy, clarity, and completeness.](image)

Photo by HMSO
*Staff should double-check all measures and committee reports for accuracy, clarity, and completeness.*

*   ***What should you do before and after the hearing to make sure you receive a good final product?*** During the drafting and review process, the drafting agency usually checks the bill for both substantive and technical issues. Technical issues include grammatical issues. You should inform your Chair of these issues and any other substantive issues, if raised. You should also double-check the documents you get back and make sure they accurately reflect what your Chair wants.

*   ***Communicate!*** Communication among the drafting agency, representative, and staff is very important, especially given the hectic nature of session and deadlines.

<mark>
**Tips**

*   Notify the drafting agency if your Chair wants to characterize testimony in a certain way.
*   Bills may include effective dates that are far into the future such as "July 1, 3000" or another strange date. These are **defective dates** that are often used for strategic purposes.
*   Committee reports may also contain the phrase "making technical, nonsubstantive amendments for clarity, consistency, and style." This shows that technical amendments or "techs" were made. **Technical amendments** are nonsubstantive changes that involve correcting grammar and style and do not change the bill's substance. Techs *cannot* be made to a bill passed "as is."
*   In addition to drafting agencies, staff are also able to draft bills, committee reports, and resolutions using the templates built into Microsoft Word (on House computers). However, your drafts of bills and bill committee reports must be formatted by a drafting agency to be accepted for filing at CCO. Your drafts of resolutions and resolution committee reports do not need to be formatted by a drafting agency to be accepted for filing at CCO. Vice-Chair's staff should familiarize themselves with resolution drafting procedures. For House resolutions and House concurrent resolutions, please use the following naming conventions:
    HR (Rep.'s/agency's initials) (identifying no.) or HCR (Rep.'s/agency's initials) (identifying no.).
*   Committee reports for HR/HCR companions may not always be identical. Some proponents of resolutions may only choose to submit testimony on either the HR or HCR. As a result, testifier paragraphs for the HR and HCR may be different.
</mark>

<page_footer>
48
</page_footer>

title: Glossary
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Glossary

The Legislature uses its own set of terms. Here is a brief list of terms you may hear during the upcoming Legislative Session:

**Act:** A bill that has passed both houses of the Legislature; been enrolled, certified, and approved by the Governor or passed over the Governor’s veto; and published.

**Appropriation:** A legislative authorization to make expenditures and incur obligations for specific governmental purposes.

**As is:** Unamended.

**Bill Intro:** Beginning of session when representatives can introduce bills. Be aware of bill intro limits and deadlines set by the Speaker.

**Bill Status:** Where the bill is at in the legislative process. You can find this info by going to the capitol website and typing in the bill number. Bill information from previous sessions from 1999 to the current year can also be found on the capitol website.

**Carry-over (bill):** A bill pending at the final adjournment of a regular session in an odd-numbered year. Such bills automatically carry over and are still alive for consideration in the next regular session (even-numbered year).

**CCO:** House Chief Clerk’s Office (ext. 6-6400).

**Certs:** Congratulatory certificates.

**Con Am:** Constitutional Amendment, or an amendment proposed to the Hawaii State Constitution. Requires a ballot vote.

**Conference:** The very busy last few weeks of session. Representatives and Senators meet to resolve differences in bills and resolutions.

**Deck, Decking:** An internal house deadline referring to the placement of a bill on the chief clerk’s desk for third or final reading. *See House Internal Legislative Timetable.*

**Defective Date:** An effective date that is far in the future, inserted for strategic purposes. Example: July 1, 3000.

49

title: Glossary
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Glossary

**Double (referral):** A measure (usually a bill) referred to two different, consecutive committees.

**Dumped Docs:** Documents electronically transferred to one of the drafting agencies or CCO.

**Effective Date:** The date on which the bill will take effect if it passes the Legislature and is enacted into law.

**Fiscal (bill):** A "fiscal" bill is a bill referred to the House or Senate "money" committees that has fiscal implications for the State.

**Floor Amendment:** An amendment to a bill that is introduced on the floor, rather than in committee.

**Floor Session:** When the House or Senate, respectively, meets as a body to conduct legislative business, typically in their chambers. A legislative day is one on which there is a floor session.

**Forty-Eight-Hour (48-hour) Rule:** The constitutional requirement that printed copies of a bill in the form to be passed be made available to members at least 48 hours before its passage on third or final reading. May also refer to the House rule that requires the notice of a standing committee meeting to be publicly posted or announced on the House floor at least 48 hours before the meeting.

**Gavel In/Gavel Out:** For public hearings, the procedure by which the Chair signals the start or end of a hearing.

**Governor's Message:** Official communications from the Governor. Often refers to emergency appropriations and gubernatorial appointments.

**HAR:** Hawaii Administrative Rules.

**HD/SD/CD:** House, Senate, or Conference draft of a measure, as in H.B. No. 553, H.D. 1.

**Hearing:** An opportunity for committees to obtain information on measures from interested parties and the public.

50

title: Glossary
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Glossary

**Hearing Notice:** A notice typically put out by the Chair of the committee notifying the public of a scheduled hearing.

**HMIR:** House Minority Research Office (ext. 6-7033). Drafting agency that serves only members of the House minority party.

**HMSO:** House Majority Staff Office (ext. 6-6300). Drafting agency that serves only members of the House majority party.

**HRS:** Hawaii Revised Statutes. The HRS are the navy hardback books and white soft cover cumulative supplements that contain codified laws.

**Joint Hearing:** A hearing at which two or more committees meet to hear measures jointly referred to those committees.

**Lateral:** An internal house deadline. *See* House Internal Legislative Timetable.

**Legal Check:** A form that indicates that a bill has been checked for form and legality and may be filed with CCO. CCO requires a legal check for each bill that is up for third or final reading.

**LRB:** Legislative Reference Bureau (ext. 7-0666). LRB provides drafting and research services for all members, conducts studies, and runs the LRB Library and Public Access Room (PAR).

**Member of a committee:** A representative on a committee. Chairs and Vice-Chairs are considered members of a committee.

**OD:** Order of the Day, which is the official agenda of the floor session.

**OM:** Office manager of a representative's office.

**Prior Concurrence:** The requirement that, where there is more than one committee referral, subsequent committees on the referral must obtain the concurrence of the previous committee(s) before making any changes to the measure within the respective jurisdiction of the previous committee(s).

**Quorum:** The requirement that a majority of a committee's members be present for decision making to take place. For joint committees, quorum is required for *each* committee.

51

title: Glossary
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Glossary

**Ramseyering:** The method used by the Legislature to show how a bill proposes to amend the HRS or SLH. New material is underlined and repealed material is bracketed and stricken.

**Resos:** Resolutions. Resos usually do not have the force and effect of law.

**SAA:** House Sergeant-At-Arms (ext. 6-6500).

**Session:** 60-legislative-day period in which the Legislature convenes to conduct legislative business, including introducing and passing legislation.

**Short-Form (bill):** A very brief bill containing no substantive language. Used as vehicles for ideas that arise after the bill introduction cut-off date. Only Chairs may introduce short-form bills.

**Sine Die:** The last day of session!

**Single (referral):** A measure (usually a bill) that is referred to one committee.

**SLH:** Session Laws of Hawaii. The SLH are the beige soft cover books that contain all of the enacted measures for each year. Usually referred to as "SLH (year)."

**Sunset:** The date on which a certain legislative act or program is repealed.

**Techs:** Technical amendments. These are nonsubstantive amendments made to, among other things, correct grammar, prefatory language, and statutory language for the purposes of clarity, consistency, and style.

**Testimony:** Written or oral information provided by officials, departments, organizations, or the public on measures during public hearings.

**Triple (referral):** A measure (usually a bill) that is referred to three different, consecutive committees.

**Waiver:** Allows a bill to be heard with less than the required 48-hour public notice. Usually granted by leave of the Speaker.

52

title: Additional Resources
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/2026 House Staff Quick-Start Guide.pdf
text:
# Additional Resources

This list provides links and references to more detailed information on the subjects described in each part of this guide.

<u>**Part I. Overview of State Government and the Legislature**</u>

For information on specific government branches or agencies, please see:
<mark>https://lrb.hawaii.gov/wp-content/uploads/GuideToGovernmentInHawaii_2020.pdf</mark>.

<u>**Part II. Sources of Law**</u>

For more information on various sources of law utilized in Hawaii, please see the following:
<mark>https://law-hawaii.libguides.com/hawaii/hawaiilaws</mark>.

<u>**Part III. The Legislative Process**</u>

The legislative process is covered in depth in the Citizen’s Guide to the Legislative Process available on the capitol website at: <mark>https://www.capitol.hawaii.gov/citizensguide.aspx</mark>.

More information about the House Journal, which is the official record of all floor session proceedings of the House, may be found under Manuals & Guides on the House Gateway website at: <mark>http://house/Training</mark>.

<u>**Part IV. Components of Bills, Resolutions, and Certificates**</u>

For more information on bill and resolution drafting, see the Hawaii Legislative Drafting Manual published by LRB at: <mark>https://lrb.hawaii.gov/wp-content/uploads/2022_HawaiiLegislativeDraftingManual.pdf</mark>. Additional information can also be found regarding the creation and amendment of bills and resolutions under Manuals & Guides on the House Gateway website at: <mark>http://house/Training</mark>.

For more information on congratulatory certificates, see the Congratulatory Documents Manual located in the HMSO Manuals and Forms folder on your office manager’s desktop.

Information on how to e-sign a bill or resolution may be found under Manuals & Guides on the House Gateway website at: <mark>http://house/Training</mark>.

<u>**Part V. Working With Your Drafting Agency**</u>

This guide provides the most information for creating requests for service for HMSO. It may be helpful to contact other drafting agencies for information more pertinent to their request for service system. For more information on the type of work that HMSO can assist you with, please contact the HMSO front desk (ext. 6-6300).

title: Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
![The interior courtyard of the Hawaii State Capitol building, showing the open-air rotunda and tiered balconies.](image)

# Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable

### Why Do We Hold Public Hearings?

*   The Hawaii Constitution and the Rules of the House of Representatives require decision-making sessions and standing committee meetings to be open to the public.

### Public Hearings

![A man in a plaid shirt speaking at a public hearing microphone.](image)

[https://sway.cloud.microsoft/KcXaZOlMqMeKx0ee#content=bayaS25PlJRbxj](https://sway.cloud.microsoft/KcXaZOlMqMeKx0ee#content=bayaS25PlJRbxj)

title: Referrals
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
* Allow lawmakers to make informed decisions on important issues.
* Inform the public on government affairs and allow for public input.
* Allow the lawmakers to focus on specific issues.
* Provide timely and efficient solutions.

## Referrals

After a bill or resolution (also known as a measure) is introduced, it is referred to a committee by the Speaker of the House for a public hearing. Measures are referred to at least one committee based on subject matter, but are commonly referred to two or more committees.

![A group of people in professional attire running down a set of stairs outside a government building.](image)

Once a measure is referred, it receives a bill jacket. **DO NOT LOSE THE BILL JACKET.** The House Chief Clerk's Office uses the bill jacket as the official copy of a bill.

If a committee chair feels that a measure should have been referred to their committee based on the measure's subject matter, the chair can fill out a form asking Speaker for a re-referral.

The form can be found on the House Gateway with the following pathway:

House Gateway > House Tab > Forms > Under the header "Session Forms"

title: Types of Referrals
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
# Types of Referrals

There are four types of referrals:

1. **Triple Referrals**, where a measure is referred to three separate committees
2. **Double Referrals**, where a measure is referred to two separate committees
3. **Single Referrals**, where a measure is referred to one committee
4. **Joint Referrals**, where a measure is referred to two (or more) committees holding a joint hearing

**Triple Referral**

HSG, CPC, FIN

**Double Referral**

HSG, FIN

**Single Referral**

FIN

**Joint Referral**

AGR, ECD/CPC, FIN

When looking at a referral, note the **comma** between each committee, such as the comma separating HSG, CPC, and FIN in the triple referral. The comma means "then" -- i.e., first the Housing Committee (HSG) will hear the measure, then the Consumer Protection & Commerce Committee (CPC), and finally the Finance Committee (FIN).

However, note that the joint referral has a **forward slash (/)** between the two committees, rather than a comma. When looking at a referral, this means "and" -- i.e., first committee A will hear the measure, then committee B **and** C will jointly hear the measure. Additionally, when looking at a joint referral, the **lead committee** will be the **first committee listed**. In the joint referral example, the Economic Development & Technology Committee (ECD) is responsible for scheduling the joint hearing and drafting the hearing notice, in coordination with .

title: Scheduling Considerations
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
# Scheduling Considerations

## Order of Priority

When scheduling a hearing, consider the deadlines on the House Internal Legislative Timetable and schedule your measures based on the order of priority.

Measures should be considered in the following priority:

1. Triple referrals
2. Double referrals
3. Single referrals
4. Concurrent Resolutions
5. House Resolutions

Within each referral type, joint referrals should take priority because it takes extra coordination between the multiple committees involved to schedule a joint referral.

Embed://`<iframe width="640px" height= "480px" src= "https://forms.office.com/Pages/ResponsePage.aspx?id=lYJdyL7aSkmwZ_qrwuLcLCicLQE-G7ZOlfarTqbD2TtUQjVMVlUxMlYwUEZRTVNNR0JGQkM0S1NKWC4u&embed=true" frameborder= "0" marginwidth= "0" marginheight= "0" style= "border: none; max-width:100%; max-height:100vh" allowfullscreen webkitallowfullscreen mozallowfullscreen msallowfullscreen> </iframe>`

## Subject Matter and Other Considerations

When scheduling a hearing, also consider the subject matter of the measure. If you think the measure is controversial or will have long discussions with many conflicting points of view, consider scheduling the measure for its own hearing or as part of a hearing with a small number of other, non-controversial bills.

You should also take into account the Representatives' schedules to determine who will be available to attend hearings.

Finally, avoid time conflicts with other committees by following the conference room schedule. The committee brackets will determine whether your committee meets in the morning or afternoon.

title: Scheduling Committee Hearings
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
![A scene from the TV show Parks and Recreation showing characters Leslie Knope, Donna Meagle, and Ben Wyatt sitting at a committee table. A sign in the background says "NO FOOD OR DRINKS".](image) [https://sway.cloud.microsoft/KcXaZOlMqMeKx0ee#content=JyLFoMyyGmWpdY](https://sway.cloud.microsoft/KcXaZOlMqMeKx0ee#content=JyLFoMyyGmWpdY) # Scheduling Committee Hearings ## The Legislative Timetable There are several important deadlines to keep in mind when scheduling measures. The first four deadlines concern bills originating in the House and are as follows: **Triple Referral Filing (Bills) deadline** - Typically occurs three weeks after Opening Day. All House bills with **triple referrals** must be reported out of their **first** committee by this day. This means bills must have been voted on favorably and passed out of the committee and have a committee report drafted and filed by the end of the deadline day.
Before 1st lateral. After 1st lateral.
**First Lateral Filing (Bills) deadline** - Typically occurs one week after the Triple Referral Filing (Bills) deadline. All House bills with **multiple referrals** must be reported out of their **second to last** committee by this day. As with the triple referral deadline, this means that bills must have been voted on

title: 4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
favorably and passed out of the committee and have a committee report drafted and filed with the House Chief Clerk's Office by the end of the deadline day. **First Decking (Bills) deadline** - Typically occurs two weeks after the First Lateral Filing (Bills) deadline. All House bills that are still alive must be reported out of their **final committees** by this day. Decking is the act of placing bills on the lawmaker's desk for their review at least 48 hours before a third reading vote by the full house. Decking ensures a mandatory 48-hour opportunity for final review by the Representatives before they are asked to vote on the bill's Third Reading. ![A person lying on the floor covered in a large pile of papers.](image) **First Crossover** - Typically occurs less than one week after the First Decking (Bills) deadline. First crossover is the day by which all House bills must pass a **third reading vote** by the **full House** and be sent over to the Senate. Once the House bills have crossed over to the Senate, the Senate bills will cross over to the House and we'll have a whole new set of bills with similar deadlines (Second Triple Referral Filing, Second Lateral Filing, Second Decking, Second Crossover, and Final Decking) to work with. Be Aware of Deadlines! Embed://

via GIPHY

Be aware of the quick turnaround time between deadlines, especially the **one week** span between the Triple Referral Filing deadline and First Lateral Filing deadline. Most committees will only have two hearing dates available during that one-week span, so plan accordingly.

title: General Considerations
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/4 Scheduling Hearings and the Legislative Timetable.pdf
text:
# General Considerations

When scheduling a hearing, find out the Chair's priorities, what the Chair wants to do, and how the Chair wants things scheduled.

Determine what measures you want to hear (e.g., subject matter and number of measures) and how many days you have before a filing deadline. This will help you determine how many measures can be heard.

Try to schedule measures with the same subject matter on the same day. Similarly, try to schedule joint hearings with similar committees on the same day.

Complicated bills with extensive amendments should be heard earlier, not right before a deadline.

If possible, leave the last hearing day prior to a deadline open for last-minute requests or to correct oversights. If you must schedule measures on the last hearing day prior to a deadline, don't try to pack as many measures in as possible -- remember that it takes time for the drafting agencies to draft measures and committee reports, particularly if there are complex amendments.

Allow some buffer space for late additions -- be aware that a constituent or other legislator may want the Chair to hear a measure at the last minute.

It's also important to note that the House requires **48-hours notice** prior to the hearing.

Finally, be aware of where your committee falls within the referral pattern. If you are the first committee in a triple referral, your committee must hear the measure by the Triple Referral Filing (Bills) deadline. If you are the second committee in a triple referral, your committee must hear the measure by First Lateral Filing (Bills).

![A digital clock display showing "48 HRS." in glowing pink segments against a black background.](image)

HB 1654 RELATING TO AGRICULTURE. AGR
Status Short form bill relating to agriculture.
HB 1714 RELATING TO INVASIVE SPECIES. AGR, EEP, FIN
Status Appropriates funds to the Department of Land and Natural Resources for the management of the axis deer population in the county of Maui.
HB 1770 RELATING TO INVASIVE SPECIES. AGR, FIN
Status Establishes the invasive species rapid response special fund. Establishes procedures for emergency declarations and expenditures. Appropriates money.
HB 1922 RELATING TO INVASIVE SPECIES. AGR, FIN
Status Appropriates funds to the Department of Land and Natural Resources for efforts to substantially reduce and eradicate certain invasive species in the county of Maui.
HB 1861 RELATING TO COFFEE BERRY BORER. AGR, FIN
Status Extends the sunset date for the Coffee Berry Borer Pesticide Subsidy Program until June 30, 2025, for the purchase of pesticides containing Beauveria bassiana to combat the coffee berry borer. Extends the program manager position to June 30, 2026. Appropriates funds.
HB 1886 RELATING TO COFFEE LABELING. AGR, CPC, JUD
Status Requires coffee blend labels to disclose regional origins and percent by weight of the blended coffees. Prohibits using geographic origins of coffee in labeling or advertising for roasted or instant coffee that contains less than 51 percent coffee by weight from that geographic origin. Effective 7/1/2021.

text:
<u>HB 1887</u>
Status

RELATING TO IMPORTATION OF UNROASTED COFFEE INTO HAWAII.
Prohibits the importation of unroasted coffee into the State, makes it a class C felony, and imposes a fine for violation of the prohibition. Authorizes DOA to adopt rules to allow for the importation of partially roasted coffee into the State.

AGR, JUD

<u>HB 1897</u>
Status

RELATING TO COFFEE LABELING.
Expands the coffee labeling and advertising requirements to include ready-to-drink coffee beverages and inner wrapping or packaging labels. Effective 7/1/2021.

AGR, CPC, FIN

<u>HB 1898</u>
Status

RELATING TO LIVESTOCK TRANSPORTATION.
Requires the Department of Agriculture to adopt rules to protect the welfare of livestock transported to, from, or between any island of Hawaii. Requires the department to adopt interim rules.

AGR, TRN, FIN

<u>DECISION MAKING TO FOLLOW</u>

Persons wishing to offer comments should submit testimony at least <u>24 hours</u> prior to the hearing. Testimony should indicate:
* Testifier's name with position/title and organization;
* The Committee(s) to which the comments are directed;
* The date and time of the hearing; and
* Measure number.

While every effort will be made to incorporate all testimony received, materials received on the day of the hearing or improperly identified or directed, may be distributed to the Committee after the hearing.

Submit testimony in ONE of the following ways:
**PAPER**: 1 copy (including an original) to Room 441 in the State Capitol;
**FAX**: For testimony less than 5 pages in length, transmit to 808-586-6779 (Oahu) or 1-800-535-3859 (for Neighbor Islander without a computer to submit testimony through e-mail or the Web); or
**WEB**: For testimony less than 20MB in size, transmit from http://www.capitol.hawaii.gov/submittestimony.aspx.

Testimony submitted will be placed on the legislative website. This public posting of testimony on the website should be considered when including personal information in your testimony.

If you require special assistance or auxiliary aids and/or services to participate in the House public hearing process (i.e., sign or foreign language interpreter or wheelchair accessibility), please contact the Committee Clerk at 586-9605 or email your request for an interpreter to HouseInterpreter@Capitol.hawaii.gov at least <u>24 hours</u> prior to the hearing for arrangements. Prompt requests submitted help to ensure the availability of qualified individuals and appropriate accommodations.

![Signature of Rep. Richard P. Creagan](image)
Rep. Richard P. Creagan
Chair

Hearing AGR 01-29-20
![Barcode](image)

Below is an example of a joint referral hearing notice from the 2020 legislative session. This joint referral hearing has the Committee on Human Services and Homelessness (HSH) and the Committee on Heath (HLT) scheduled to hold a simultaneous or joint hearing on a specific date. The Committee on Human Services and Homelessness is the lead committee and is in charge of scheduling the joint hearing and creating the hearing notice. Note that both chairs must sign the hearing notice.

Now take a look at the measure being heard. It's a **joint triple referral**. The HSH/HLT designation is considered one referral, with JUD as the second referral, and FIN as the third and final referral.

# Scheduling Exercise

Using the calendar below as a guide, try to complete the following exercise. When you have completed the exercise, go to the next card in this stack to see how you did.

You are the Committee Clerk for the Transportation Committee (TRN) and your Chair would like you to schedule the following bills for hearings:

* H.B. No. 100 (TRN/JHA, FIN)
* H.B. No. 200 (TRN/JHA, FIN)
* H.B. No. 300 (TRN, CPC, FIN)
* H.B. No. 400 (ECD, TRN, FIN)
* H.B. No. 500 (TRN, JHA, FIN)
* H.B. No. 600 (TRN/CMV, FIN)
* H.B. No. 700 (TRN, FIN)
* H.B. No. 800 (TRN, JHA, FIN)
* H.B. No. 900 (TRN)

Use **January 29** as the first day to schedule hearings.

The Transportation Committee meets on **Wednesdays** and **Fridays**.

Other important information: **H.B. No. 300** is a **very controversial bill**. The CMV Committee happens to be meeting on **February 12**.
