title: House Training Chatbot
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
# House Training Chatbot

![Capitol Compass logo featuring a stylized building dome inside a speech bubble.](image)

### Internal Product Specification – Working Draft

***

### 1. Core Purpose

**Capitol Compass** is an internal AI-powered chatbot designed to assist staff working in the **House of Representatives** with both technical and non-technical questions related to legislative operations.

It will:

*   Support new and returning staff
*   Reduce repetitive supervisory questions
*   Improve onboarding consistency
*   Centralize procedural knowledge
*   Track confusion areas to improve training

It is an internal training and operational support tool.

***

### 2. Functional Scope

#### A. Types of Questions It Should Answer

The chatbot should support:

**Technical Questions**

*   How to upload testimony

title: House Training Chatbot - CK Notes
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
*   Hearing notice procedures
*   Committee scheduling steps
*   Deadlines and lateral movement
*   Conference committee procedures

### Non-Technical Questions

*   Who to contact for IT issues
*   Where to find hearing templates
*   How to reserve a room
*   Office phone numbers
*   Support office responsibilities
*   Who handles what type of request
*   When do I get paid?
*   Who handles parking?

***

### 3. Interface & User Experience

![Capitol Compass chatbot interface showing a conversation. User: "Which committee handles judiciary matters?" Bot: "Judiciary matters are handled by the Judiciary Committee." User: "When do they meet?" Bot: "The Judiciary Committee's next meeting is scheduled for April 25 at 2 p.m."](image)

#### A. Mobile Experience (Priority)

title: House Training Chatbot - CK Notes
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
On mobile, the interface should:

*   [ ] Look like a text messaging app
*   [ ] Have chat bubbles
*   [ ] Include timestamped responses
*   [ ] Be clean and minimal
*   [ ] Include suggested quick prompts

This aligns with how younger/newer staff communicate and lowers friction.

***

### **B. Desktop Experience**

On desktop:

*   [ ] Web-based interface
*   [ ] Chat layout preserved
*   [ ] Expandable side panel for:
    *   o Suggested questions
    *   o Legislative calendar
*   [ ] Ability to copy responses easily

***

### **4. Required Content Integrations**

#### **A. Legislative Directory Data**

The chatbot should include:

*   [ ] Names of all Representatives
*   [ ] District numbers
*   [ ] Standing committee assignments
*   [ ] Committee chairs/vice chairs

title: B. Legislative Calendar
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
*   Committee purviews
*   Contact information (if internal use approved)

This information must be:

*   Easily updateable
*   Session-specific
*   Sourced from official roster

***

## B. Legislative Calendar

The chatbot must include:

*   Most current legislative calendar
*   All session deadlines
*   Lateral deadlines
*   Decking deadlines
*   Conference deadlines
*   Sine die
*   Ability to answer: "When is first lateral?"

The system should:

*   Update automatically when calendar changes
*   Clearly indicate calendar version date

***

## C. Support Office Directory

Include:

*   Phone numbers
*   Email addresses (if appropriate)

title: House Training Chatbot - CK Notes
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
*   Description of responsibilities

Examples:

*   Chief Clerk’s Office
*   Sergeant-at-Arms
*   House Finance
*   IT Office
*   House Majority Staff Office
*   House Communications

The chatbot should be able to respond to:

*   “Who handles bill introductions?”
*   “Who do I call about livestream issues?”
*   “Who handles committee reports?”

***

### 5. Reporting & Analytics (Critical Feature)

The system must generate weekly reports including:

*   Total number of questions asked
*   Number of unique users
*   Types of questions (categorized automatically)
*   Most frequent topics
*   Questions the chatbot could not answer
*   Low-confidence responses

Reports should be:

*   Exportable (PDF or CSV)
*   Delivered automatically (optional email summary)

title: 6. New Staff Survey / FAQ Integration
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
[ ] Viewable in admin dashboard

***

## 6. New Staff Survey / FAQ Integration

The system should include:

### A. Built-In FAQ Library

Common real-world questions new staff ask:

*   [ ] “What is decking?”
*   [ ] “What does HD1 mean?”
*   [ ] “What’s the difference between first reading and first lateral?”
*   [ ] “What does it mean when a bill dies?”

This improves training over time and should come from frequently asked questions that are surveyed to new staff.

***

## 7. Access Control & Security

This section requires early architectural decisions.

### A. Login Options

#### Option 1: State Email Authentication (Recommended)

*   [ ] Require official House email login
*   [ ] Single sign-on integration
*   [ ] Cleanest access control

#### Option 2: Network-Based Restriction

*   [ ] Restrict to Capitol internal network IP range
*   [ ] Problem: Senate employees share same network

This alone cannot differentiate House vs Senate.

title: House Training Chatbot - CK Notes
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
### **B. Limiting Access to House Only**

To prevent Senate access:

*   [ ] Require House email domain authentication
*   [ ] Or maintain approved user list
*   [ ] Or restrict by Active Directory group

Network restriction alone is insufficient.

***

### **C. Majority-Only Access?**

Feasibility depends on:

*   [ ] Whether accounts can be tied to caucus-specific email groups
*   [ ] Whether political fairness concerns arise
*   [ ] Whether this becomes publicly scrutinized

Operationally possible.
Politically sensitive.
Would require leadership decision.

Most neutral implementation:

*   [ ] Available to all House staff
*   [ ] Avoid caucus-specific limitation

***

### **8. Usage Controls**

#### **A. Daily Question Cap**

Purpose:

*   [ ] Prevent spam
*   [ ] Prevent misuse

title: 9. Guardrails
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
*   [ ] Protect system load

Recommendation:

*   [ ] Cap at 50–100 questions per user per day
*   [ ] Soft warning at 80%
*   [ ] Admin override capability

Log excessive use.

***

## 9. Guardrails

The chatbot must:

*   [ ] Avoid legal interpretations
*   [ ] Avoid political strategy advice
*   [ ] Avoid HR or disciplinary advice
*   [ ] Provide escalation guidance:
    *   o “Please contact the Chief Clerk’s Office.”
    *   o “Consult your supervisor.”

It should never imply authority over official rulings.

***

## 10. Governance & Ownership

Must identify:

*   [ ] Who updates calendar?
*   [ ] Who uploads new documents?
*   [ ] Who reviews unanswered questions?
*   [ ] Who reviews weekly reports?
*   [ ] Who maintains system during interim?

title: 11. Technical Architecture Considerations
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
Without ownership, the system will decay.

## 11. Technical Architecture Considerations

*   [ ] Secure cloud hosting
*   [ ] Audit logging
*   [ ] Content version tracking
*   [ ] Ability to ingest structured + unstructured documents
*   [ ] Admin interface
*   [ ] Analytics dashboard
*   [ ] Role-based permissions
*   [ ] Scalable during peak session days

## 12. Open Questions for Leadership

1. House-only or full Legislature?
2. Include minority staff?
3. Include members or staff only?
4. Should it be public-facing eventually?
5. Who signs off on content accuracy?
6. Who owns risk management?

## 13. Potential Risks

*   [ ] Outdated legislative data
*   [ ] Overreliance by new staff
*   [ ] Misinterpretation of guidance

title: House Training Chatbot - CK Notes
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/House Training Chatbot - CK Notes.docx
text:
*   Political sensitivity
*   Data privacy concerns
*   Scope creep

***

### Strategic Observation

This is becoming more than a chatbot.

It is evolving into:

*   A training system
*   A legislative directory tool
*   A procedural assistant
*   A knowledge analytics engine
*   An institutional memory archive

That’s powerful — but it requires clarity about scope.

***

# A BASIC INTRODUCTION TO USING LIVESTATUS During the legislative session, the organization of bill reports is a common task among legislative analysts, legislative attorneys, committee clerks, and office staff. LiveStatus is an Excel-based template created by Jon Shimabuku (Senate Data Systems Director (Retired)) and available for use in the House and Senate. It allows you to manage and update lists of bills in Excel and is a useful tracking and organizational tool. ![Screenshot of the LiveStatus Excel interface showing various configuration buttons and options for tracking legislative bills.](image)

title: A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus.pdf
text:
<u>**OPENING LIVESTATUS**</u>

To get a blank LiveStatus workbook, go to:
**RESEARCH & DRAFTING RESOURCES (AAs) folder – LiveStatus subfolder.**

![File explorer path: Network > Hservr6 > HMSO > RESEARCH & DRAFTING RESOURCES (AAs) > LiveStatus](image)

![Grid of file icons showing various LiveStatus templates and guides. A red circle highlights "05_LiveStatus Blank Template (No preprogramme..".](image)

> *The other templates in this subfolder have special sorting and formatting already applied and are designed to be used for tracking measures during Session. An overview of those is provided in a separate training.*

**<u>Note:</u>** There are two blank LiveStatus templates saved on our network. The only differences between ours and the "From Gateway" version is that version renames the default worksheets and allows you to pull in bills from a particular Order of the Day.

You can also access the "From Gateway" version by going to the House Gateway and clicking on the House Forms icon along the right side of the Gateway.

![House Gateway icons with the House Forms icon (a clipboard with a list) circled in red.](image)

Then, scroll to the bottom of the Forms page to the Other Templates section and click on "**Live Status – Update Bill Status Reports in Excel**".

### Other Templates

Creating Outlook Calendar in MS Word
Date & Time Received Stamp
Late Stamp
Listing Files Stored in a Selected Folder

title: THE "HEARING" SHEET
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# THE "HEARING" SHEET

To use LiveStatus, you will need to get a list of bills via the **"Hearing"** worksheet within the LiveStatus workbook.

![Screenshot of the Excel "Hearing" worksheet showing the Choose Notice dropdown, Update List of Bills and Create Report Sheet buttons, and a sidebar with Purpose and Usage instructions. The "Hearing" tab at the bottom is circled in red.](image)

The list will begin in cell B7 (the peach colored cell) and continue downward. You can get the list of bills in those cells in one of three ways:

1. Use the dropdown in cell B2, which will have a list of upcoming hearings. Once you've chosen a hearing, click on the button to **"Update List of Bills"** and the list will populate;
2. Type a list of bills; or
3. Copy and paste a list of bills from a report exported from the Capitol website.

A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus | Rev. 01-27-26 | 3

Now that you have the list of bills, click on the **"Create Report Sheet"** button. This will create a new worksheet in the LiveStatus workbook called Report, and this is where the list will get updated. (Note that the sheet name indicates the date and time it was created; this is to help you keep track of when you first created this sheet.) Next, click the **"Buttons"** sheet.

Each button on this sheet corresponds to a column in your "Report" worksheet. To update a column on the "Report" worksheet, make sure the column letter for that column is entered into the appropriate space next to the button. In the example above, the bill number will be in column A, the measure title will be in column B, and so forth. Once the column letter is specified here, you can click on the button to update that column in the Report sheet. For any column that you do not want to have on the "Report" sheet, simply delete the letter next to the button. Note that you can also double-click on any of those cells to add or remove the letter. Double-clicking to add a letter will cause the workbook to find the next available letter. **Helpful Tip:** A column without a letter next to it remains static on your Report sheet. This means that column will not be overwritten with data when you click "Update All". These static columns can be used to add your own notes. If you have not already done so, it's a good idea to save your workbook at this point. A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus | Rev. 01-27-26 | 5

title: <u>SPREADSHEETS TO UPDATE</u>
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus.pdf
text:
# <u>SPREADSHEETS TO UPDATE</u>

You can have multiple "Report" sheets in your workbook. You can create multiple worksheets in various ways: using the "Create Report Sheet" button on the Hearing sheet multiple times; using the "Go" button on the Lists sheet multiple times; or by manually adding sheets to the workbook.

Once you have multiple "Report" sheets, you can use the "**Spreadsheets to update**" dropdown in the top row of the Buttons sheet to choose which sheet you want to update as you use the buttons on this page. If you want all sheets updated, choose the first option list, "(All sheets)". Feel free to rename the sheets to whatever makes the most sense to you.

![Screenshot of the "Spreadsheets to update" dropdown menu in Excel, showing options: (All sheets), Fiscal Bills, District Bills, and Resolutions.](image)

# <u>YEAR</u>

In the upper righthand corner of the Buttons sheet, you'll see a box with the current year. You can use the dropdown in that cell to pick any year going back to 2001. The year that is in that cell determines which database LiveStatus will pull the data from, and what year's documents the links in the LiveStatus workbook (for things like committee reports) will bring up.

![Screenshot of the Excel header showing the "Spreadsheets to update" field and the "Year" field set to 2026, which is circled in red.](image)

If you have columns in your Report sheet that the chosen year does not support (for example, if you choose 2001 and include "Latest HSCR"), then that entire column will have "\<N/A\>" in it. This is to distinguish it from the "N/A" you'll see when information isn't available for that one measure (for example, having a CCR Number column and some of the measures are not in conference).

A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus | Rev. 01-27-26 | 7

On this sheet, you indicate the criteria that you want to use to determine which measures to pull from the system, then when you click on the "Go" button, the Report sheet is created with the list of measures you specified. Note that the year chosen on the Buttons sheet affects which measures this sheet creates. As mentioned in the note on this sheet, all of your choices are "AND"ed together, meaning bills must meet all criteria in order to be listed. The one exception to this is the keywords. If you type more than one keyword, and/or if you select more than one area to search (Measure Title, Report Title, etc.), then measures need to only match one of those selections in order to be included. title: A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/A Basic Introduction to Using LiveStatus.pdf
text:
**OTHER THINGS TO KNOW** There are a few other things you should know about LiveStatus to make your life easier. **Notes for the "Buttons" Sheet:** * Double-clicking on **cell A1** will clear all of the letters next to the buttons, except for the "A" next to the "Bill No./Drafts" button, which should always have a letter next to it, although it doesn't have to be "A" if you want the measure numbers in a different column. * Double-clicking on **cell B1** will pre-fill all of the lettered cells from A to Z . * Double-clicking on any of the "Yes"/"No" fields will toggle them between "Yes" and "No". * Clicking on the **"Add Headers"** button will add headers to the first row of the "Report" sheet for any column that is being used. It will also format the column as appropriate (for width, word wrapping, etc.). * Clicking on the **"Update All "** button is equivalent to clicking on each button that has a letter next to it. (i.e., your whole worksheet, except for your static columns, will be updated using the latest live data.) * If you want to have your own notes in a particular column, you can skip that column's letter in the Buttons sheet and that column won't be touched when you click on the Update All button. * The main purpose of the Full Status button is so you can do filtering or conditional formatting based on prior status entries. * The **"Auto/Yes/No"** toggle at the top (in cell I1) determines whether the current workbook is updated whenever you reopen it. This can be handy if you only open the workbook periodically (once a week, for example) and want to have it populated with the latest status information. ![Screenshot of an Excel spreadsheet interface showing the "Update All on open?" setting and descriptions for measure titles and CCR numbers.](image)

If you have your workbook open and a new hearing notice is filed, double-clicking cell A2 will also show that latest hearing notice.

title: WHAT CAN I USE THIS DISTRICT CIP TEMPLATE FOR?
source_file: UH Capstone Training Files 2-16-26/PRESENTATION 2026 District CIP Training.pdf
text:
# WHAT CAN I USE THIS DISTRICT CIP TEMPLATE FOR?

This District CIP Template has the following capabilities:

* Appropriate general obligation bond revenue only

* Appropriate funds for capital improvement projects within a single representative district

* Appropriate funds for only one fiscal year beginning on the next July 1

* Sort by Expending Agency, then Project Title, then Project Description

* Sort by Project Number, which allows you to choose any order

## BASIC STEPS

1. Gather all District CIP information (District CIP request form, etc.).

2. Open the District CIP Template, which is a Microsoft Excel macro-enabled template.

3. <mark>Save the file as an Excel Macro-Enabled Workbook</mark> (Under Save As, select Excel Macro-Enabled Workbook under Save as type) using the naming convention District XX CIP, where XX is your district number. If it is a single-digit district, pad the number with a "0". For example, for the 5th district, name your spreadsheet District 05 CIP.

4. When you open the template, you will be immediately prompted to enter a district number then taken to a Project Information form. However, you can always cancel out of the Project Information form to save your template then hit the "Add Project" button to begin adding projects.

5. Enter the representative district number as a numeral (not text).

6. Complete one Project Information form for each project.

7. If you are copying projects from a previously introduced District CIP bill in an older format and have multiple Project Descriptions listed under the same Project Title, enter each project individually, repeating the Project Title for each related project description.

8. If you were provided a Project Title with no Project Description, repeat the Project Title as the Project Description.

9. You may stop work on this template at any time. The information in the Project Information form will be added to the spreadsheet only if you click the "OK" button. If you click on "Cancel", all information in the form will be cleared and will NOT be added to the spreadsheet.
